# The effect of notetaking method on academic performance: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Author links open overlay panelDanielVoyer,Scott T.Ronis,NarissaByers
Show more
Add to Mendeley
Share
Cite
[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cedpsych.2021.102025](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cedpsych.2021.102025)[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0361476X21000849&orderBeanReset=true)
## Highlights
* •This meta-analysis explores the effect of longhand and digital notetaking on performance.
* •Results showed no effect of method on performance under controlled conditions.
* •Distractions might account for past finding of an advantage for longhand notetaking.
* •We provide recommendations to ensure that students do not seek distraction in class.
## Abstract
The present meta-analysis aimed to synthesize the extant research on the influence of longhand (written) versus digital notetaking methods, unconfounded by distractions, on performance, and to identify key potential moderators of such effects. After a systematic literature search, we obtained 77 effect sizes from 39 samples in 36 articles and conducted a multilevel meta-analysis complemented with robust variance estimation. Overall, results showed a mean effect size (mean estimated g=-0.008, 95%*CI*: −0.18, 0.16) that was not significantly different from zero, suggesting no effect of notetaking approach. Moderator analyses, justified by effect sizes with significant heterogeneity, demonstrated only three significant moderators referring to the topic covered, the learning objectives, and the duration of the material. Overall, however, the present results suggest that an apparent advantage of longhand notetaking reported in some previous studies can be explained at least partially by distractions from notetaking by other applications that are present only with digital devices. Nevertheless, more research is required to identify moderators that might account for variability in the findings. Work using large representative samples of all ages is particularly needed.
## Section snippets
## Digital notetaking and distraction
In what appears to be the only existing meta-analysis on the effect of notetaking method on learning performance, Allen, Lefebvre, Lefebvre, and Bourhis (2020) aggregated data from 14 studies (24 estimates). Their results showed poorer performance for digital compared to longhand notetaking (average*r*=-0.142). However, the Allen et al. meta-analysis did not separate the influence of distraction from the notetaking method. Specifically, they acknowledged that digital notetaking also allows the
## Notetaking functions
In general, there is a robust benefit of notetaking on performance, as it typically serves process-related and product functions (Luo et al., 2018). Among the process-related functions, researchers have identified the promotion of attention and encoding of presented material as well as the roles of complex cognitive demands in memory and performance. In particular, after attending to presented material, notetaking involves a range of generative (e.g., summarizing, paraphrasing) and
## Notetaking approaches
Notetaking typically is completed through either a longhand format or a digital format with computers, tablets, or other technological devices. In particular, digital notetaking on laptops or tablets has been used increasingly, which has engendered questions about the effects of this form of notetaking on attention, memory, learning, recall, and performance. Because individuals generally type notes faster than when writing (Aragón-Mendizábal et al., 2016), typed notes tend to be nongenerative
## Role of depth of processing
One obvious aspect that distinguishes written and digital notetaking pertains to mechanistic processes. For example, the two approaches involve different physical movements, use of physical objects, production of concrete products, freedom to or ease of ability to draw, color, scale, or work in non-linear fashion. Of course, these mechanistic constraints vary also with specific digital platforms and with individual differences. Interestingly, although some researchers have examined mechanistic
## Sample factors
In examining longhand and digital notetaking approaches, it is important to consider that some sample factors potentially moderate effects on academic performance. In particular, demographic information (e.g., socioeconomic status, ethnicity) and other sample characteristics (e.g., clinical versus general community sample) are considered to have important direct or indirect effects on academic performance (Gurley, 2018, Richards et al., 2016). For sample characteristics, although studies
## Current meta-analysis
The purpose of the present meta-analysis was to fill a gap in the literature by determining whether the method used in notetaking during a course lecture or relevant experimental tasks affects performance. In addition, we documented potential factors that moderate this effect to guide future basic research as well as classroom applications, following approaches that promoted transparency and reproducibility. To our knowledge, aside from the limited analysis conducted by Allen et al. (2020),
## Study selection
The retrieval of studies was performed through searching PsycINFO, ERIC, SCOPUS, Academic Search Premier, PubMED, SocioINDEX, and Canadian Business and Current Affairs databases in May 2020. The search terms incorporated truncation (or equivalent) in different databases to include all related terms. The terms used were:*(“note taking” OR notetaking OR “tak\* notes”) AND (academic OR learning OR student\* OR science OR math\* OR reading OR exam\* OR course OR class\* OR scholastic OR school OR test)*
## Preliminary analyses
Considering that our sample included within-subject designs for which we computed only an approximation of the appropriate effect size, we believed it was important to determine whether this factor unduly affected the results. Accordingly, we conducted a moderator analysis with design (between or within) to decide if such studies fit within our population of effects. Results of this analysis showed that design accounted for significant variability,*F*(1, 37)=5.52,*p*=.024. This reflected the
## Discussion
The purpose of the present meta-analysis was to fill a gap in the literature by developing a more definitive understanding of whether notetaking method (longhand writing or digital) affected academic performance. We pursued this goal by retrieving literature from as many sources as possible (published and unpublished) and by integrating relevant effect sizes into a multilevel meta-analysis that also took into account correlated sampling errors by means of robust variance estimation. In
## Conclusions
The purpose of the meta-analysis presented here was to quantify overall effects of notetaking method, defined as longhand or digital, in various learning situations and to identify potential moderators of these effects. We began this meta-analysis fully expecting to find evidence favoring the use of longhand notetaking over digital notetaking for optimal performance. ## Cited by (7)
* ### [Notetaking as validity evidence: A mixed-methods investigation of question preview in EAP listening assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1475158524000146)
2024, Journal of English for Academic Purposes
Show abstract
Recent scholarship has questioned the cognitive validity of listening tests with preview, in which test-takers can see test questions before listening. This study mined student notes for evidence of cognitive processes in listening tests with and without preview, using a mixed-methods design that explored the effect of test format on notetaking behaviors. Qualitative analysis indicated that students who previewed items were more likely to systematically omit information, highlight previewed keywords, and engage in shallower structural representation. Conversely, Kruskal-Wallis tests revealed that students who listened without preview took more notes, especially of main ideas and details, and had better coverage of the lecture. However, correlation and hierarchical linear regression analyses found these notetaking achievements did not predict higher scores in the no-preview condition, while in the preview condition, only note quantity and focus on minor ideas predicted scores. Both strands of data suggest that students' cognitive processes were shaped by the format of the exam they experienced. These findings may bear on validity arguments for listening assessment and inform the way that language instructors prepare their students for academic listening.
* ### [Learning neuroscience: Investigating influences of notetaking materials and individual differences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1041608022001303)
2023, Learning and Individual Differences
Citation Excerpt :
This is consistent with perspectives that emphasize the role of the individual processing that learners undertake during learning (e.g., Kiewra, 1985, 1987; Wittrock, 1992) and the importance of considering how context such as notetaking materials influences learning (see Bronfenbrenner, 1979; Flower, 1989; Greeno, 1994; Prather, 2021). There was no observable effect of notetaking medium on learning, consistent with others' research (including a large meta-analysis; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021; Voyer et al., 2022). This finding continues to offer insight into the question of whether notetaking medium influences learning.
Show abstract
How can we support classroom learning? Individual differences between students (e.g., cognitive skills and notetaking styles) is one factor that may relate to learning and interact with notetaking materials (e.g., diagram handouts and notetaking medium) to influence learning. However, the interaction between these factors is not well-understood. Accordingly, in this study, we presented short neuroscience lectures to 18–23-year-old undergraduates and investigated the interactions between notetaking materials and individual differences (cognitive skills: spatial/verbal reasoning; and notetaking style: verbatim copying/key terms) on learning. We found minimal overall effects of notetaking materials on learning. However, spatial and verbal reasoning related to learning. Additionally, in a handwritten condition, verbatim copying in notes was associated with lower learning whereas more key terms in notes was associated with higher learning. These results demonstrate that, to best support neuroscience learning in the classroom, we must consider individual differences and how they interact with notetaking materials.

# The Effects of the Medium of Notetaking on the Delayed Learning Effect of College Students: A Mediated Moderation Model

Download keyboard_arrow_down

Browse Figures

## Abstract

This study systematically probed the relationship between the medium of taking classroom notes (virtual variable, electronic notetaking = 0 vs. traditional notetaking = 1), the word count in each medium, as well as the review process, and the students’ delayed learning effect for each notetaking approach. Data were collected from 189 college students, with the influence of gender and prior knowledge being controlled. The conclusions were as follows. (1) The notetaking medium was positively correlated with delayed test scores, irrespective of whether reviews were allowed or not. (2) The mediating role of word count between notetaking medium and delayed test scores was moderated by review. That is, when reviews were allowed, a significant correlation was found between the medium of the notes and the delayed test scores; when reviews were not allowed, the mediating effect of word count was not significant.

Keywords:

## 1. Introduction

Scientific and technological progress is undoubtedly one of the most significant driving forces for the profound change in contemporary education ecology. With the rapid development of information technology, the field of education is undergoing an unprecedented digital transformation. Electronic devices have become an indispensable part of individual learning. Electronic notetaking, defined as “the act of using Microsoft and related Office software to take notes in electronic devices” [1] has gradually become a part of people’s learning lives. Many students tend to type notes on computers instead of taking notes by hand [2,3]. Therefore, what impact the change in notetaking media will have on student learning has become an urgent question to answer.

Although some studies have pointed out that the “pen is mightier than the keyboard”, advances in electronics are turning the generative processing of notetaking into nongenerative transcription [4]. However, other researchers believe that taking notes on a computer is more conducive to learning [5]. When such contradictions arise across studies, it seems reasonable to show caution and not tout one notetaking medium over the other [6]. To explore the impact of the two notetaking mediums on learning performance, we need to start with notetaking strategies, which includes both encoding and external storage functions. According to the tenants of distributed memory, the encoding function that occurs during the act of notetaking benefits both learning and retention, thus improving internal memory. However, the external storage function emphasizes the importance of utilizing notes to store information to facilitate the subsequent reviewing of material, thus enhancing external memory [7,8]. Electronic notetaking, which turns the traditional paper storage of handwriting into more fluent electronic digital copies of writing, allows for higher storage efficiency and consequent lower search costs. However, over-reliance on storage function may come at the cost of deeper encoding benefits, a finding supported by Mueller and Oppenheimer [4]. In their study, all undergraduates were instructed to take notes through a designated medium while watching a TED talk, after which they were immediately tested. Participants who took electronic notes typed significantly more words than those who took notes by hand, with the word count positively predicting their immediate recall performance. Furthermore, students who used laptops for notetaking revealed more verbatim overlap than did their longhand counterparts, with the verbatim overlap shown to negatively predict their study performances. It is worth noting that the effect of verbatim overlap on test scores was stronger than that of word count.

However, the enhanced effects on encoding and external-storage functions are most often evident (or found) when learners all allowed to review their prepared notes [4]. Bui and his team found [9] that when individuals were not allowed to review, verbatim-notetakers scored significantly lower on a delayed test when compared to an immediate test, while notetakers who organizationally paraphrased the learning material revealed similar levels of enhanced retention at both testing intervals. In other words, in a review-absent situation, the delayed study effect for electronic notes was also inferior to that for traditional notetaking. This is because the favorable encoding effect of electronic notes caused by recording more was weakened by forgetting in the delay test, while the disadvantage in the immediate test due to insufficient generative processing was maintained. Hence, people rely more on review, anticipating that the stronger storage of electronic notes can neutralize its deficits in encoding. This critical question determines how we should assess the severity of the insufficient encoding capacity of electronic notes. Accordingly, it is necessary to deeply explore how the notetaking medium affects the delayed study performance from the perspective of review or no review.

Review promotes the interaction of new and old knowledge by arousing the recognition of the outside-stored information. In essence, it is a constructive process between prior knowledge encoded before review and learning material in notes [10]. Review outcomes are closely related to prior knowledge and learning material and are a trade-off between the quantity and quality of notes. Whether learning material can be better constructed depends on the prior knowledge. Review could improve the retention of the encoding effect for transcription notetakers [11]. One research work has shown that when review is allowed, word count is positively correlated with delayed test performance. Therefore, more notes were undeniably advantageous to review, and more transcribed notes would be more beneficial when review is allowed [9]. However, another research work has argued that more notes recorded by laptops were insufficient to offset its disadvantages in cognitive processing [4]. Alexa’s [12] research showed that compared with electronic notes, paper-and-pencil notes were more beneficial for long-term memory as the number of reviews increased.

It can be seen from the above discussion that the effect of electronic notetaking on delayed learning is still controversial. One thing is certain, however: that review makes a difference. So, we designed this study to examine the moderating effect of review. Accordingly, we propose hypothesis 1, that when review is not allowed, the notetaking medium (virtual variable, electronic notes = 0, traditional notes = 1) will positively predict the delayed test scores, with no significant mediation effect of word count. We proceed with hypothesis 2, that when review is allowed, the notetaking medium is positively correlated with delayed learning performances. Meanwhile, the word count will significantly mediate the relationship between the notetaking medium and the delayed test scores, as notetaking medium negatively predicts delayed test performance through word count.

Overall, we aimed to explore whether review would moderate the mediating effect of word count on the notetaking medium and the delayed test performance under the control of extra variables (i.e., undergraduates’ prior knowledge, attitudes towards electronic notes, gender; Reddington) [4].

## 2. Materials and Methods

### 2.1. Participants

As a field study, four public elective classes, including psychology, electrical mechanics, computer science, and other majors, were randomly cluster sampled from two universities (two classes per university, so that the level of universities and majors was matched) in China. Two classes in the same university (at time 1) were randomly assigned to an experimental condition: the electronic-notetaking or traditional-notetaking group. Then (at time 2), half of the students in each class were randomly assigned to an experimental condition: the review-present or review-absent group. Yet, regrettably, due to the random cluster sampling method forced by the real environment, we only achieved this by matching different levels of universities and majors. Other demographic variables, such as gender, can only be controlled for by statistical means.

All 236 participants were proficient in operating their computers and office software. After screening out invalid data, such as those who omitted any items (n = 45), and excluding 2 participants who dropped out of this study, 189 participants (80% of those initially enrolled) remained for analysis. The distribution was as follows: 89 valid cases completed their participation in the electronic-notetaking group (38 for the review-present group, where there were 23 males and 15 females; 51 for the review-absent group, comprising 7 males and 44 females), and 100 in the traditional-notetaking group (60 for the review-present group, where there were 18 males and 42 females; 40 for the review-absent group, comprising 5 males and 35 females).

### 2.2. Material and Measurements

Learning Material. We downloaded a video clip titled The Palace Museum from the Sohu Video website as our stimulus. The transcript of the video was the text of a Chinese textbook of the China People’s Education Press for grade eight, lesson 14 in the first volume. The amount of text was 1600 words. To ensure that the participants had enough time to take notes, the original video was slowed down from 8 min and 20 s to 16 min and 34 s.

Measurements of Control variables. Considering that the prior-knowledge level and attitude towards electronic notes will affect the follow-up learning effect, these were measured as the control variables for the follow-up analysis.

Prior-Knowledge Level: To ensure content validity, a prior-knowledge test was developed by five educational psychologists jointly, drawing upon common-sense issues about the Palace Museum, which included 10 Yes/No and 11 fill-in-the-blank questions. One point was scored for each question, resulting in the highest personal total score of 21.

Attitudes towards Electronic Notes: Following Seigel’s approach [13], attitudes were tested with one item on a 5-point Likert scale: “What is your attitude towards using electronic means (such as computer, mobile phone, etc.) to take notes while studying?”.

Measurements of dependent variable. The Delayed Test Score: A delayed test was developed by the same five educational psychologists, which contained 21 fill-in-the-blank questions, worth one point for each question. The personal total score was between 1 and 21.

Measurements of mediate variable. The Word Count: The number of words in the traditional-notetaking group was calculated manually, while this was performed automatically in the electronic-notetaking group by the office software.

### 2.3. Procedure

At time 1, the students were first asked to complete the prior-knowledge level test and the demographic scale. The video of the learning material was then played, and students in both the longhand and laptop groups were instructed to use traditional-notetaking and electronic-notetaking strategies, respectively, after which their notes were collected. After one week (time 2), participants (who were not informed that a follow up test would be included) at time 1 were required to complete the delayed test. Before the test, the review-present group was allowed to take back their notes and review them for five minutes, while the review-absent group was solely allowed to “mind review”, without access to their notes, for five minutes.

## 3. Results

### 3.1. Descriptive Analysis

The scores of study variables in different situations are shown in Table 1.

### 3.2. Correlation Analysis

As shown in Table 2, the medium for notetaking was correlated positively with the delayed test scores and negatively with the word count in both groups. In addition, the word count positively predicted the delayed test scores. This means that laptop typists can take more notes, and more notes indeed can be beneficial to the learning results to some degree. However, whether review was permitted or not, electronic notes were generally inferior to the traditional notes in terms of the delayed test scores. Moreover, these results also provided preliminary support for further mediation and moderation analyses.

### 3.3. Mediation and Moderation Analyses

Because the notetaking medium is a virtual variable and review is a categorical variable, structural equation modeling (SEM) multigroup analysis was used to examine the moderation effect of review on the mediation effect of the word count [9,14,15]. The underlying idea is that when the mediation effect is significant in one group but not in another group, this effect may be moderated by the grouping variable.

Firstly, we examined whether the word count mediated the relationship between the notetaking medium and the delayed test scores in the review-absent group. After controlling for gender and the attitude to electronic notes, we used the Mplus 7.0 maximum likelihood ratio test to build the model. Third, we tested the mediation hypotheses using a bias-corrected bootstrap 95% confidence interval analysis based on 5000 bootstrap samples [16]. SEM yielded a moderate model fit: χ2 = 6.180, df = 5, p > 0.05; RMSEA = 0.051, CFI = 0.967, TLI = 0.941, SRMR = 0.051. As shown in Figure 1, there was a positive direct effect between word count and delayed test scores, while its path coefficient was not significant. The data in Table 3 further show that the indirect effect of the word count on bootstrap 95% confidence intervals contains a value of 0, so that in the review-absent group, word count did not significantly mediate the relationship between notetaking medium and delayed test scores. Therefore, hypothesis 1 was supported.

Next, we examined the mediation effect of word count in the review-present group. SEM also yielded a moderate model fit: χ2 = 0.865, df = 1, p > 0.05; RMSEA = 0.000, CFI = 1.000, TLI = 1.031, SRMR = 0.017. As shown in Figure 2, there was a positive direct effect between word count and delayed test scores. The notetaking medium indirectly negatively predicted the delayed test scores via the word count. According to Table 3, a value of 0 was not contained in the indirect effect of the word count on bootstrap 95% confidence intervals, suggesting that word count partially mediated the relationship between the medium and the scores. The ratio of absolute values of both indirect and direct effects was 35.7%. Thereby, hypothesis 2 was supported.

To sum up, the partial mediation effect was found only in the review-present group, so that review moderated the mediation effect of the word account on the notetaking medium and the delayed test scores. Thus, whether review is allowed or not, the pen is mightier than the keyboard. Though the use of laptops facilitates the verbatim transcription of video content, it has less advantages for delayed test scores when reviewing is forbidden. Accordingly, taking more notes benefits reviewing to some extent, but this is not enough to offset its deficits in encoding due to the tendency to make thoughtless transcriptions, occasioned by taking electronic notes.

## 4. Discussion

### 4.1. Relationship between Notetaking Medium and Delayed Learning Effect under Review-Absent Group

The current study found that electronic notes predicted poorer performance than traditional notetaking in the delayed test. When review was not allowed, notetaking medium was positively related to delayed test scores, while no significant mediation effect of word count was found.

The levels of the processing framework suggest that organized notetaking (i.e., summarizing, concept mapping) involves deeper cognitive processing and thus promotes enhanced information retention [17]. However, transcribed notes have generally been found to be less effective at promoting encoding. Their encoding function is mainly shown in the convention between orthographic and phonological processes when the learner records the input information, such as writing down what is heard [18]. The greater the number of notes taken, the more processing conversions will be generated. Though this benefits the immediate learning effect to some extent, it has been shown to impair retention, which means that delayed learning is less effective [19]. This was supported by our finding of there being no significant mediating effect of word count between notetaking medium and delayed test scores. Hence, when not allowed to review, the longhand participants also outperformed the laptop-typing participants in the delayed test.

The results suggest that, compared with notetaking media, the level of notetaking processing seems to be more worthy of attention. Whether it is electronic notes or traditional notes being taken, it is essential to take organized notes of the learning material. The organized notetaking of learned material can combine the external storage and easy-search functions, as well as the encoding function of electronic notes.

### 4.2. Relationship between Notetaking Medium and Delayed Learning Effect under Review-Present Group

People rely on the notion that reviewing can partially offset the detriment associated with electronic notes to encoding. However, the current study casts doubt on this reliance. We empirically supported Mueller’s view that more notes being taken with electronic notes hardly balanced the associated deficits in encoding. In general, it showed that when reviewing was allowed, notetaking medium positively predicts delayed test performance, while word count negatively mediates this relationship.

As mentioned, the essence of reviewing is to construct prior knowledge encoded before the review and learning the material in one’s notes [10]. Indeed, more learning material benefits learners to some degree. Consistent with previous studies, our findings also showed that when reviewing is allowed, notetaking medium negatively predicts delayed test performance through word count. Yet, prior knowledge is the basis of whether more learning material can engender more constructive processing. To take an extreme example, reviewing is unhelpful when all coding effects before reviewing are forgotten. This is because all notes will be newly and thoroughly learned. This was supported by our findings that the positive direct effect between notetaking medium and delayed test scores was weaker than the negative indirect effect of the word count.

It is worth noting the studies in which note integrity promotes performance. Given that the true benefit of lecture notetaking appears to stem from the external storage function [20], it is plausible that the extent to which students record incomplete lecture ideas into their notes hinders the external storage function of lecture notes and diminishes learning and achievement. Regardless of notetaking medium, however, participants failed to record most of the ideas presented during the lesson on average, recording just 35% of lesson ideas, and 20% of those noted ideas were partial notes [21]. Despite the speed advantage of typing notes, computer-using notetakers still record fewer than half of the ideas presented during lectures [2,9,22]. As Morehead points out, most college students are not sufficiently trained in the use of electronic notetaking [2]. However, there is no doubt that electronic notetaking as a technology requires adequate training. If a digital-note notetaker wanted to take notes in a certain way but their word processing software was not pre-set to do so, they would need to search for and adjust the settings during the notetaking process, thereby theoretically diverting attention to software settings rather than attending to the incoming speech [13]. Therefore, we believe that the advantages of electronic notes are not fully represented in this study. When the electronic-note notetaker is given more time to train, the distraction caused by this lack of proficiency will be reduced, more attention will be transferred to the content of the lecture, the completeness of the notes will be increased, and the effect of reviewing will be enhanced.

### 4.3. Brief Summary

Firstly, this study affirms the positive role of reviewing in the learning process. The true value of notetaking is more in the review of notes than in their recording [23]. Other researchers have also found the activity of notetaking cognitively demanding and sometimes ineffective when recorded notes are not reviewed [24]. Review makes the external storage function of electronic notes realized.

Secondly, notetaking is not only a way for students to learn and understand knowledge [25]. For teachers, it is also a signal that students are paying attention [26]. Researchers have shown that electronic notetaking can improve student performance in classroom settings [27] and that “deliberate engagement” in large classes can increase student engagement. Moreover, this strong interaction with the teacher and high level of activity in the class is also reflected in communication over the Internet: emails and social media are often distractions for electronic notetakers [28], as well as for other people sitting nearby [13]. In addition, one study has shown that 20% of students now experience digital distraction compared to when they did not use electronic devices in class [29]. Therefore, in addition to the impact of the storage and encoding function of electronic notes on academic performance, its role and influence in the process of learning is also worthy of further in-depth study.

Lastly, having diverse options is the most important feature of self-regulated learning [11]. Similarly, when taking notes, learners can also select what information to record or to neglect, and it is the content of the information rather than how much information is written down that is most important. Among all kinds of note symbols, pictures play an important role. Some studies have combined pictures and notes to effectively promote students’ learning [30]. However, compared to electronic notes, pens and paper are viewed as providing a less restrictive format, with symbols, diagrams and graphs easier to draw by hand [31]. Research has found that longhand notetakers did record more images in their notes while the lesson was ongoing than computer-note notetakers. Students need to be guided on how to recognize and record the value of course-related images [21].

### 4.4. Limitations and Future Research

Despite the rigorous design of this study, combined with previous research, there are still some shortcomings. (1) Due to the limitation of notetaking software, this study only examined the transcription of electronic notes. As mentioned above, organized notes are more conducive to realizing the positive effects of notes, so subsequent studies can pay more attention to organized notes in either notetaking medium. (2) The measurement of prior knowledge as the control variable, using a non-scale questionnaire, can only test its content validity, and more standardized measurement could be used in subsequent research. (3) The representativeness of the samples needs to be strengthened. In the sampling, statistical control was taken for demographic variables such as gender, limited by the conditions of the field study. Follow-up studies should carry out stricter controls to enhance the representativeness of the sample.

## 5. Conclusions

We conclude with that the following: (1) Whether a learner was allowed to review or not, the medium of notes was positively correlated with their delayed test scores. (2) After controlling for extraneous variables, reviewing moderated the mediating effect of the word count on the note medium and the delayed test scores. Specifically, under the review-absent condition, the mediating effect of the amount of text was not significant, while, under the review-present condition, the note medium impaired the delayed test scores through the amount of text. (3) Electronic notes have more external storage and faster search capabilities, but this does not make up for their encoding shortcomings during review. This study emphasizes that it is not the notetaking medium that is important, but rather how notetaking is organized, rather than transcribed. This suggests to educators that in the formative stage of notetaking, no matter what kind of notetaking medium is used, it is crucial to carry out productive/organizational notetaking. After taking notes, review plays an irreplaceable role. In addition, for the developers of electronic notetaking software, it is necessary to enrich the notetaking function of the software, especially in the recording of picture information, and to explore alternative note-transcription assistance software. For users of electronic notes, it is important to increase the proficiency of notetaking and to increase the awareness of the content of the notes.

highlights:
Although some studies have pointed out that the “pen is mightier than the keyboard”, advances in electronics are turning the generative processing of notetaking into nongenerative transcription [4]. However, other researchers believe that taking notes on a computer is more conducive to learning [5]. When such contradictions arise across studies, it seems reasonable to show caution and not tout one notetaking medium over the other [6]. To explore the impact of the two notetaking mediums on learning performance, we need to start with notetaking strategies, which includes both encoding and external storage functions. According to the tenants of distributed memory, the encoding function that occurs during the act of notetaking benefits both learning and retention, thus improving internal memory. However, the external storage function emphasizes the importance of utilizing notes to store information to facilitate the subsequent reviewing of material, thus enhancing external memory [7,8]. Electronic notetaking, which turns the traditional paper storage of handwriting into more fluent electronic digital copies of writing, allows for higher storage efficiency and consequent lower search costs. However, over-reliance on storage function may come at the cost of deeper encoding benefits, a finding supported by Mueller and Oppenheimer [4]. In their study, all undergraduates were instructed to take notes through a designated medium while watching a TED talk, after which they were immediately tested. Participants who took electronic notes typed significantly more words than those who took notes by hand, with the word count positively predicting their immediate recall performance. Furthermore, students who used laptops for notetaking revealed more verbatim overlap than did their longhand counterparts, with the verbatim overlap shown to negatively predict their study performances. It is worth noting that the effect of verbatim overlap on test scores was stronger than that of word count.
...
As shown in Table 2, the medium for notetaking was correlated positively with the delayed test scores and negatively with the word count in both groups. In addition, the word count positively predicted the delayed test scores. This means that laptop typists can take more notes, and more notes indeed can be beneficial to the learning results to some degree. However, whether review was permitted or not, electronic notes were generally inferior to the traditional notes in terms of the delayed test scores. Moreover, these results also provided preliminary support for further mediation and moderation analyses.

results.title: The Differential Effects of Handwritten and Typed Academic Notetaking: Finding the right advice for students to optimize durable learning
results.author: L. Richardson
results.text:
# The Differential Effects of Handwritten and Typed Academic Notetaking: Finding the right advice for students to optimize durable learning

2023. Dissertation. 0 citations.

## Authors

- L. Richardson (Carleton University): h-index 1; 84 citations; corresponding author

## Topics

Visual and Cognitive Learning Processes, Innovative Teaching and Learning Methods, Educational Strategies and Epistemologies, Psychology, Note-taking

---

# by Lindsay Richardson

The Differential Effects of Handwritten and Typed Academic Notetaking:

Finding the right advice for students to optimize durable learning by Lindsay Richardson

A thesis submitted to the Faculty of Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of Doctor of Philosophy in Psychology

Carleton University Ottawa, Canada

© 2023 Lindsay Richardson

## ABSTRACT

It is uncontroversial that lecture notetaking influences academic success (Dunkel & Davy, 1989).

However, the influence of modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) on this association remains contentious (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Kutta, 2017; Manzi et al., 2017;

Morehead et al., 2019a; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014). This research aimed to provide a comprehensive examination of the effects of notetaking modality and method (i.e., transcription vs. paraphrasing) on academic performance. The first set of experiments evaluated the recall performance for handwritten, typed, and drawn words, delving into the underlying cognitive processes. The second part of the research deployed a simulated-lecture experiment to analyze the impact of notetaking modality and method on information retention, considering relevant factors such as review, working memory, typing proficiency, and note quantity. Finally, an Academic Experience Survey study assessed nearly 600 students’ academic behaviours and notetaking strategies throughout the forced-shift to online learning during the COVID-19 pandemic. Overall, the findings did not show a reliable retention advantage for handwritten notes over typed ones concerning the encoding of information. Instead, the evidence pointed towards computers as potentially more effective tool for capturing reference materials for review, thereby highlighting the importance of the external storage benefit to notetaking. Interestingly, students reported a preference for paraphrasing via handwriting, possibly due to perceived retention benefits. However, a predominant inclination toward passive note review was observed, which could compromise the external storage advantage. Researchers, educators, and students should focus their efforts on developing efficient active note review habits that will optimize student learning and ultimately their success.

Keywords: notetaking, higher education, memory, learning, technology

## ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

I would like to express my deepest gratitude to everyone who has supported and encouraged me throughout the process of completing this dissertation, and my PhD more generally. First and foremost, I would like to thank my advisor, Dr. Guy Lacroix, for his unwavering guidance, valuable feedback, continuous encouragement (and reality checks), and mentorship. His expertise and passion for both research and pedagogy have been a constant source of inspiration, and his mentorship has been instrumental in shaping my academic journey.

Additionally, I wish to extend my sincere thanks to my internal dissertation committee members, Dr. Rachel Burns and Dr. Chris Herdman. Their insightful comments and invaluable feedback were instrumental in refining this work. I also want to express my appreciation in advance to my external committee members, Dr. John Hayes and Dr. Evan Risko. Their willingness to participate in my defence is deeply appreciated, and I eagerly anticipate their perspectives and guidance on my research.

I must extend a special acknowledgment to our Graduate Studies Administrator, Etelle

Bourassa. Her support throughout my journey has been instrumental and truly memorable.

Beyond just recognizing me as a graduate student, Etelle has shown a remarkable ability to remember the individual nuances of my journey. Her profound knowledge of the institution and her unwavering commitment to assisting me in reaching my goals have been nothing short of remarkable. Her dedication has left an unforgettable mark on my academic experience.

In addition to the many I've already thanked, I'd be remiss not to acknowledge Dr. Kim

Hellemans from the Neuroscience department. Even though our paths crossed from different disciplines, her steadfast commitment to excellence and her position as a strong role model for women in science have been a profound source of inspiration to me. Her exemplary leadership

NOTETAKING BY TYPING OR HANDWRITTING 6 and untiring support for female scholars served as a beacon, guiding my journey and reinforcing my belief in the invaluable contributions of women to the world of academia.

I would also like to express my profound gratitude for the continual support I have received during my tenure at Carleton University's Teaching and Learning Services. My employment with this esteemed department has not only facilitated the fulfilment of my financial obligations as a graduate student, but it has also provided me with an invaluable network of colleagues and managers who have consistently championed my academic pursuits and celebrated my accomplishments. Furthermore, I have been fortunate enough to acquire a wealth of knowledge, an array of practical skills, and a deeper understanding of the intricacies of my field. These experiences have culminated in my ongoing professional development and have undeniably contributed to my success as both a graduate student and a dedicated educator.

My heartfelt appreciation goes out to my family. My husband, Hardy Richardson, and my two children, Kailey Morgan and Nicholas Atkins, have demonstrated exceptional patience, understanding, and unfaltering belief in me. To my friends: thank you for understanding the countless times I seemingly vanished into the realm of research. Your forgiveness for my academic escapades and occasional disappearances means the world. Here's to less "ghosting" and more gatherings in the days ahead!

Finally, this document was written in loving memory of my mother, Deborah Louise

Ramsay, who instilled in me the value of grit and perseverance. Though she is no longer with us, her influence has been with me every step of the way. I hope my endeavours serve as a testament to the lessons she provided.

PREFACE

The Differential Effects of Typing and Handwriting:

Finding the right advice for students to optimize durable learning

Every fall, thousands of young minds embark on university courses, many grappling with the challenging transition from high school to university. A central aim of educators is to equip these students with durable knowledge as opposed to temporary learning enhancements (Dunlosky et al., 2013). Consequently, research spanning over a century has sought to discern the efficacy of various learning strategies, with particular attention given to the academic behaviours conducive to heightened performance (Brown et al., 2014; Busse & Lee, 2015; Butler, 2007;

Dunlosky et al., 2013; Igo et al., 2005; Putnam et al., 2016; Ravizza et al., 2017; Rawson &

Dunlosky, 2011). Prominent among these is the widely-accepted understanding that academic notetaking facilitates improved outcomes, such as superior final grades (Kiewra, 1989). This effect is so universally recognized that students regularly engage in notetaking during lectures, often unprompted (Williams & Eggert, 2002). The boost in student performance through academic notetaking has been attributed to two distinct hypotheses (DiVesta & Gray, 1972). The encoding hypothesis refers to the memory enhancement experienced from the actual practice of taking notes and the external storage hypothesis refers to the benefit that students gain from studying notes. It is plausible that both these aspects—the act of taking notes and their possession—collectively contribute to enhanced student performance (Kiewra, 1989).

The advent of technology in education has profoundly transformed the academic experience. This evolution is most evident in the emergence of online learning, enabling students to attend lectures remotely and even complete entire courses at a distance. The pertinence of this trend was captured during the COVID-19 pandemic when nearly all post-secondary education abruptly transitioned online, often without pedagogical redesign. Changes in student academic

NOTETAKING BY TYPING OR HANDWRITTING 10 behaviour due to technological advancements are evident in Morehead et al.’s (2019b) survey findings, indicating that merely 49% of students engaged in lecture notetaking for online courses compared to 96% in traditional in-person lectures. If notetaking is indeed beneficial to memory enhancement and test preparation in conventional lecture settings, it stands to reason that it would be equally advantageous in online courses, suggesting a gap in optimal student behaviour.

Alongside the shift to online coursework, another significant technological alteration in students' academic journey has been the prevalence of portable computers in lecture halls (Gaudreau et al., 2014). As per Morehead et al.’s (2019b) survey, almost half of the students attending face-to-face lectures reported notetaking via laptops. However, the impact of this modality shift on student performance remains ambiguous. Moreover, it remains to be seen if laptop notetaking prevalence rates have continued to rise with the rapid evolution of technology, or if a significant proportion of students remain aligned to traditional handwritten notetaking regardless of technological advancements. Given that students can typically type faster than they can handwrite (Brown, 1988), laptops could theoretically enable students to capture more lecture content, thereby enhancing the external storage benefits of notetaking. Conversely, the ease of transcription might deter in-depth processing of lecture material, potentially diminishing the encoding benefits of notetaking. As a result, researchers have sought to establish the impact of in-class laptop use on academic outcomes.

A growing body of research strongly advocates for handwritten over typed notetaking (e.g., Blankenship, 2016; Fried, 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Gaudreau et al., 2014;

Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kodaira, 2017; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010;

Manzi et al. 2017; Muller & Oppenheimer, 2014; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013;

Yamamoto, 2007). One of the primary criticisms of laptop use in the classroom is their potential

NOTETAKING BY TYPING OR HANDWRITTING 11 to distract, providing students with opportunities for non-academic activities not available with traditional pen-and-paper notetaking (e.g., Fried, 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrooke & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008;

Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007). Given that multitasking is often associated with diminished performance (Rubinstein et al., 2001), this stance seems reasonable. However, beyond the distraction potential, a separate line of research argues for handwriting's superior role in facilitating a deeper level of encoding, suggesting that even in the absence of distractions, typed notetaking may not match the cognitive benefits offered by handwriting. For instance, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) discovered that keyboards facilitated verbatim-style notetaking (i.e., transcription), which resulted in reduced information retention of content. This influential study has been instrumental in fostering a widely accepted premise: students ought to take notes by hand because it improves encoding and ultimately academic performance.

However, evidence across various studies suggests that the general advice for students to handwrite their notes may not be universally applicable. A few studies have demonstrated that in class laptop use does not always correlate with decreased academic performance (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Kutta, 2017). Moreover, Morehead et al. (2019a) argued that the acceptance of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s conclusion was premature, as their findings could not be reliably replicated. This lack of reproducibility has been echoed by several independent research groups (Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019a; Urry et al., 2021). Interestingly, Bui et al. (2013) suggested that a subset of students, specifically those with lower working memory capacity, might actually benefit from typed notetaking. The idea that working memory capacity plays a role in notetaking effectiveness is not new. In fact,

Berliner (1968) found that students with lower working memory capacity performed worse when

NOTETAKING BY TYPING OR HANDWRITTING 12 tasked with taking notes during a lecture. Therefore, the ability to type notes could potentially aid this subset of students, especially if they are proficient typists.

The overarching aim of the research presented in this dissertation was to re-evaluate the impact of written and typed lecture notes on academic performance, especially in the context of established predictors such as study habits and organizational skills. The role of technology in reshaping the relationship between academic behaviour and performance also remains ambiguous. Students’ adaptability to technological changes in course delivery, such as the shift towards online lectures and typed notetaking, could influence their academic performance. This is especially pertinent during the often-challenging transition from high school to university. As such, a series of studies was conducted to examine more specifically the differential effects that handwriting and typing have on memory and learning.

The first goal was to delve deeper into the cognitive processes involved in notetaking.

Researchers have demonstrated that handwriting is cognitively, mechanistically, and kinaesthetically different compared to typing (e.g., Bourdin & Fayol, 1994; Bouriga & Olive, 2021; Grabowski, 2010; Kiefer et al., 2015; Longcamp et al., 2005), and some have shown handwriting to elicit memory benefits over typing (Mangen et al., 2015). Therefore, recall performance for handwritten, typed, and drawn words was examined, aiming to shed light on how these different modes of encoding affect memory. The second goal was to understand the effects of notetaking strategy (i.e., modality and method) on information retention in a controlled environment. To this end, Bui et al. (2013) and Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) methodologies were integrated. This research simulated lecture-based learning to evaluate how notetaking using different modalities and methods, such as transcription versus paraphrasing, impacts memory and learning. Finally, the third goal was to explore the strategies and behaviours

NOTETAKING BY TYPING OR HANDWRITTING 13 adopted by students during the abrupt shift to online learning due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

As such, a larger-scale academic experience survey study was conducted to examine trends in notetaking strategies alongside other academic-related behaviours thought to impact student success. This study also assessed the subsequent effects on academic performance, offering real world context to the experimental findings. Taken together, this research provided an extensive re-examination of the relative effect that notetaking modality exerts on academic performance, while concurrently considering the influence of other critical behaviours associated with academic success, such as class attendance, organizational skills, and study habits.

The first chapter delves into a comprehensive review of the literature linking notetaking to academic performance, also dissecting the two main theories surrounding the topic: the encoding and external storage hypotheses. Subsequently, the second chapter addresses the ongoing debate over notetaking modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing). This chapter includes an exploration of the various changes introduced by technology in the academic sphere, the distractions associated with computer use, the role of working memory and note quantity, potential encoding benefits, potential external storage benefits, and general limitations of the research. It also provides a brief overview of the cognitive psychology of learning. The third chapter lays the groundwork for the empirical research undertaken within this dissertation, providing detailed descriptions of the methods used in the three studies. Each of the subsequent chapters (chapters 4–6) then presents a complete report for each study, including the research objectives, methods, results, and interpretations. Finally, the seventh chapter concludes the dissertation, integrating the findings of all three studies and placing them within the wider context of the literature. The conclusion further discusses educational implications, limitations, and future research directions.

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 14

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE

Research investigating student notetaking dates back almost a century when educational psychologists first began to investigate the potential benefits of the practice. Crawford (1925) sought to investigate the effects of notetaking on related quiz grades in a sample of 211 college students from seven different classes. All students were instructed to take notes as they usually would. The quiz was administered after a delay of two to 35 days. The results demonstrated a relationship between the clarity and accuracy of students’ notes and their quiz grades. However, this correlational design did not definitively show that taking notes increased academic performance. Several researchers then attempted to determine whether notetaking during a lecture, rather than simply attending to it, was beneficial to learning (e.g., Berliner, 1968;

DiVesta & Gray, 1972; DiVesta & Gray, 1973; Eisner & Rohde, 1959; Peters, 1972). Two theoretical positions were defended during this time: some advocated academic notetaking while others went as far as to forbit it (e.g., Peters, 1972). Some researchers purported that learning became inefficient with the practice of notetaking for two main reasons (DiVesta & Gray, 1972).

First, they argued that notetaking was distracting because writing interfered with paying attention to the lecture. Additionally, they worried that students might believe that taking notes was equivalent to studying. Some researchers found that lecture notetaking was neither beneficial nor detrimental to academic performance outcomes (e.g., Eisner & Rohde, 1959). Thus, the literature during this time appeared to be mixed.

These inconclusive results could be the result of the materials used in most notetaking research during this time, as they did not account for the typical pace of a lecture (Dwivedi et al., 1978). In fact, Ash and Carlton (1953) had provided evidence to suggest that the pace of the film-type experimental stimuli did not allow for the notetaking facilitation strategies often used

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 15 by instructors (e.g., pauses, emphases, and repetitions of important points). Hence, the inconsistent results seemed to stem from problems with ecological validity. Another explanation for the inconclusive results was that the relationship between notetaking and performance could be subject to the time between the presentation of materials and testing in experimental designs.

Typical experimental designs allowed participants to review the to-be-tested material either immediately following the presentation of them or immediately preceding testing. This presented a review-period confound within the research. In fact, Weiland and Kingsbury (1979) argued that the omission of a review period would be better able to isolate the effectiveness of notetaking on recall. So, their participants completed a test either immediately following the presentation of to be-tested material or after a delay. The results demonstrated that the notetakers performed better on both the immediate and delayed tests compared to those who did not take notes. However, both groups performed significantly worse on the delayed test. Weiland and Kingsbury concluded that the act of taking notes during a lecture increased the participants’ ability to retain the information. Additionally, they argued that the time between stimulus presentation and testing might have diminished the effect of notetaking on performance. Overall, once lecture pace and the inclusion of review periods were considered, it became clearer that notetaking during lectures increased student performance.

Ultimately, the positive impact of notetaking on information retention has been substantiated by numerous researchers, with evidence stemming from a multitude of research designs (e.g., Aiken et al., 1975; Ash & Carlton, 1953; Bretzing & Kulhavy, 1981; Carter & Van

Matre, 1975; Crawford, 1925; DiVesta & Gray, 1972; DiVesta & Gray, 1973; Einstein et al., 1985; Hartley & Davies, 1978; Nye et al., 1984; Weiland & Kingsbury, 1979). For instance, Nye et al. (1984) conducted a large-scale naturalistic study, gathering complete sets of notes from 75

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 16 lectures, which demonstrated a positive relationship between the quantity of notes students recorded and their examination grades. This result echoed the findings from several controlled experimental studies, such as Einstein et al. (1985), which also underscored the beneficial impact of notetaking on memory and learning. In a longitudinal design, Carter and Van Matre (1975) reported consistent benefits of notetaking over an academic year. As a result of such broad and diverse research evidence, student notetaking is now considered to be vital to academic success (Dunkel & Davy, 1989). In fact, students almost invariably decide to take notes during class, as they perceive a clear relationship between notetaking and their academic performance (Williams & Eggert, 2002).

While the effects of notetaking on academic performance are thoroughly documented, it is also worth noting that students often cite notetaking as a strategy to maintain attention during lectures and to prevent off-task thoughts, known as mind wandering (Morehead et al., 2019b).

The importance of avoiding mind wandering in academic settings cannot be understated. In their seminal work, Risko et al. (2013) presented compelling evidence that mind wandering can significantly compromise memory performance. Their research has been instrumental in advancing the field’s understanding of how attention impacts learning and has driven new lines of inquiry in the field. It is, therefore, unsurprising that students seek strategies to combat such detrimental effects. There are several proposed methods to reduce mind wandering, including somewhat unconventional ones like doodling (Andrade, 2010). However, a more traditional academic task, notetaking, appears to be superior in reducing this off-task thinking (Spencer

Mueller & Fenske, 2019). In their comprehensive study, Spencer-Mueller and Fenske found that notetaking outperformed doodling in reducing mind wandering during a 45-minute lecture. This study underscored the fundamental role of notetaking in academic settings, elucidating its dual

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 17 function in promoting both information retention and sustained attentional engagement. Hence, the multifaceted benefits of notetaking in academia are now indisputable.

Nonetheless, the precise mechanisms that underpin the positive relationship between notetaking and academic performance remain somewhat elusive. DiVesta and Gray (1972) proposed two potential explanations—the encoding and external storage hypotheses—that may independently or jointly account for the noted increase in academic performance. The encoding hypothesis posits that the act of notetaking itself during a lecture is the primary contributor to performance benefits. In contrast, the external storage hypothesis asserts that it is the resultant notes, serving as reference material for subsequent review and preparation, that facilitate performance improvement. The relative importance of these two hypotheses in affecting memory and learning has been a topic of considerable debate in the literature (see Kiewra, 1989 for a comprehensive review).

The Encoding Hypothesis

The encoding hypothesis rests on the understanding that the act of taking notes during an academic lecture can foster learning. This hypothesis is grounded in the evidence suggesting that generative processes such as notetaking enhance information retention by promoting deeper levels of encoding (Peper & Mayer, 1978). This principle of deeper processing leading to robust memory formation is encapsulated by the levels-of-processing effect (Craik & Lockhart, 1972).

One such deep processing method is generative learning, where learners actively establish links and associations among different pieces of information (Wittrock, 1974). Lecture notetaking embodies generative learning when students summarize, paraphrase, or arrange the content they record. Peper and Mayer have demonstrated that the process of taking notes fosters interconnections among concepts, as opposed to merely attending a lecture which may lead to

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 18 learning fragmented information. Thus, the improved academic performance observed due to notetaking could potentially be ascribed to this deeper level of encoding.

Several studies have evidenced a notetaking benefit even in the absence of review, indicating that the act of taking notes itself augments academic performance (e.g., Aiken et al., 1975; Bretzing & Kulhavy, 1981; Einstein et al., 1985; Kobayashi, 2005). Thomas (1978) discovered that students could recall more ideas from their own notes than from provided notes, hinting at a possible exploitation of the self-generation effect, which is the phenomenon where personally-generated ideas are more likely to be recalled than provided ones (e.g., Rabinowitz &

Craik, 1986; Scardamalia & Bereiter, 1986). Indeed, Fisher and Harris (1973) identified a positive correlation between the volume of personally recorded lecture ideas and test performance without review. This suggests that the quality and comprehensiveness of a student's notes could influence the association between notetaking's encoding benefits and performance outcomes. However, the investigation extends further, as some research focuses on an alternative explanation: the external storage hypothesis.

The External Storage Hypothesis

The external storage hypothesis suggests that learning results from the capacity to revisit the material to be evaluated. This hypothesis builds upon the understanding that the review of notes enhances retention (e.g., DiVesta & Gray, 1972; Hartley, 1983; Rickards & Friedman, 1978). It is widely accepted that reviewing aids learners in excelling in assessments such as midterms and exams. This notion has its roots in research dating back to the late 1800s, where scholars like Ebbinghaus (1885; as cited by Ebbinghaus, 2013) documented the learning advantages of multiple study sessions as opposed to a single one. This phenomenon, known as the distributed practice effect, has been found to be universally applicable regardless of the CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 19 retention interval (Cepeda et al., 2006). Essentially, every additional spaced learning session enhances the probability of retention. Furthermore, the notion of interweaving, wherein different topics are mixed within a study session, has also been suggested to improve learning outcomes (Kornell & Bjork, 2008). The principle of interweaving shares some similarities with the distributed practice effect in that it promotes variation in study materials and thus promotes deeper processing. When learners get the opportunity to revisit their notes, they engage in at least one additional encoding episode compared to those who do not review. Consequently, their retention likelihood would be higher than those who did not review their notes.

Indeed, proponents of the external storage hypothesis argue that the benefits of notetaking can be attributed to the facilitation of note review, irrespective of notetaking engagement.

Kiewra's (1985) study supports this argument, demonstrating comparable performance between students who borrowed notes for study and those who compiled their own. Predominantly, research exploring the external storage function of notetaking has examined differences between students permitted to review notes and those who were not. Hartley's (1983) review synthesized 24 studies, all substantiating the performance advantages reviewers held over non-reviewers.

However, this body of research does not exclude potential encoding benefits. Thus, advocates of the external storage hypothesis predominantly contend that the primary value in lecture notetaking lies in the possession of the lecture material (e.g., DiVesta & Gray, 1972; Hartley, 1983). Rickards and Friedman (1978), for instance, provided evidence suggesting the predominance of the external storage function of notetaking over the encoding function.

Nonetheless, they also recognized that lecture notetaking simultaneously serves both external storage and encoding functions.

CHAPTER 1: NOTETAKING AND ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE 20

Given that much research exploring the encoding benefits of notetaking relies on comparisons between notetakers and non-notetakers in the absence of review, disentangling the relative benefits of encoding and external storage remains challenging. Kiewra (1989) posited that the relative importance of each might not merit heated debates as it does little to inform best practices. Instead of advocating for the supremacy of one function over the other, Kiewra asserted that students likely accrue benefits from both efficient notetaking and reviewing before an evaluation. It is reasonable to infer that high-performing students would stand to gain from leveraging both the encoding and the external storage benefits of academic notetaking.

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY

Much of the evidence that supports the performance benefits of notetaking in the classroom focuses on the process of handwriting (Muller & Oppenheimer, 2014). However, since Kiewra’s (1989) review on the external storage and encoding benefits of notetaking, many technological innovations have changed the classroom. The advent of personal computers has had a major impact on the way university lectures are presented and recorded. For example,

PowerPoint has become a pervasive instructional tool that has begun to accompany most university lectures (Worthington & Levasseur, 2014). Moreover, students have now come to expect online copies of lecture slides to be available to them (Adams, 2006) and they report that access to slides before class helps them compile a more complete set of notes from which to study (Frey & Birnbaum, 2002). Copies of notes and slides are not the only academic-related resource now available to students online. Students can now watch recordings of live lectures online (Newbury et al., 2011). In addition to these recordings, some lectures are produced with the sole intention of online dissemination. Additionally, personal computers have become so lightweight that students can now bring them into the classroom. This means that many students may now utilize a keyboard to take their lecture notes. Thus, research concerning the effectiveness of notetaking on academic performance has begun to include handwriting and typing and has started revisiting arguments that had been presented more than half a century ago.

For instance, DiVesta and Gray (1972) argued that notetaking was distracting, and they worried that students might opt out of studying because they perceived the act of notetaking as studying in and of itself. Today, the same argument is being made about the use of computers to assist with lecture notetaking. Again, researchers worry about the distracting nature of computers (e.g., Fried, 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrooke & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007).

Moreover, if students merely transcribe their lectures and fail to review their notes, they might not encode the material sufficiently well to be adequately prepared for evaluations.

As such, it is possible that the encoding and external storage functions of notetaking could be differentially affected by notetaking modality. Students who type their notes could increase the quantity of lecture materials they record because they can typically type faster than they can write (Bui et al., 2013). Thus, the external storage hypothesis would predict that they would benefit from typing their lecture notes. However, the proficiency of speedy typing increases students’ ability to transcribe their lectures (Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014). Since the encoding hypothesis relies on generative notetaking to increase memory retention, it would predict that typists’ transcription would result in a decrement in performance.

Unfortunately, the research examining the differential effects of handwriting and typing lecture notes on learning have yielded mixed findings. Some have found that handwriting lecture notes is more conducive to learning (e.g., Blankenship, 2016; Fried, 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kodaira, 2017;

Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Manzi et al., 2017; Muller & Oppenheimer, 2014; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007), while others have found the opposite result (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Kutta, 2017; Morehead et al., 2019a; Urry et al., 2021). Given the varying results and debates around the efficacy of different notetaking modalities, this chapter aims to critically review the research on handwriting versus typing, delve into the potential mechanisms underlying their respective impacts on learning, and discuss the implications for contemporary academic practice.

Technology-Related Changes in the Academic Experience

Morehead et al. (2019b) sought to investigate the implications of technological changes on students’ notetaking behaviours. They observed that the most recent large-scale notetaking behaviour survey conducted by Palmatier and Bennett (1974) was likely outdated due to the technology-related changes that had since occurred. Morehead et al. argued that the online availability of recorded lectures and students’ ability to take notes using a keyboard might have changed that way students record the to-be-learned information. As such, they administered a 55question survey to a total of 577 students from both in-class and online lectures. The questionnaire probed the likelihood that students would take notes in class and their notetaking strategies. For instance, students were asked “do you try to organize your notes or copy what the teacher says?” The results showed that 96 percent of students reported taking notes in live classrooms, whereas a mere 49 percent of them reported doing so for their online lectures. These results suggested that students do alter their behaviours, depending on extraneous technology related factors such as lecture style. These results supported the notion that the Palmatier and Bennett survey results were outdated due to advances in modern technology. However,

Morehead et al. did not report whether the observed behavioural changes (e.g., online-lecture students opting out of notetaking) were beneficial or detrimental to student performance. One potential explanation for the notetaking differences among traditional- and online-lecture students is that students might be over-relying on the external storage benefit of the online lecture. More specifically, they might resort to re-watching their lectures as a form of studying in the same way that students might re-read their lecture notes for more traditional courses. The research on study habits has demonstrated that re-reading lecture notes is an inefficient preparation strategy for tests and exams (Dunlosky, 2011). Extrapolating from this research, it

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 24 seems probable that re-watching online lectures would have similar outcomes. In fact, recent studies have started to address the negative impacts of re-watching online lectures as a study strategy (e.g., Martin et al., 2018; Palmer & Persky, 2019). While the recent shift towards online learning has likely increased the prevalence of re-watching lectures, it may not be the most efficient method of study.

Morehead et al. (2019b) also found that approximately half of their sample reported taking notes with the use of their laptop. While much of their sample reported attempting to organize their notes (56 to 70%) rather than copy what the teacher says (25 to 30%), most of them considered bullet listing as organized notetaking (83 to 85%) rather than rephrasing (36 to 42%). Since it is still possible to transcribe at least partially using bullet points, these statistics do not distinguish paraphrased notetaking from transcription. Most of the sample also reported a flexible notetaking approach using either a laptop or a notebook depending on the lecture pace and the availability of PowerPoint slides. However, it remains unclear as to whether the choices students make about their notetaking strategies would differentially impact their learning.

Some studies have found that students report benefiting from using their laptops for lecture notetaking (Barak et al., 2006; Mitra & Steffensmeier, 2000; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008).

Unfortunately, this self-reported data does not appear to match the findings within the literature; that is, students who use laptops in the classroom are outperformed by their counterparts who do not (e.g., Fried; 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010). Moreover, Wurst et al. (2008) found that students who tended to use computers in the classroom reported understanding that it might impede their performance. They also reported having less overall satisfaction with their education. It is important to note that this correlational evidence does not imply that typing lecture notes causes students to be dissatisfied with their education. Still, there

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 25 appeared to be a consensus among researchers; that is, students who aspire to succeed in learning should handwrite their notes rather than type them (e.g., Blankenship, 2016; Fried, 2008; Grace

Martin & Gay, 2001; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011;

Kodaira, 2017; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Manzi et al., 2017; Muller & Oppenheimer, 2014;

Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007). One important caveat to consider is that it was unclear why handwriting would enhance performance more than typing.

Computers are Distracting

One plausible explanation for a benefit of handwriting notes rather than typing them is that computers create distractions (e.g., internet browsing, mail notifications, social media) that a pen and paper would otherwise not (Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrooke & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013;

Yamamoto, 2007). For instance, Fried (2008) aimed to discern whether computer-related distractions significantly detracted from students’ performance. To this end, 137 students from two general psychology courses completed weekly surveys and an end of term questionnaire regarding their laptop use and classroom experiences. The results showed that students who took notes using laptops in class tended to engage in unrelated activities (e.g., checking email, instant messaging, surfing the net, playing games) more than others. Overwhelmingly, the students who reported computer-related multitasking also reported paying less attention to lectures. Gaudreau et al. (2014) also examined undergraduate students’ in-class laptop use, but in relation to both academic satisfaction and performance (i.e., GPA). Unsurprisingly, they found that school unrelated laptop behaviours were associated with a decrease in academic satisfaction and performance. While they found that school-related laptop behaviours were positively related to satisfaction, there was only a weak relationship to performance. Moreover, they found that

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 26 students who took notes with a computer did worse on performance tests than those who took notes by hand.

Nevertheless, students can obviously become distracted in class even without computers (Kane et al., 2017).
