Even though the primary reason for notetaking is the possession of recorded material for subsequent study, students often report that notetaking helps them attend to the lecture (Morehead et al., 2019b). This suggests that at least some students are using notetaking as a strategy to increase sustained attention and thus avoid off-task thinking. Hence, computers may provide difficult to resist opportunities for multitasking and task-switching (Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007) and it has been shown that the former negatively impacts performance (Rubinstein et al., 2001). So, the literature on notetaking modality appeared to support the following conclusion: portable computers hinder students’ academic performance.

However, it is crucial to consider that not all computer usage in the classroom may be detrimental. In fact, a study by Risko et al. (2013) indicated that any diversionary computer use may simply be a substitute for mind wandering. In other words, students might not necessarily be more distracted with a computer than without one; that is, they may simply shift their distractions to a digital format. So, while it appears that laptops might contribute to more visible distractions in the classroom, the tendency for students to get sidetracked might be independent of the technology available to them. Therefore, the relation between notetaking modality and academic performance may be more nuanced than what the current literature suggests, warranting further investigations into the interplay of notetaking strategies, student behaviours, and classroom technologies.

While some attempts have been made to suggest that computers in the classroom as distracting, it is also important to explore the potential benefits of digital notetaking. In contrast to the consensus that handwriting may enhance learning, Bui et al. (2013) introduced an intriguing proposition that typing could improve performance for certain students. Specifically, they posited that students with lower working memory capacity might incur certain advantages from typing lecture notes, assuming they have adequate typing skills. Normally, students cannot write as quickly as a lecturer speaks, which creates a cognitively demanding task of listening to the lecture, understanding the content, retaining it in memory, identifying significant information, and ideally paraphrasing it to jot down the key points. Considering these demands, typing could arguably serve as a strategy to lessen cognitive load by facilitating more efficient transcription of comprehensive lecture notes. Bui et al. proceeded to design experiments aiming to contrast notetaking modalities (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) and methods (i.e., organized vs.

transcribed), focusing on their impact on working memory and subsequent test performance.

Bui et al.’s (2013) first experiment involved 80 undergraduate students who were instructed to listen to an 11-minute nonfiction passage, spoken at an average speed of 140 words per minute. Depending on the condition, the participants were equipped with either a pen and paper or a computer for notetaking, with the intent of preparing for an upcoming test. Half the group was tasked with paraphrasing and organizing their notes, while the remaining half were directed to "capture as much of the lecture as feasible" (p. 301). Prior to the lecture, participants underwent a reading span task and a lexical decision task to measure their working memory ability and processing speed, respectively. Right after the lecture, they were presented with an 18-question test that comprised eight questions on critical details and 10 on less significant ones.

From their analysis, Bui et al. (2013) discovered that notes taken by those in the typing condition consisted of more idea units than those by their handwritten counterparts, and transcribed notes had more idea units than organized ones. Interestingly, the transcription instruction resulted in more idea units only in the typed notes. As a crucial finding, transcription appeared to drive the most effective recall performance.

Following Experiment 1, Bui et al. (2013) conducted two additional experiments that both replicated the first. Moreover, they revealed that transcription led to reduced performance after some time had passed and that organized notetaking boosted performance when study opportunities were absent. This outcome underscores the significance of the encoding hypothesis. Furthermore, Bui et al. combined the participant groups from their three experiments to analyze the influence of working memory on notetaking. Before the participants took the test, they underwent a free recall task, which involved the recall of as much information from the lecture as possible without any cues. The analysis demonstrated a correlation between working memory and note quantity as well as the amount of information recalled for the participants who adopted organized notetaking. However, this correlation was absent for those who transcribed their notes. Consequently, Bui et al. posited that these findings supported their hypothesis that transcription can be a viable strategy for students to alleviate the working memory demands during notetaking.

Overall, Bui et al. (2013) convincingly demonstrated that those students who opted for transcription of the lecture yielded superior performance compared to their counterparts.

Nevertheless, a notable limitation in Bui et al.'s research was the use of the same computer for both notetaking and presenting the nonfiction passage. This represents a rather unusual setup. In typical post-secondary lecture settings, students must juggle multiple visual and auditory

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 29 elements, such as the lecture's discourse, PowerPoint presentations, and blackboard writings.

Furthermore, while students might take notes on the same device when learning online, they typically take notes on a distinct device when learning in more traditional face-to-face learning formats. The same computer was also utilized for test administration in Bui et al.'s study, which, although convenient, might have influenced the outcomes. Traditional post-secondary testing predominantly relies on handwriting. Furthermore, the principle of transfer appropriate processing (Morris, et al., 1977) might elucidate the enhanced performance demonstrated by the typists. This interpretation suggests that the congruence between the method of information encoding and retrieval, in this case typing, might have amplified the odds of participant recollection. Consequently, those typing their notes might have been at an advantage over those who were handwriting their notes.

Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) also sought to investigate the impact of notetaking modality on memory retention, positing that keyboard-based notetaking could lead to a decrease in performance due to its facilitation of verbatim-style notetaking. This non-generative form of notetaking leads to shallower encoding levels. Moreover, they suggested that Bui et al.'s (2013) results might have been influenced by their explicit instruction for participants to transcribe content, rather than encouraging a more natural notetaking style. Additionally, they believed that allowing participants to adopt their own notetaking strategy would directly address the natural variations in verbatim overlap (i.e., the textual overlap between students' notes and the lecture transcript), an aspect they believed was inadequately represented in the existing literature. They also noted that prior research had predominantly used factual, rather than conceptual, test questions, which they believed limited the generalizability of the research. Thus, Mueller and Oppenheimer set out to further explore the influence of notetaking strategies on academic performance.

Their experiment involved 67 undergraduate students who were asked to watch five 15minute TED Talks projected at the front of the room. Depending on their assigned condition, students were given either laptops (without internet connectivity) or notebooks with pens and asked to take notes in preparation for a subsequent test, much as they would in their regular classes. A reading span task was subsequently completed to measure working memory, followed by a test on the talks' factual and conceptual aspects 30 minutes later. The results revealed equal performance across conditions for factual questions, whereas for conceptual questions, participants who handwrote their notes outperformed those in the laptop condition. Additionally, they found that laptop notes exhibited a higher word count and textual overlap with the lecture transcripts compared to handwritten notes. Interestingly, while a higher word count positively predicted performance, textual overlap negatively predicted performance. Thus, their findings suggested that note quantity only boosts performance when students avoided verbatim transcription of lectures. Crucially, even after adjusting for the benefits of higher word count, the predictive power of handwritten notetaking on performance was only marginally significant.

However, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) were unable to draw any conclusions on differences between textual overlap and idea units retained due to the absence of idea unit measurements in their study. This was a chief concern they had with Bui et al.'s (2013) research.

It could be possible that the number of idea units retained is unrelated to verbatim overlap.

Moreover, students may derive benefits from recording a large number of idea units with minimal textual overlap. Furthermore, Mueller and Oppenheimer's results do not rule out the potential of successful paraphrasing through typing. Therefore, proficient typists who can

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 31 effectively paraphrase may not experience the predicted performance decrement associated with typing notes. Despite these limitations, Mueller and Oppenheimer's research has gained significant traction in the field, with over 2000 citations and an altmetric score of 4590 by 2023.

Morehead et al. (2019a) conducted a study directly replicating Mueller and Oppenheimer's (2014) methodology and aimed to further investigate the differing effects of handwriting and typing on learning. Utilizing a sample of 188 students and adopting a 3 (method: handwritten, laptop, eWriter) x 5 (video) x 2 (test group: immediate and delayed, delayed only) between-subjects design, their methods largely mirrored those of Mueller and Oppenheimer. However, they made a few key modifications: they balanced the number of testing points per test type (i.e., factual or conceptual) and they investigated the impact of notetaking after a longer retention interval instead of confining the research to an immediate test as Mueller and Oppenheimer had done. As such, half of their participants took an immediate test, while all participants took the delayed test. This modification aimed to avoid the reactive effect of test enhanced learning effects (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006). In contrast to Mueller and Oppenheimer's results, this experiment found a handwriting superiority effect for factual questions only. There was no observed difference in performance for conceptual questions, which contradicts Mueller and Oppenheimer's findings. Additionally, when a delay was included before the test, participants performed worse than those in the immediate testing group, but this was also only true for factual questions. Interestingly, for the delayed testing groups, no superiority effect was found for either laptop or handwritten notetaking.

Potential Encoding Benefits

To investigate potential encoding benefits of notetaking, Morehead et al., (2019a) carried out a second experiment. This experiment contrasted the handwritten and laptop notetaking

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 32 groups against a group that did not take notes at all, within a sample of 217 undergraduate students. Consequently, they employed a 4 (method: handwritten, laptop, eWriter, no notes) x 2 (test group: immediate and delay, delay only) x 2 (video) between-subjects design. They maintained the procedure from their first experiment but introduced an additional group that did not take notes and therefore only participated in the immediate test. In their findings, no distinctions were noted between notetaking groups on either factual or conceptual questions.

Furthermore, no differences were found among the notetaking conditions for the immediate testing results. However, a trend toward a laptop-superiority effect was apparent for both the immediate and delayed tests. Notably, even though the laptop group had a higher word count compared to the other groups, neither the extent of verbatim overlap nor the number of idea units recorded distinguished the groups.

In sum, the results from Morehead et al. (2019a) differed from those of Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014). While Experiment 1 displayed that verbatim overlap was higher for the laptop group, thus replicating Mueller and Oppenheimer's findings, Experiment 2 revealed no differences between the notetaking method groups. Additionally, their results indicated no performance differences between these groups. In some cases, their findings even leaned toward favouring a laptop superiority effect. So, even though Mueller and Oppenheimer’s results have become prominent within the literature, Morehead et al. argued that the acceptance of their take home message may have been premature.

Potential External Storage Benefits

In yet another attempt to replicate Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) results, Urry et al.

(2021) conducted a direct replication with 142 participants. As the participants watched one of the five 15-minute TED Talks used in Mueller and Oppenheimer’s original study, half of them

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 33 were assigned to take notes with a computer, while the other half used pen and paper. Upon completion of a 5-minute distractor task, participants (without having an opportunity for review) undertook the same open-ended comprehension quiz that was deployed in the original study.

Urry et al.'s findings aligned with Mueller and Oppenheimer’s results in that typists were more inclined to take verbatim-style notes as compared to those who handwrote their notes.

Furthermore, typists' notes encompassed more content than those of the handwriting notetakers.

However, they did not discover a handwriting superiority effect. Rather, Urry et al. found no significant performance differences between the typing and writing conditions.

To further scrutinize this null result, Urry et al. (2021) conducted a mini meta-analysis, comparing eight different studies aimed at investigating performance differences between typing and handwriting. This analysis appeared to support their conclusion; that is, there seems to be no discernible difference in test performance between handwriting and typing notetakers. Despite this, the meta-analytic review showed a difference in note quantity, with keyboard notetakers generating more content and exhibiting more verbatim overlap in their notes. This suggests that the storage benefit of notetaking may be influenced by opting to take notes by hand, while the encoding benefit of notetaking seems less impacted by the choice of typing.

One limitation with Urry et al.’s (2021) experiment is that they did not consider idea units. Even though typed notes were more plentiful than handwritten ones, it could have been the case that handwritten (and paraphrased) notes contained the same amount of idea units with fewer words. Nevertheless, these replication failures underscored the necessity for further research into the differences between notetaking modalities before recommending students to abstain from laptop notetaking.

General Limitations of the Research

The research conducted by Bui et al. (2013) and Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014), along with the unsuccessful replication attempts by Morehead et al. (2019a) and Urry et al. (2021), all share some common limitations, which this present research aims to address. First, a consistent issue has been the interchangeable and inconsistent use of terminology. For example, the terms “transcription” and “verbatim” are often used synonymously. Likewise, the references to “paraphrased” and “organized” notetaking tend to be treated as identical. Additionally, the term “longhand” has been deployed to refer to any type of handwritten notetaking.

As an illustration, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) refer to all handwriting as “longhand,” a term that suggests fully formed words and sentences. Another method of writing lecture notes, however, could be shorthand, traditionally defined as the symbolic representation of words or sounds (Gard, 1923). While it is unlikely that today's undergraduate students are well-versed in established shorthand systems (e.g., Gregg or Script shorthand), they could very well use common abbreviations, symbols, and jottings instead of complete words and phrases.

As such, potential differences might exist between notetakers who use true longhand and those employing a more shorthand-like system. Moreover, the use of a shorthand-like system could possibly enable students to write information as rapidly as they might type it. Despite its potential relevance, the literature makes no reference to this style of lecture notetaking, and it is plausible that students may be attempting to use longhand, shorthand, or some hybrid-style notes.

Importantly, these differing notetaking methods might involve unique processes with varied recall outcomes. Such a possibility necessitates further exploration to uncover any meaningful impacts on learning and academic performance.

Indeed, related research has demonstrated that individuals tend to recall more words that they have drawn compared to those they have written, a phenomenon known as the drawing effect (Wammes et al., 2016). Interestingly, this effect could not be attributed to individual types of memory codes such as levels of processing, visual imagery, or picture superiority. Rather, the researchers posited that the act of drawing to-be-remembered information fosters the integration of various types of memory codes into a unified and consistent memory trace. They suggested that this process of integration enhances retrieval. As such, it remains plausible that the encoding of symbolic information might improve the likelihood of retrieval. Because handwriting inherently enables symbolic-type notetaking compared to typing, individuals might experience a handwriting effect akin to the drawing effect. Exploring these methods could help pinpoint the encoding benefits of handwriting and elucidate the source of the effect. Subsequently, research could expand to demonstrate similar effects in the complex nature of notetaking during a lecture.

Nevertheless, it is important to note that research within the realm of handwritten lecture notetaking has indicated that the encoding of material during notetaking is not the only advantage of taking notes in class. As suggested by researchers, there is also an external storage benefit (e.g., Hartley, 1983). However, the introduction of laptops in the classroom might have altered how these two benefits affect performance outcomes linked to notetaking. For instance, typing notes on a keyboard could enhance students' ability to leverage the external storage benefit of notetaking, as most students can type faster than they can write by hand, potentially resulting in a larger volume of notes for subsequent review. This proposition, though, relies on the assumption that students who handwrite their notes primarily paraphrase the content.

Conversely, typing notes might diminish students' ability to capitalize on the encoding benefit of CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 36 notetaking, especially if they are more likely to employ non-generative notetaking strategies when typing.

Along this line of inquiry, Luo et al. (2018) sought to differentiate the effects of notetaking modality on academic performance by distinguishing between the encoding and external storage benefits. They posited that these notetaking benefits could vary in their value and effectiveness based on whether learners were handwriting or typing their notes. To test this proposition, they analyzed students' performance when randomly assigned to either record and review lecture notes or to record notes without the opportunity to review them afterwards. In their study, participants watched a 23-minute PowerPoint presentation that included both image and text-related content, supplemented with narration. Half of the participants took notes by hand, while the other half used laptops for notetaking. After a 30-minute distractor task, all participants completed a multiple-choice test.

Luo et al.'s (2018) results were nuanced. They found that when reviewing notes was not an option, typists' performance did not deteriorate as much as might have been expected given the common notion of shallow-level encoding associated with typed notes (Mueller &

Oppenheimer, 2014). In other words, typists seemed to benefit more from the encoding process during notetaking than from the external storage of the notes they produced. This implies that even without reviewing the notes, typists were able to extract substantial benefit from the act of notetaking itself. Conversely, the participants who handwrote their notes exhibited better performance when allowed to review their notes, suggesting that for these students, the external storage function of notetaking was more advantageous. Thus, when note review was not an option, the performance of the handwriting group fell short of the typists' performance.

Intriguingly, Luo et al. (2018) also found a distinct pattern for image-related content.

Regardless of the notetaking modality, participants performed better on image-related test items when they were not allowed to review their notes. This finding could be potentially linked to

Wammes et al.’s (2016) drawing effect since participants might have been more likely to engage in symbolic notetaking while dealing with image-related content, thus facilitating a deeper encoding process. These unexpected results challenge the conventional wisdom around notetaking, suggesting that the relative effectiveness of the encoding and external storage functions of notetaking might not be as straightforward as previously assumed, particularly when considering the complex interactions with notetaking modalities and the types of information being processed.

The intricate findings of Luo et al. (2018), Bui et al. (2013), Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014), and Morehead et al. (2019a) certainly emphasize the complexity inherent in the interplay between notetaking modality and academic performance. This body of work reveals that numerous factors, including but not limited to presentation rate, working memory capacity, and review periods, can significantly influence the impact of notetaking on learning outcomes.

However, these studies predominantly focus on the micro-level dynamics of notetaking, leaving the broader context largely unexplored. The integration of notetaking strategies within the wider ecosystem of academic-related behaviours such as class attendance, effective study habits, time management, and use of additional learning resources is crucial for a holistic understanding of its role in academic success. Such an integrative perspective would illuminate how notetaking, as an individual learning strategy, interconnects with and influences a constellation of other academic behaviours, potentially affecting overall educational outcomes. With this in mind, the following section will delve deeper into the broader academic behavioural framework within which

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 38 notetaking strategies operate. These aspects will be critically discussed to highlight the need for comprehensive investigations that consider notetaking as an element of a larger, multifaceted academic performance picture, rather than an isolated entity. This integrative approach would provide a richer and more nuanced understanding of the impact of notetaking on students' academic success.

The Cognitive Psychology of Learning

The goal of this current research was to thoroughly examine the impact of notetaking modality on students' learning, as manifested in their academic performance. However, it is important to recognize that there is an array of academic-related behaviours that significantly contribute to a student's overall success in academia, which are not directly linked to notetaking.

While the ability to guide students on whether to use laptops for notetaking in class is certainly beneficial, researchers must probe further into how much notetaking strategies, in a broader sense, contribute to academic achievement. Furthermore, the prescriptive nature of advice often provided to students on optimizing durable learning tends to overlook the variability in lecturing styles. Given that lectures vary greatly in their delivery, it is vital to equip students with the tools to discern and adopt effective learning strategies that align with the diverse demands of different courses. It is also essential for students to comprehend how their preferences for certain academic-related behaviours influence their performance outcomes. A significant part of the challenges students face when transitioning from high school to university could be attributed to their understanding—or lack thereof—of effective learning strategies.

For the purpose of the present research, individual academic-related behaviours will be categorized into six individual groups: class attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognitive skills. Importantly, these behaviours will be

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 39 considered as distinct from, albeit closely intertwined with, notetaking strategies (i.e., the primary focus of this research). This allows for a holistic analysis of not only the role of notetaking but also the interplay of these behaviours in influencing academic success.

Class attendance. Empirical evidence has consistently emphasized the role of class attendance in promoting learning (Jenne, 1973) and improving grades (Moore, 2003), despite historical records of high rates of class absenteeism among students (e.g., Friedman et al., 2001;

Marburger, 2001). Butler (2007) corroborated these findings in a study involving 253 full-time college students. By implementing the four-item School Attendance Scale, Butler demonstrated a positive correlation between class attendance and overall academic performance. Notably,

Kassarnig et al. (2017) observed that prior research establishing the connection between class attendance and academic performance was predominantly reliant on self-report data. To mitigate this limitation, they used location data gleaned from the smartphones of approximately 1000 undergraduate students, thereby establishing a robust correlation between class attendance and academic performance. Additionally, in a meta-analytic review, Credé et al. (2010) scrutinized the correlation between class attendance and corresponding grades. Remarkably, they discovered that class attendance superseded other recognized academic predictor variables such as standardized testing scores and study habits, in its capacity to predict class grades. This underlines the significance of class attendance as a major determinant of academic performance.

Study habits. It may seem intuitive that the amount of time students dedicate to studying would correlate directly with their academic performance. Yet, numerous studies have only identified a weak, and at times, inconsistent connection between these variables (e.g., Allen et al., 1972; Beer & Beer, 1992; Gortner et al., 2000; Wagstaff & Mahmoudi, 1976). Plant et al.

(2004) conducted a study with 88 participants to explain this discrepancy. When they controlled

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 40 for skill and ability, study time did predict students' grades, suggesting that merely accounting for study time without considering the quality of studying can lead to inconclusive results.

Unfortunately, the mere quantity of study time does not guarantee high grades as studying can encompass a range of activities, including passive reading of lecture notes. Dunlosky et al.

(2013) found that students often mistook highlighting and underlining text as effective studying, despite these strategies' limited capacity to facilitate learning. Similarly, Hartwig and Dunlosky (2011) discovered that students often engaged in counterproductive study habits such as last minute cramming before exams.

Contrastingly, there is ample evidence pointing towards the effectiveness of certain study strategies. For instance, the principle of spaced practice (Carpenter et al., 2012) proposes that distributing studying over time yields better results compared to cramming (Dunlosky et al., 2010; Pashler et al., 2007). The generation effect (Rabinowitz & Craik, 1986) suggests that information creation, as opposed to passive consumption, enhances memory retention. This effect supports strategies like creating flashcards, paraphrased notetaking, and peer mentoring.

Elaboration, or linking new information to existing knowledge (Chi et al., 1989), supports study strategies such as self-testing, pre-class reading of texts, and attempting comprehension questions before reading the relevant text. Furthermore, retrieval practice, or recalling information, has been shown to enhance durable learning (Karpicke & Roediger, 2008). Despite their effectiveness, these strategies might seem time-consuming and challenging compared to the illusion of learning offered by passive activities such as re-reading lecture notes or re-watching lectures. However, adopting effective study strategies should be a priority for students to achieve academic success.

Organizational skills. Academic stress is often exacerbated by the responsibility to adhere to externally imposed deadlines (Morgan, 2003). Students need to strategize to meet such deadlines while concurrently managing other non-academic tasks in their lives. A good starting point for students to stay organized is using course outlines to map out their semester and set reminders for important deadlines, starting from the first day of class (Putnam et al., 2016).

Despite feeling as though their time might be better invested in studying, research has shown that this preliminary organization can predict academic performance, as measured by GPA (Dickenson & O’Connel, 1990). Moreover, good organizational habits have been found to mitigate stress levels (Cooper & Cartwright, 1994). Considering that high levels of cortisol (the hormone associated with stress) have been found to impair executive functioning (McCormick et al., 2007), maintaining organization and planning for the semester can possibly also foster enhanced focus while studying. Thus, organizational skills may indirectly influence academic performance through stress reduction and improved cognitive functioning.

Attentional skills. The importance of attention as a predictor of academic success begins early in education, as it is one of the critical indicators of school readiness in children (Duncan et al., 2007). Studies have demonstrated that attentional skills, such as task persistence and self regulation, continue to play a significant role in predicting academic performance up to young adulthood, even when controlling for cognitive ability (e.g., McClelland et al., 2000). This connection makes intuitive sense when attention is defined as a learner's ability to concentrate on pertinent information while concurrently filtering out distractions. In the context of higher education, these attentional skills are of paramount importance. For instance, during lectures, students must strive to focus on essential concepts and details within the presented material, while ignoring extraneous sensory stimuli. Similarly, while studying, students must either

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 42 adeptly ignore distractions or find an environment devoid of such disturbances. Much of the research highlighting the importance of attention in academic success is rooted in studies exploring the negative impact of attention disorders on academic performance. For instance, it is well-established that students with attention-deficit disorders, such as ADHD, often struggle with learning (e.g., Langberg et al., 2008). This suggests that attentional skills can significantly impact academic success, particularly in challenging learning environments.

Adaptability. The ability to flexibly adjust one's study strategies according to diverse teaching styles and learning environments is an often overlooked but essential aspect of effective learning. Many of the self-regulated learning strategies discussed thus far, such as effective notetaking, maintaining organization, and improving attentional skills, have been shown to predict student performance across different lecturing styles and content. However, students may also need to be adaptable, choosing different strategies depending on the nature of the course (e.g., the modality of lectures and types of evaluation; Cleary & Zimmerman, 2004).

Adaptability, defined as the ability to modify one's behaviour in response to varying and evolving situations (VandenBos, 2007), becomes especially important in the context of learning in higher education. Students often face uncertain and novel learning environments and must use appropriate academic-related behaviours to navigate these challenges successfully. Variations between courses are common due to factors such as instructors' teaching styles, lecture formats, testing styles, learning outcomes, course materials, and prerequisites.

The importance of adaptability in academic success has been validated through research.

Martin et al. (2013) developed an Adaptability Scale that is appropriate within higher learning.

Through a longitudinal study involving 959 students, they found that adaptability significantly predicted academic outcomes. Further, Collie and Martin (2017) argued that the transition from

CHAPTER 2: NOTETAKING MODALITY 43 high school to university, which often involves new academic experiences, necessitates student adaptability. In a study with 186 undergraduates, they found that adaptability had an indirect effect on academic success, as measured by GPA. Specifically, adaptability was positively related to academic engagement, which in turn predicted students' GPA scores. Therefore, adaptability in higher education settings appears to be critically linked to academic success.

Metacognitive skills. Metacognition encompasses the higher-order cognitive processes that enable individuals to actively monitor, regulate, and appraise their cognitive processes and learning strategies (Flavell, 1979). It incorporates metacognitive knowledge, which pertains to an individual's awareness of their cognitive processes, and metacognitive regulation, which refers to the ability to oversee and adapt those processes. Both these elements contribute significantly to academic success as they promote effective learning strategies and self-regulation (Tanner, 2012). Research has indicated that students with higher metacognitive skills are more likely to excel in their academics (Schneider & Preckel, 2017). Moreover, training to enhance metacognitive skills has been demonstrated to positively influence students' learning outcomes (Sitzmann & Ely, 2011). Certainly, nurturing metacognitive skills, including planning, monitoring, and assessing one's learning processes, is vital for university students to maximize their academic performance. Therefore, while considering the impact of notetaking modalities on academic success, it is essential to take into account the role of metacognitive skills in modulating learning efficacy and performance.

CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH OVERVIEW 44

CHAPTER 3: OVERVIEW OF THE PRESENT RESEARCH

Having explored the role of various individual academic-related behaviours, such as class attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognitive beliefs, it is apparent that these factors contribute significantly to academic success. However, their interplay with notetaking strategies, a central aspect of academic behaviour, remains to be further exposed. This understanding is essential for a more holistic approach towards academic performance and will be explored throughout the Academic Experience Survey study. First, the research presented focuses on the re-evaluation of the relationship between notetaking modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) and method (i.e., transcription vs. paraphrasing) on academic performance outcomes. Historically, notetaking modality in the literature has been delineated as either typing or handwriting. However, for the purposes of this present research, notetaking is further defined as either transcribed or paraphrased. Transcription notetaking represents lecture notes that have been described as verbatim-style or word-for-word copying. Conversely, paraphrased notetaking identifies any means of condensing or organizing one’s notes using strategies such as symbolizing and concept mapping. Thus, the complex landscape of notetaking strategies was explored across four conditions: transcription handwritten, paraphrased handwritten, transcription typed, and paraphrased typed.

To this end, several experiments (presented in Chapter 4 & 5) and one study (presented in

Chapter 6) were implemented. First, to provide a deeper understanding of the effect of notetaking modality and method on academic performance, a series of experiments was designed to meticulously examine the impact of handwriting on memory recall, starting from the most fundamental unit of language: single words. The initial experiment in the series aimed to establish a foundational understanding of the potential handwriting effect on memory. In this

CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH OVERVIEW 45 experiment, participants were prompted to interact with words in different ways as they appeared on the screen: drawing, writing, and typing. After a brief distractor task, they were then surprised with a one-minute free recall task, where they were asked to remember as many words as possible without consideration for the order. Following the first experiment, a between-subjects version was executed, with each participant either drawing, writing, or typing words depending on their assigned condition. This experiment aimed to further clarify the potential influence of different modes of interaction with the words on memory recall. Lastly, the third experiment served to further refine the field’s understanding by focusing only on writing and typing words, eliminating the drawing prompt. This adjustment allowed for a more direct comparison between the effects of handwriting and typing on memory recall. These experiments were designed in succession, each building on the learnings from the previous one. They serve to illuminate the relationship between notetaking modality, specifically handwriting and typing, and memory recall, which is a critical consideration for understanding academic performance. The results and in-depth discussion will be presented in the fourth chapter, illuminating the real-world implications of these findings for student learning strategies and practices.

Transitioning from the foundational experiments exploring the handwriting effect on memory, the research then shifted into a more complex and contextually relevant domain: the lecture environment. This part of the research drew upon the methodologies of Bui et al. (2013) and Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014), aiming to bridge their insights and extend the understanding of notetaking performance in a simulated lecture setting. This experiment was constructed to dive deeper into the potential performance differences stemming from variations in notetaking strategies. This design sought to create a more real-world application of the previous experiments, integrating the complexities of the lecture context, including the speed of CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH OVERVIEW 46 information delivery, the need for real-time processing, and the cognitive load associated with comprehending and notetaking concurrently. Recognizing the role individual differences might play in notetaking, measurements were taken for working memory and typing proficiency. By controlling for these variables, the research aimed to isolate the effect of notetaking modality and method on learning outcomes. Moreover, note quantity was assessed through character counts, and the degree of textual overlap between students' notes and the lecture transcript was used to distinguish between notetaking methods. Increased overlap suggested transcription, implying a more direct, verbatim copy of the lecture content. This experiment was a significant step in situating the research in a more authentic academic context, allowing for insights directly applicable to student learning within the dynamic environment of university lectures. The outcomes and implications are explored in depth in the fifth chapter.

Rounding off the research undertakings was the Academic Experience Survey study, the focus of which extended beyond the immediate dynamics of notetaking. This study sought to situate notetaking within a broader network of academic behaviours, such as class attendance and study habits, to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the determinants of academic performance. The Academic Experience Survey study made use of a questionnaire administered to two cohorts of undergraduate students enrolled in Introductory Psychology courses. The questionnaire served as an instrument to probe the different notetaking strategies employed by the students and how these strategies co-existed with other academic behaviours. One critical variable to consider was the unique circumstances under which these cohorts studied. The first cohort was surveyed during the Fall 2020 semester, a time marked by the complete and sudden transition to online learning due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The second cohort was surveyed in the subsequent Fall 2021 semester, providing a window into how students might have adapted

CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH OVERVIEW 47 their academic behaviours after a year of online learning. Notably, with the students' consent, final grades were collected from their instructors, providing a concrete measure of academic performance. This allowed for the correlation of observed academic behaviours with actual performance outcomes. This holistic examination of academic behaviours, with notetaking as a focal point, presented in Chapter 6, sheds further light on the nuanced interplay between academic behaviours and performance in a real-world setting.

Insofar, this dissertation has sought to present a nuanced understanding of the elements that contribute to academic success, especially students’ notetaking strategies, as defined by both modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) and method (i.e., paraphrasing vs. transcribing). The upcoming chapters will dive deeper into the research conducted, reflecting on three central studies that provide in-depth insights into the relationship between notetaking modality and method on academic performance outcomes. These studies, moving from controlled experiments to a more holistic and authentic academic context, will reveal new dimensions of understanding, bridging the gap between empirical research and real-world application. They serve as the crux of this dissertation, each building upon the next, offering a comprehensive examination of the handwriting effect on memory, notetaking strategies within a lecture environment, and the broader interplay of academic behaviours.

I anticipate that these insights will echo beyond the confines of this dissertation, impacting future research, education policies, and most importantly, the students at the centre of this endeavour. The comprehensive understanding generated through this research forms a solid foundation, setting the stage for the conclusion in Chapter 7, where I will revisit these themes and synthesize the findings to draw conclusions about the complex realm of academic success.

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRTING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS

Which Modality Results in Superior Recall for Students:

Handwriting, Typing, or Drawing?

Richardson, L. & Lacroix, G.

Carleton University

Lindsay Richardson, Carleton University, lindsayrichardson@cunet.carleton.ca

1125 Colonel By Dr, Ottawa, ON K1S 5B6

Guy Lacroix, Carleton University, guylacroix@cunet.carleton.ca

## Abstract

One of the most common interests among cognitive psychologists is establishing ways to enhance human learning. An additional layer of complexity has been brought on by the rapid evolution of technology. Specifically, examining if the mechanisms involved in typing differ from those involved in handwriting. The literature concerning the implications of encoding modality on memory have been inconclusive. This present research examined whether encoding modality resulted in performance differences for word recall. Wammes et al.’s (2016) drawing versus handwriting methodology was utilized with the addition of a typing condition. The results replicated the drawing effect, whereby drawn words were better recalled than handwritten ones.

Overall, the evidence did not suggest that the mechanisms involved in handwriting led to better free recall than those involved in typing. However, if the pen is indeed mightier than the keyboard (Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014), then the effect is not explained by visual attention or sensorimotor action differences between modalities. Implications for education are discussed.

Keywords: drawing, handwriting, typing, recall, memory

Handwriting, Typing, or Drawing?

For decades, cognitive psychologists have sought to identify ways to promote more effective recall and these efforts have yielded many well-known memory enhancing phenomena.

For instance, expanding on a concept (i.e., the elaboration effect; Craik & Tulving, 1975), reading aloud (i.e., the production effect; MacLeod et al., 2010), and creating something (i.e., the generation effect; Karpicke & Roediger, 2008) have all been shown to increase one’s likelihood of remembering encoded information. More recent research has focused on the influence of motor movement on memory. This field, known as embodied cognition, examines the influence that the mind-body connection has on memory and learning (Stolz, 2015). It has produced many fascinating findings. For example, participants who perform actions or observe others doing them remember the actions more accurately than those who simply hear a verbal description (i.e., enactment effect; Madan & Singhal, 2012). Even movements that are unrelated to words can improve memory. In fact, Sullivan et al. (2018) found that the act of pulling words written on paper toward oneself rather than pushing them away increased the likelihood of recall. They dubbed this phenomenon the mere ownership effect, whereby information related to one’s possessions is well-remembered (Cunningham, 2008).

Research on embodied cognition is directly relevant to determining whether the memory benefit offered by digital encoding strategies differ from those associated with analogue methods. This present research aims to explore the effect that encoding modality has on one’s recall ability. Handwriting has been used as a means of encoding for centuries, but relatively recent technological advances have allowed people to use a keyboard instead. Ostensibly, the mechanisms involved in typing on a keyboard are not the same as those involved in handwriting.

If typing is used as a method for encoding information, it makes sense that psychologists would be interested in whether it offers similar memorial benefits. Longcamp et al. (2005) explored this idea with a sample of pre-literate children aged 3 to 5. They were asked to either handwrite or type letters with feedback from the experimenter. Then, they completed a letter recognition task.

Longcamp et al. found that the older children, but not the younger ones, recognized more letters if they had handwritten them instead of having typed them. This result supports the argument that typing and handwriting involve distinct sensorimotor processes. With handwriting, the relationship between movement and the shape of the letter (or word) is learned over time.

Conversely, with typing, one merely needs to locate a key on a keyboard and tap it.

Even though handwriting might yield better memory for letters or strings of letters, the same might not be true for words. When semantics are applied to strings of letters, as they are with real lexicon words, the mechanism of encoding differs because the encoder can group the letters of the string into one meaningful unit (i.e., or chunk) of information. Thus, it is likely that literate individuals, as opposed to Longcamp et al.’s (2005) pre-literate children, encode real words as opposed to a series of letters from the string. So, when the modality for retrieval is not either handwriting or typing (e.g., verbal), the encoder should be able to rely on the meaning of the word to recall it. Still, Longcamp et al.’s research provided evidence to support the notion that the peripheral kinaesthetic signals that accompany handwriting and typing movements differ. This finding raises the question of how these modality differences evolve developmentally.

Investigating the development of each modality can elucidate the underlying cognitive and motor processes to help explain the mechanistic differences between the two. For instance,

Kiefer et al. (2015) studied the effects of handwriting and typing training on preschool children's

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 52 reading and writing performance. They found that handwriting training led to better outcomes, suggesting a strong link between motor action and perception in the context of literacy training (see also Bouriga & Olive, 2021). Studies by Grabowski (2010) and Bourdin and Fayol (1994) found that writing is more cognitively demanding than speaking, and that written language production requires more working memory than oral language production. These findings contribute to the field’s understanding of the cognitive processes involved in handwriting and typing as well as their effects on learning and memory.

Mangen et al. (2015) also argued that there are physiological, cognitive, and ergonomic differences that make handwriting and typing distinctly different processes. Specifically, handwriting is a more kinaesthetic process that is governed by the spatial consistency involved in the motor action of shaping letters. This theoretical framework was the basis for their hypothesis that free recall and recognition of words would be superior when written by hand compared to both a screen and a mechanical keyboard. To this end, 36 participants completed a within subjects encoding task, where they were asked to use a pen and paper, a mechanical keyboard, or a screen keyboard to record words played through headphones. After the encoding task, half of the participants were asked to take as much time as they needed to orally produce the words they remembered. The other half were presented words orally and asked to indicate whether the word was present in the encoding task. The results demonstrated no effect in the recognition group.

However, free recall was better for words that had been handwritten than those that were typed (regardless of keyboard type).

Mangen et al. (2015) claimed that handwriting was associated with better free recall compared to typing because of the cognitive and sensorimotor differences between the modalities. They also argued that keyboarding can be considered less generative than

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 53 handwriting because keystrokes do not need to be created by a typist; that is, the keys remain in the same position and the word dictates where the fingers must go to produce it on a page.

Comparatively, when handwriting, one must generate each letter from scratch to form the words.

Ultimately, Mangen et al. provided empirical evidence to suggest that humans’ sensory and perceptual experience of logging information differs depending on whether the information is handwritten or typed and that this is partly explained by the kinaesthetic differences between modalities.

However, the literature suggests that it is quite difficult to produce a reliable modality effect, as seen with the number of mixed results found throughout the literature. This is especially true when they have been examined under more ecologically valid conditions (e.g.,

Bui et al., 2013; Morehead et al., 2019; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014). While researchers agree that there are mechanistic differences between handwriting and typing that could lead to performative differences when used for encoding (e.g., Aragón-Mendizábal et al., 2016; Bouriga & Olive, 2021; Longcamp et al., 2005; Mangen & Balsvik, 2016; Mangen et al., 2015; Smoker et al., 2009), it is not clear which modality leads to superior memory for the information encoded.

A salient example of these mixed results can be found in the literature that pertains to modality differences in university notetaking. It is unequivocally true that the act of taking notes during a lecture enhances university students’ academic performance (Dunkel & Davy, 1989).

Until recently, though, most of the notetaking literature pertained to the act of handwriting.

Because it is now very common for students to bring their laptops into the classroom (Morehead et al., 2019), many of them choose to take lecture notes by typing them. This provides yet another implication for understanding how the mechanistic differences that exist between these two modalities affects memory. Students’ in-class notetaking serves two main purposes:

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 54 encoding the information and storing it for later review (see Kiewra ,1989 for a thorough review). If the mechanisms involved in one of the notetaking modalities enhances memory for the information, students would benefit from adopting that modality when taking notes in class.

Unfortunately, answering this question empirically has been proven difficult because many variables come into play (e.g., review period, instructor differences, handwriting and typing proficiency, working memory capacity, academic support, organization, study habits, class attendance). Still, many researchers have investigated how lecture notetaking modality affects students’ academic success (e.g., Blankenship, 2016; Bui et al., 2013; Fried, 2008; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011;

Kodaira, 2017; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Manzi, et al., 2017; Morehead et al., 2019; Muller &

Oppenheimer, 2014; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Urry et al., 2021; Yamamoto, 2007).

Alas, this line of research has produced inconsistent results. For instance, Bui et al.

(2013) found that students who took notes with the assistance of a keyboard outperformed their handwriting counterparts on a comprehension test. Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) obtained results in the opposite direction. They found that participants retained more conceptual information when they took notes by hand compared to typing them, as would be predicted by the research suggesting that handwriting would lead to superior memory for information encoded compared to typing (e.g., Longcamp et al., 2005; Mangen et al., 2015). Nonetheless, Morehead et al. (2019) and Urry et al.’s (2021) exact replications of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s experiment failed to generate a difference between the handwriting and typing conditions.

When investigating how students’ typed and handwritten notes differed, Fiorella and Mayer (2017) found that typists tended to use more verbal strategies. They also found that some students opted to use maps and drawings when taking notes by hand and that this ability had an CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 55 impact on their learning. Interestingly, Alesandrini’s (1981) results showed that concepts were better recalled when drawn compared to paraphrased. More recently, Wammes et al. (2016) investigated the difference between handwritten transcription of words and the drawing of what they represent. Because the processes associated with visual and tactile perception are reciprocally connected (Mangen et al., 2015), it makes sense that the extended integration of visual attention and sensorimotor action involved in drawing would result in memory improvements over handwriting. In fact, Wammes et al. sought to demonstrate this empirically.

As such, participants drew and handwrote words presented on a screen during an incidental learning task. After a three-minute distractor task, they were asked to orally recall as many words as they could in one minute in whatever order they chose. Wammes et al. found that the words that were encoded by drawing were better remembered than those that were transcribed with a pen and paper. Importantly, these results established that the integration of the multiple memory trace components involved in drawing (i.e., visual, semantic, and motor) improves memory.

To support this interpretation of the drawing effect, Wammes et al. (2016) empirically and systematically ruled out alternative accounts. For instance, a levels of processing account for the results could have been argued (Craik & Lockhart, 1972). Simply put, information that is encoded more deeply (e.g., creating cue cards) is better remembered than information encoded in a shallow way (e.g., re-watching lecture videos). To rule this potential explanation out, Wammes et al. added a feature-listing condition to their basic paradigm. This condition required participants to list the features of words. For instance, when given the word dog, participants might have listed features such as four legs, barks, fur. If a levels-of-processing account could explain the drawing effect, then listing should have been shown to be just as beneficial as drawing in terms of memory improvements over handwriting. The experiment did not generate

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 56 this result, however. Drawing improved memory more than listing or handwriting. Fernandes et al. (2018) replicated this finding, whereby drawing was shown to promote memory improvements compared to listing. Ultimately, Wammes et al. similarly excluded a series of other plausible alternate explanations for the drawing effect (e.g., encoding time, visual imagery, picture superiority).

If the memory enhancement for drawn concepts can be attributed to the integration of multiple well-known encoding techniques (i.e., elaborative, motoric, and pictorial), then recall for drawn words should be superior to any one technique alone. This was exactly what Wammes et al. (2019) sought to demonstrate. To this end, they replicated Wammes et al.’s (2016) methodology and demonstrated that drawing was superior to four novel conditions: drawing without visual feedback, tracing, imagining, and viewing. This provided strong evidence to support Wammes et al.’s (2016) argument that drawing recruits multiple memory-enhancing encoding techniques, which results in superior performance to any single encoding technique.

Ultimately, the literature has provided ample empirical support for the notion that drawing concepts promotes improved memory for information when compared to handwriting because of the integration of multiple memory trace components (e.g., Fernandes et al., 2018; Wammes et al., 2016; Wammes et al., 2019).

When comparing handwriting to typing, though, the act of handwriting promotes the integration of more memory trace components. So, taking Wammes et al.’s (2016) memory trace component explanation of the drawing effect combined with Mangen et al.’s (2015) sensorimotor and visual attention explanation of the handwriting effect, one might conclude that the act of handwriting, albeit inferior to drawing, is superior to typing when attempting to encode to-be-

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 57 remembered information. In other words, even though handwriting does not involve as many encoding benefits as drawing, it might involve more than typing.

The goal of this present research was to build upon Mangen et al.’s (2015) research to examine whether the mechanistic differences between handwriting and typing impact memory for information encoded. As such, Wammes et al.’s (2016) paradigm was replicated with the addition of a typing condition. Moreover, the present research used some of Wammes et al.’s words in addition to others frequently found in academic texts (also see Roberts & Wammes, 2021). Across three experiments, 191 participants completed an encoding task, whereby they were asked to draw what the words represented or to transcribe them by either typing or writing.

Experiments 1 and 3 employed a within-subjects design, whereas participants in Experiment 2 used a between-subject design. After a 3-minute distractor task, participants were surprised with a free recall task, where they were asked to recall as many words from the encoding task as they could in one minute. If Wammes et al.’s results are replicated, then words that are drawn should be the most likely to be recalled by participants. Moreover, if support for Mangen et al.’s handwriting superiority effect is found, then handwritten words should be recalled more than those that are typed.

Experiment 1

## Method

Participants

Participants were 64 undergraduate students recruited from Carleton University’s SONA system. They earned 1% bonus credit in their psychology courses as compensation for participation.

Materials

Target items. The 80-item word list is shown in Table 1. Forty words were selected from the Academic Word List, provided by the EAP Foundation (publicly available online) to be representative of words most used in academic texts. The other 40 words comprised a selection of the words used by Wammes et al. (2016). The words ranged in length from 3 to 12 (M = 6.66); in frequency from 379 to 326 758 (M = 28 233.10); and in number of syllables from 1 to 4 (M = 2.11). The English Lexicon Project’s website was used to gain the lexical information on the words within the list (Balota et al., 2007).

Filler task. Following Wammes et al.’s (2016) methodology, the filler task comprised a continuous reaction time task (CRT). Sound files were constructed to represent low-, medium-, and high-pitched tones using Audacity 3.2 software (Crowder, 2015). Each tone was presented to participants for 500ms at frequencies of 350, 500, and 650 Hz for low-, medium-, and high pitched tones, respectively.

Table 1

This 80-item word list was used in the encoding task of all three experiments and is presented in alphabetical order.

analyze duck indicative shoe assess ear individual significant assumption elephant interpret similar available environment involved skirt benefit establish issue specific concept estimate jacket spider consist evidence kettle spoon context factors method stool couch flute occur stove cow fork percentage strawberry create formula proceed sweater data frog process theory define function require toaster derive giraffe research trumpet desk glove response turtle distribution grapes ruler variable divided guitar sailboat violin doll hammer scissors wagon door harp screwdriver whistle drum identity sheep wrench Note. Words in this list are sorted alphabetically.

Procedure

The recruitment announcement indicated that participants would be required to use Zoom along with their own writing utensil and notepad. Participants completed the experiment online and in a location of their choosing over a recorded Zoom call with one of two different experimenters. Participants were instructed to keep their webcams on for the duration of the experiment.

After providing informed consent, participants completed the encoding task. PowerPoint slides were created to display all instructions and stimuli. The instructions prompted participants

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 60 to either handwrite, type, or draw the words shown on the screen. They were informed that the prompt “handwrite” meant to continuously write down the word in their notebook. Similarly, the prompt “type” meant to continuously type the word into the Zoom chat window. Additionally, they were informed that the prompt “draw” meant to take the allotted time to draw out what the word represented in their notebook. An Excel spreadsheet was used to randomize the display order of prompts and record the randomized order of target words. Participants were not informed of the upcoming memory task.

Participants completed two practice trials for each prompt to familiarize themselves with each of them. From the list of 80 words, 30 of them were randomly selected per participant such that 10 were randomly selected to belong to each of the three prompts. This was done so that each participant received a unique set of target words. For each participant, each of the 30 words was shown on the screen, one at a time. A cue card creating site was used to randomize and display target words and a mask “&”. Participants were shown the target word for approximately 1s. Then, the mask replaced the word on the screen and participants had 40s to follow the prompt for that word. Next, a tone sounded to indicate that a new word would be displayed on the screen in 3s. Immediately following the encoding task (i.e., 30 randomized trials), participants were instructed to take a self-paced break before continuing to the next task. They were also instructed to put their notebooks out of sight and reach.

Subsequently, participants completed the CRT as a filler task. The experimenter’s computer played a shuffled playlist of tones that included 1500ms of silence between tones. This was played through the participants’ speakers via Zoom screenshare (audio only). Hence, there was no way to control for audio quality and volume. Participants orally classified each of the tones, in turn, as either “low”, “medium”, or “high”. Each tone played for 500ms and participants

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 61 had 1500ms to respond. Participants continued to classify tones for three minutes. Following the filler task, they were then be asked to freely recall as many words as they could in 60s in whatever order they choose. For this free recall task, participants spoke the words into their microphone and the Zoom recording was used to record their responses. In total, the experiment lasted approximately 35 minutes.

All methods were approved by the Carleton University Research Ethics Board-B (CUREB-B) at Carleton University, which is constituted and operates in compliance with the Tri-Council Policy Statement: Ethical Conduct for Research Involving Humans (TCPS2).

## Results

Participants' CRT accuracy was calculated (M = 0.64, SD = 0.26). However, the data for 27 participants were missing because they chose to take a self-paced break that lasted the given maximum of three minutes, which left them no time to attempt the CRT. Consequently, this metric was not used to determine participant compliance.

To ensure that results could not be attributed to experimenter differences, an independent samples t-test was conducted on words recalled. The results found no significant differences between participants run by Experimenter 1 (n = 33, M = 6.94, SD = 0.52) and Experimenter 2 (n = 31, M = 6.42, SD = 0.45), t(62) = 0.75, p = .46. Thus, participants’ ability to recall words cannot be explained by the difference in experimenter.

The proportion of words recalled (M = .22, SD = .09) was calculated for each of the three prompts by dividing the number of words participants recalled for that prompt by the number of words they were shown for that same prompt. To investigate whether modality influenced the proportion of words recalled, a 1 x 3 within-subjects ANOVA was conducted on proportion of words recalled. The independent variable was Prompt, which comprised three levels: Draw (M

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 62 = .34, SD = .18), Write (M = .16, SD = .14), and Type (M = .17, SD = .13). The results are presented in Figure 1 and they demonstrated a significant omnibus test, F(2, 126) = 32.27, p < .001, 2 = .34. It was expected that this study would replicate the effects found by Wammes et al. (2016), whereby drawn words would be better recalled than handwritten ones. The results did replicate Wammes et al.’s drawing effect, t(63) = 6.71, p < .001, d = 0.84.

Figure 1. The proportion of words freely recalled is depicted and differentiates between whether they were drawn, written, or typed during the encoding task. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Expanding upon Wammes et al.’s results, this experiment demonstrated that drawn words were also better recalled than typed ones, t(63) = 6.76, p < .001, d = 0.84. Finally, it was also hypothesized that handwritten words would be better recalled than typed ones. Unfortunately, this hypothesis was not supported, t(63) = 0.39, p = .35, d = 0.05. To follow up on this null effect, a Bayesian one-way Repeated Measures ANOVA was conducted, which found strong evidence to support the null, BF = 0.10.

## Discussion

The results from this present study have provided an independent replication of Wammes et al.’s (2016) very robust drawing effect. Free recall for drawn words was very clearly superior to that of written or typed words. It failed, however, to find a memory benefit of handwriting over typing. Nonetheless, it should be noted this inability to replicate Mangen et al.’s (2015) result might have stemmed from the drawn words distinctiveness as originally argued by

Wammes et al. (See Experiment 7).

A distinctiveness effect is an enhancement of memory that is experienced for stimuli that are unique in some aspect (Restorff, 1933). Hunt (1995) argued that perceptual salience is not required for the effect to emerge. Rather, it is the differential attention elicited by the distinctive stimuli that yields it whether it is due to perceptual salience or contextual incongruity. With regard to the present research, it could be argued that the drawing condition enhanced memory for these words not because of any encoding benefit inherent to the task, but because drawing made them more distinctive relative to the words in the other conditions. In fact, Wammes et al.

(2016) noted the possibility for the drawing effect to be accounted for, at least in part, by the distinctiveness effect. Hence, this possibility will be explored in Experiment 2 using a between subjects design.

Experiment 2

If drawing certain words yielded a distinctiveness effect so powerful that it nearly precluded the retrieval of handwritten or typed words, then it might still be possible to generate a handwriting effect using a between-subject design. Experiment 2 was designed to examine this notion. More specifically, participants were randomly assigned to one of three encoding conditions: Draw, Write, or Type. If the distinctiveness of drawing prevented an effect between

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 64 handwriting and typing conditions to emerge in Experiment 1, then participants who handwrote during Experiment 2’s encoding task should outperform those who typed on the surprise free recall task.

## Method

Participants

Ninety undergraduate students from Carleton University’s SONA system participated in the Draw (n = 30), Write (n = 30), or Type (n = 30) condition. They each earned 1% bonus credit in their psychology courses as compensation for participation. Exclusion criteria ensured that no participants had previously completed Experiment 1.

Materials and Procedure

The materials and procedure used in this experiment replicated those of Experiment 1.

The only difference was that this present experiment utilized a between-subjects design.

Therefore, participants only completed one of the three prompt conditions: Draw, Write, or Type to which 30 randomly-selected words from Table 1 were assigned. Once more, this encoding phase was followed by the CRT used as a filler task and a surprise test of free recall.

## Results

As was done in Experiment 1, participants' CRT accuracy was calculated (M = 0.66, SD = 0.26). This time, the data for five participants were missing due to time constraints during the retention interval. Still, this metric was excluded from determining compliance.

Again, an independent samples t-test was conducted on words recalled ensuring that results could not be attributed to experimenter differences. The results found no significant differences between participants run by Experimenter 1 (n = 45, M = 7.98, SD = 0.53) and Experimenter 2 (n = 45, M = 3.29, SD = 0.49), t(88) = 0.71, p = .48. Thus, participants’ ability to recall words cannot be explained by the difference in experimenter.

The proportion of words recalled (M = .27, SD = .11) was calculated for all participants.

To investigate whether modality influenced the proportion of words recalled, a 1 x 3 Between

Subjects ANOVA was conducted on proportion of words recalled. The independent variable was

Prompt, which comprised three levels: Draw (n = 30, M = .35, SD = .11), Write (n = 30, M = .26,

SD = .11), and Type (n = 30, M = .21, SD = .08). The results are presented in Figure 2 and they yielded a significant omnibus test, F(2, 87) = 14.94, p < .001, 2 = .27. Just like in Experiment 1, drawn words were better recalled than handwritten ones, t(58) = 3.17, p = .001, d = 0.81. This replicated Wammes et al.’s drawing effect. Also, replicating the results found in Experiment 1, drawn words were better recalled than typed ones, t(58) = 5.57, p < .001, d = 1.44. While the results of the within-subjects Experiment 1 were unable to support the hypothesis that handwritten words are more likely to be recalled than typed ones, the results of this between subjects Experiment 2 did. Specifically, participants in the Write condition remembered more words than those in the Type condition, t(58) = 2.15, p = .02, d = 0.55. However, a Bayesian independent sample test—conducted between the Write and Type conditions—found anecdotal evidence to support a null effect, BF = 0.67.

Figure 2. The proportion of words freely recalled is depicted and differentiates between participants who completed the drawing, writing, or typing encoding task. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

## Discussion

The contrast between the results of Experiments 1 and 2 was informative. While

Experiment 1 was unable to generate evidence in support of a handwriting superiority effect, the change from a within-subject to a between-subjects design in Experiment 2 did replicate Mangen et al.’s (2015) results. However, while the Bayesian test provided merely anecdotal evidence in support of a null effect, it was unable to provide evidence of a difference between conditions.

Therefore, one last more stringent experiment was conducted to establish the existence of a handwriting superiority effect. A within-subject design including only writing and typing conditions was conducted.

Experiment 3

A final experiment was conducted to establish the reliability of a handwriting superiority effect for single words. Hence, a replication of Experiment 1 was conducted, but the drawing condition was excluded. This allowed for a within-subject comparison of participants’ memory for handwritten and typed words without the possibility that the drawn words’ distinctiveness would conceal a difference between these conditions. Thus, if there are attentional and sensorimotor encoding benefits inherent to writing words, then participants should be more likely to remember them than typed words.

## Method

Participants

Participants were 37 undergraduate students recruited from Carleton University’s SONA system. They earned 1% bonus credit in their psychology courses as compensation for participation. Exclusion criteria ensured that no participants had previously completed

Experiment 1 or 2.

Materials and Procedure

The materials and procedure used in this experiment replicated those of Experiment 1.

However, participants did not experience a drawing condition. As such, to ensure all participants received the same number of words in total, 15 randomly selected words were written and 15 were typed. Moreover, the research assistants from Experiments 1 and 2 had since graduated and 3 new research assistants were hired to be experimenters for this experiment.

## Results

As was done in Experiment 1 and 2, participants' CRT accuracy was calculated (M = 0.76, SD = 0.09), and eight participants’ data were missing due to the retention interval time constraints. This metric was not used to determine participant compliance.

Once more, potential differences in words recalled between experimenters was examined.

Since there were three experimenters, a 1 x 3 Between-Subjects ANOVA was conducted to determine if they had influenced the results. Fortunately, no significant differences between participants run by Experimenter 1 (n = 12, M = 7.42, SD = 2.91), Experimenter 2 (n = 12, M = 7.25, SD = 2.00), and Experimenter 3 (n = 13, M = 7.92, SD = 3.75), F(2, 34) = 0.17, p = .84.

Thus, participants’ ability to recall words cannot be explained by the difference in experimenter.

The proportion of words recalled (M = .25, SD = .10) was calculated for all participants.

To investigate whether modality influenced the proportion of words recalled, a 1 x 2 Within

Subjects ANOVA was conducted on proportion of words recalled. The independent variable was

Prompt, which comprised two levels: Write (M = .25, SD = .17) and Type (M = .25, SD = .15).

As seen in Figure 3, the conditions generated almost identical recall. Participants were just as likely to remember words they had handwritten compared to those they had typed during encoding, F(1, 36) = 0.02, p = .90, 2 < .001. To examine an absence of effect, a Bayesian one way Repeated Measures ANOVA was conducted with the dependent variables proportion of written words recalled and proportion of typed words recalled. It found strong evidence to support the null effect, BF = 0.12.

Figure 3. The proportion of words freely recalled is depicted and differentiates between whether they were written or typed during the encoding task. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

## Discussion

Experiment 3 demonstrated no effect of handwriting or typing on the memory of incidentally learned information. Moreover, it is unlikely that the null effect is explained by lack of power. The only reasonable conclusion that can be drawn from this experiment is that there is no difference in performance between the act of moving a pen on paper and making keystrokes when transcribing words before a surprise free recall task.

Across the three Experiments, 191 participants completed both the encoding and the free recall task. To investigate potential differences between experiments, a 1 x 3 Between-Subjects
