ANOVA was conducted on Words Recalled (M = 7.58, SD = 3.18). The results were found to be significant, F(2, 188) = 4.58, p = .01. Post hoc comparisons demonstrated that the difference

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 70 existed between Experiment 1 and 2, t(152) = 2.98, p = .003. Participants in Experiment 1 recalled fewer words (M = 6.69, SD = 2.77) compared to those in Experiment 2 (M = 8.23, SD = 3.42). A Bayesian independent sample test found strong evidence for a null effect, however, BF

= 0.12.

The Effect of Word Concreteness

One of the key differences between this present research and that of Wammes et al.

(2016) was the use of abstract or unimageable words. It is possible that the words’ concreteness might have impacted participants’ ability to recall them differentially depending on condition.

Thus, word concreteness was examined to determine whether it impacted word recall. As such,

Brysbaert et al.’s (2014) word concreteness ratings database was used to collect concreteness ratings for the words used in this present research. These ratings matched our expectation based on the source of the words. When a median split was conducted, words with concreteness ratings below the median were those from academic texts, whereas words with ratings above the median were those from Wammes et al.’s materials selected to facilitate drawing. The likelihood of recall (i.e., the number of times a word was recalled as a function of how many times it was shown) was found to be correlated with concreteness ratings, r = .38, p > .001. Moreover, abstract words (M = .20, SD = .09) were less likely to be recalled than concrete ones (M = .28,

SD = .11), t(78) = 3.53, p < .001.

To investigate this issue further, the number of concrete words seen relative to abstract words was calculated for each participant. Numbers greater than 1 indicated that participants received more abstract words and those less than 1 indicated that they received more concrete words. The correlation between this Concreteness Ratio (M = 1.13, SD = 0.36) and proportion of words recalled was not significant, r = .01, p = .77. Moreover, an independent samples t-test

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 71 found that the proportion of words recalled did not differ between participants who saw more concrete words (M = .24, SD = .10) and those who saw more abstract words (M = .26, SD = .11), t(189) = 1.41, p = .16. Therefore, even though the concreteness of a word can influence the likelihood of recall, this did not impact participants’ overall performance on the word recall task.

The goal of this research was to determine whether the visual attention and sensorimotor processes that distinguish writing and typing were sufficient to generate a handwriting superiority effect, which could be explained by embodied cognition. Theories of embodied cognition posit that cognitive processes, such as memory, are closely tied to the physical actions and experiences, such as the kinaesthetic and spatial aspects involved in handwriting (e.g.,

Longcamp et al., 2005; Mangen et al., 2015). It was hypothesized that if the mechanisms involved in handwriting—a more kinaesthetic process governed by the spatial consistency of shaping letters—are more conducive to memory compared to those involved in typing, then participants would recall more words that were handwritten compared to those that they had typed during an encoding task. Following Wammes et al.’s (2016) general methodology, participants in Experiment 1 drew, wrote, and typed words that were presented on a screen, during an encoding task. After a three-minute filler task, they were surprised with a one-minute free recall task. The results demonstrated that participants who typed words performed just as well on the free recall task as those who wrote them by hand. Interestingly, a between-subjects version of the exact same experiment (i.e., Experiment 2) yielded a significant difference between writing and typing groups. It provided evidence to suggest that handwriting might be more conducive to memory. Given the mixed results from the first two experiments, Experiment 3 was conducted to examine whether a distinctiveness effect could explain the inability for Experiment 1 to demonstrate a handwriting effect. More specifically, it was argued that the distinctiveness of drawn words might have reduced the experiment’s potential to reveal a difference between the handwriting and typing conditions. To this end, participants in

Experiment 3 only encoded words by writing or typing them.

The results of Experiment 3 failed to demonstrate an effect of modality on memory. In other words, participants were just as likely to recall words they had handwritten compared to those they had typed during incidental learning. Collectively, this series of experiments failed to replicate the results of Mangen et al. (2015). However, the one that most closely resembled that of Mangen et al. (i.e., Experiment 2) did replicate their findings. In Mangen et al.’s experiment, the presentation of condition was blocked, and thus, participants completed one encoding type at a time. In our within-subjects experiments (i.e., Experiment 1 & 3), we did not use a blocked presentation of the different encoding conditions. However, the results of our Experiment 2, which used a between-subjects design, successfully replicated Mangen et al.’s results. It might be that a handwriting effect is only achievable when participants are not switching encoding strategies within an experiment.

Thus, these considerations beg the question: Why would a between-subjects design result in a handwriting effect, whereas a within-subjects design would not? Since participants were generally able to recall fewer words in Experiment 1 compared to Experiment 2, one possible explanation is that the inclusion of three different encoding prompts generated a cognitive load that was difficult to manage for the participants. At least one other example of this exists within the literature, albeit the procedure and task are not directly comparable. That is, Bouriga and Olive (2021) found that typing takes up more cognitive resources than handwriting, but they did not investigate those involved in drawing. Moreover, drawing is certainly a cognitively

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 73 demanding elaborative process (Jonker et al., 2019), so it might have added to task’s complexity too. This does not explain the lack of effect found in Experiment 3, however, since there was no drawing condition and the words recalled were not significantly different from the other experiments. Future research might help clarify the issue, but in comparison, the drawing effect is certainly not vulnerable to changes in experimental design (see also Wammes et al., 2016).

Still, this research provided more evidence to support the notion that drawn words are better recalled than handwritten ones. Expanding upon this result, the present research also demonstrated that drawn words are better recalled than typed ones. Moreover, it added evidence to support the notion that the drawing effect is not sensitive to word concreteness (Roberts &

Wammes, 2021). Our experiments used a wider variety of words than those included in most work on the drawing effect (e.g., Fernandes et al., 2018; Wammes et al., 2016; Wammes et al., 2019). It included more abstract expressions found in academic texts in addition to words that were easily imageable. The results demonstrated that the concreteness of words was related to the likelihood of a word being recalled. Because participants’ word lists were randomized, it was possible for them to have received a different number of concrete or abstract words. This was found to not have impacted participants’ ability to recall the words, however.

Limitations and Future Directions

While these experiments offer meaningful insights into the effect of different encoding modalities on word recall, this present research is not without its limitations. Notably COVID-19 restrictions necessitated online testing, which reduced our ability to control the participants’ compliance with experimental instructions and prevented us from using the CRT filler task to measure it. Moreover, we did not anticipate individual differences based on participants’ demographics (e.g., age, gender, ethnicity, first language, learning differences) and, as such, this

CHAPTER 4: THE HANDWRITING EFFECT EXPERIMENTS 74 information was not collected. This resulted in our inability to examine whether demographic differences could explain the results and reduced its external validity in this regard. Finally, no stratification of the words was conducted prior to randomization to ensure that participants received an equal number of concrete or abstract words. Thus, it is impossible to say with certainty that word concreteness did not affect the results. Future research should aim to intentionally manipulate word concreteness to examine its impact on the relationship between modality and word recall.

Educational Implications

Across three experiments, we explored participants’ ability to recall lists of unrelated words that they had encoded using drawing, handwriting, and typing. The drawing effect proved to be extremely robust, but unfortunately, the evidence to support a handwriting effect was not particularly convincing. Thus, if there is any benefit to taking handwritten notes over typed notes in the classroom, then we would argue that it probably does not stem from the cognitive and sensorimotor differences between these modalities (see Mangen et al., 2015).

Nonetheless, our experiments do not allow us to exclude other reasons why handwriting notes might enhance students’ encoding more than typing. When students take notes under more ecologically valid conditions, they are recording lectures; that is, rich narratives designed to impart expertise in a given academic domain. Thus, in that context, other factors might lead handwritten notes to yield better encoding than typed ones. For instance, Muller and Oppenheimer (2014) argued that handwriting forces students to use “deeper processing” when taking notes more than typing because the speed at which lectures are given require them to paraphrase the information if their handwriting is to keep up. Typing unfolds more quickly and allows for notes to be taken verbatim, which was argued to yield “shallow processing.”

Unfortunately, while Muller and Oppenheimer’s results initially supported their claim, more recent research has failed to replicate them (Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021). In fact, it can be argued that this line of research has led educators to give more attention to the role of external storage (Kiewra, 1989). If students take notes to build studying materials in preparation for examinations, then the more complete notes afforded by the fastest notetaking method (i.e., typing) might be preferred. Yet, this possible advantage must be weighed in relation to the finding that laptops in the classroom tend to generate many distractions (e.g., access to social media and disruptive notifications) that compete with the lecture for students’ attention (Fried, 2008; Gaudreau et al., 2014). Thus, the issue is complex, and students ought to consider the advantages and disadvantages to both notetaking modalities. Nevertheless, if handwriting can be shown to generate superior encoding under certain conditions, the explanation will be at some higher level of cognitive processing like Muller and Oppenheimer’s proposed depth of processing proposal.

Finally, this research and the growing literature supporting the drawing effect (Fernandes et al., 2018; Jonker et al., 2019; Roberts & Wammes, 2021; Wammes et al, 2016; Wammes et al., 2019) do lead to several suggestions. In the context of lecture notetaking, it might encourage students and educators to explore if a modern version of shorthand-like notetaking: “visual notetaking”, whereby symbols are used to replace words and phrases, could increase students’ academic success (Tutt, 2021). The multiple memory traces involved in visual notetaking could enhance memory and ultimately lead to better learning. Minimally, when the content allows, drawing certain concepts presented in class—colloquially referred to as “Sketchnoting”—might yield better encoding than writing them. Importantly, like concept mapping, this practice would not thwart the external storage benefit of academic notetaking.

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING

Quantity Over Modality:

Re-evaluating the Benefits of Typed and Transcribed Notes on Academic Success

Richardson, L. D. & Lacroix, G.

Carleton University

Author Note

Lindsay D. Richardson, Department of Psychology, Carleton University; Dr. Guy

Lacroix, Department of Psychology, Carleton University.

In a series of experiments, Muller and Oppenheimer (2014) showed that students who handwrite notes perform better on tests. They argued that this effect stemmed from enhanced encoding, which naturally emerges when students handwrite notes in their own words rather than type them verbatim. However, subsequent research has yielded mixed results with some supporting laptop use for notetaking. This experiment re-examined the effect of notetaking modality (handwriting vs. typing) and method (transcription vs. paraphrasing) on memory. Participants took notes during a realistic 20-minute lecture and completed a comprehension test after 24 hours. While working memory and note quantity predicted performance, modality and method did not. Both handwritten and typed notes generated similar amounts of transcription and paraphrasing whether participants were asked to transcribe the lecture or put it into their own words. These results strongly suggest that, while no notetaking method is superior, students should focus on taking complete notes for effective test preparation, which might be more easily achieved with the assistance of a computer.

Keywords: education, learning, memory, notetaking, note quantity

Quantity Over Modality:

Re-evaluating the Benefits of Typed and Transcribed Notes on Academic Success

There has been an ongoing debate about notetaking in the classroom (May, 2014;

Richardson & Lacroix, 2023). Should students handwrite or type their notes if they wish to improve their learning outcomes? To help them make an informed decision about which modality to use, researchers have been forced to revisit the two main hypotheses that explain why in-class notetaking leads to superior academic performance: encoding and external storage.

Encoding posits that notetaking deepens students’ processing of the material presented in class and therefore enhances their ability to remember it (Di Vesta & Gray, 1972). Conversely, the external-storage hypothesis postulates that the performance gain elicited by in-class notetaking can be explained by the fact that notetaking is a means of creating something from which to study. Prior to the proliferation of laptops in the classroom, Kiewra (1989) argued that the discussion regarding the relative efficacy of these two notetaking benefits was unproductive, and that both benefits were likely to exert an impact on students’ performance. Ultimately, students should take notes because it helps them encode the information whilst notetaking and because they leave the classroom with materials for later review.

However, Kiewra’s (1989) argument predated the influx of technology in the classroom and was therefore relevant to handwriting only. Now that students have the choice between two different modalities, the question regarding the relative retention benefits of encoding and external storage has resurfaced. The prevalence of students coming to class with laptops has surged (Morehead et al., 2019), and because students can typically type faster than they can handwrite, laptops might offer a means for students to benefit from external storage. However, computers are undoubtedly distracting (Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrooke & Gay, 2003;

Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013;

Yamamoto, 2007), and they can divert students’ attention away from classroom-related activities (Fried, 2008). Moreover, the less-distracting pen and paper might promote a deeper level of encoding (Longcamp et al., 2005; Mangen et al., 2015). This was the core tenet behind Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) seminal research that demonstrated a handwriting superiority effect, whereby those who handwrote their notes outperformed typists. Mueller and Oppenheimer argued that this effect can be explained by the fact that keyboarding promotes transcription (i.e., verbatim-style notetaking), which ultimately reduces one’s ability to benefit from encoding during notetaking. Unfortunately, several researchers have failed to replicate these findings (e.g.,

Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021), and others have even demonstrated a laptop superiority effect (Bui et al., 2013). The goal of this present research was to examine this gap in the literature to discern whether students ought to handwrite or type their notes in the absence of distractions. The research that has attempted to answer this question will be reviewed in depth and the methodology that attempts to address its limitations will be explained.

Encoding Benefit of Handwritten Notetaking

Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) aimed to investigate whether laptop notetaking resulted in a performance degradation regardless of computer-related distractions. To this end, they conducted three studies in which a total of 327 participants were either provided with a pen and paper or a computer that was not connected to the internet and instructed to take notes while watching an informational video. In Study 1, participants watched one of five TED Talks alone or in pairs, taking notes as they would in their regular courses. After a half-hour delay, their comprehension was assessed through factual and conceptual questions. Handwriters

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 87 outperformed typists on conceptual questions, but no difference was found for factual ones.

Additionally, typists generated more notes, albeit with more text matching the lecture transcript compared to handwritten notes. While note quantity (as defined by Word Count) enhanced performance, textual overlap negatively impacted it. Since the negative impact of laptop notetaking was attributed to verbatim transcription, Mueller and Oppenheimer tried to mitigate this behaviour by instructing participants to avoid transcribing the videos in Study 2. The participants once again watched a TED talk and were assigned to one of three conditions. In two conditions, participants were simply asked to handwrite or type their notes as they would regularly in class. A third group instructed to type their notes were specifically told the following:

“We’re doing a study about how information is conveyed in the classroom. We’d like you to take notes on a lecture, just like you would in class. People who take class notes on laptops when they expect to be tested on the material later tend to transcribe what they’re hearing without thinking about it much. Please try not to do this as you take notes today. Take notes in your own words and don’t just write down word-for-word what the speaker is saying” (Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014, pp. 1162).

The results were consistent with those from Study 1. However, the intervention did not change typists' notetaking habits; that is, their notes still overlapped with the video transcript, despite instructions to avoid transcription. This could be due to the inhibitory nature of the instruction rather than promoting a new behavior via expectation-based instruction (Croce &

Salter, 2022). Moreover, the somewhat contradictory or verbose instructions might have confused participants. Statements like "take notes on a lecture, just like you would in class" may contradict "don’t just write down word-for-word what the speaker is saying." A concise

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 88 emphasis on expectation and avoiding redundancies like "try not to do this as you take notes today," might improve notetaking method fidelity.

In Study 3, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) explored whether an opportunity to study could offset the performance detriment incurred by taking notes with a laptop. Participants watched a graduate student read one of four prose passages from a teleprompter. In addition to the change in stimuli, the researchers introduced a one-week delay (instead of 30 mins) between encoding and retrieval. After the delay, half of the participants were given 10 mins to study from their notes. Several issues arose that make it difficult to compare this experiment with the previous two: the review condition did not moderate the effect of handwriting and typing on retention; the change in stimuli made it difficult to interpret any differences in effects; and the delay likely resulted in a floor effect. Interestingly, handwriting yielded benefits for factual but not conceptual performance. Across all experiments, the most consistent finding was that typists took more notes and having taken more notes resulted in better performance.

Several non-mutually exclusive explanations may be evoked in an attempt to understand

Muller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) results. For instance, the visuospatial or motor processing mechanisms involved in handwriting and typing differ (Aragón-Mendizábal et al., 2016; Bouriga & Olive, 2021; Longcamp et al., 2005; Mangen & Balsvik, 2016; Smoker et al., 2009) and it has been suggested that those associated with handwriting may promote better memory for words (Mangen et al., 2015). Richardson and Lacroix (in press) examined this hypothesis. In three experiments, nearly 200 participants encoded words that appeared on a screen by typing, writing, or drawing them. After a three-minute distractor task, participants were surprised with one minute for free recall. The most salient result was that words were recalled much more easily when they were drawn, replicating the robust drawing effect (Wammes et al., 2016). These

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 89 results once more established that the distinct cognitive processes or sensory modalities used during encoding may enhance memory (see Wammes et al., 2019). Nonetheless, the evidence to support a recall advantage for written words over typed ones was very limited. In sum, if there is an encoding benefit to handwriting, Richardson and Lacroix argued that it was probably not explained by its associated visuospatial or motor processing.

Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) contended that handwriting could improve conceptual retention because it encourages deeper information processing through reorganization, aligning with Craik and Lockhart's (1972) Levels of Processing theory. This theory suggests that deeper, more meaningful information processing aids memory retention compared to superficial processing. Students paraphrasing notes engage more deeply with the material than simply transcribing it, requiring a semantic understanding and rephrasing in their own words, while transcription can occur with minimal cognitive engagement. Consequently, Mueller and Oppenheimer attributed a handwriting superiority effect to its promotion of deep processing through spontaneous rephrasing, while typing encourages shallower, verbatim transcription due to its speed. It is important to note, however, that these results do not provide evidence to preclude the possibility that typists could paraphrase notes or that handwriters could be attempting to transcribe them.

Failures to Replicate the Handwriting Superiority Effect

Considering the timeliness of their research and the applicability of their results, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) had a substantial impact in the field. Very quickly, the idea that handwriting notes was more conducive to academic success compared to typing started appearing in various applied articles and guides (e.g., Holstead et al., 2015; Putnam et al., 2016).

According to PsycINFO and Google Scholar, the article had been cited nearly 2000 times by

2023. The attention that Mueller and Oppenheimer generated for this important area of inquiry is undeniable.

Unfortunately, the research that has attempted to replicate and extend Muller and Oppenheimer (2014) has been mostly unsuccessful or inconclusive (e.g., Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021). For example,

Morehead et al. (2019) replicated and extended Mueller and Oppenheimer’s research, including a delayed test and two new conditions: eWriters and No Notes. In Experiment 1, 193 participants watched one of five TED Talks and took notes via keyboard, pen and paper, or eWriter. Half of them completed an immediate test (replicating Mueller and Oppenheimer), whereas all of them completed a delayed test two days later. The stimulus videos and the comprehension tests were the same as those that were used by Mueller and Oppenheimer in the original experiment.

Morehead et al. found that performance on an immediate test was better for the handwriting group only for factual questions (replicating Mueller and Oppenheimer’s Study 3). However, these differences disappeared when examining the results of the delayed test. Furthermore, no differences were found between groups for conceptual performance. While they did not replicate

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) key findings, they did successfully replicate the most robust result within this line of inquiry: typed notes contained more words than handwritten ones. They also found that typed notes had more textual overlap with the TED Talks. Interestingly, note quantity increased conceptual performance consistent with Mueller and Oppenheimer, yet textual overlap was unrelated to performance. Morehead et al. acknowledged that they had not assessed whether notetaking itself produced an encoding benefit. They also suggested that the different notetaking modalities could lead to different storage benefits. These two observations prompted them to conduct subsequent experiments.

Thus, Morehead et al. (2019) conducted a second experiment in which 222 participants watched one of two TED Talks. The participants took notes with a pen and paper, laptop, or eWriter, or did not take notes. Then, half of them completed the comprehension test immediately after encoding, and all of them (save for those who did not take notes) returned two days later for the delayed test. All participants were given the opportunity to study for seven minutes immediately before the delayed test. Morehead et al. found no difference between handwriting and typing conditions. In fact, for conceptual performance, there was a trend toward a laptop superiority effect. Moreover, while they replicated the robust note quantity result, the amount of textual overlap between notes and the video transcript did not differ between handwriting and typing groups. Minimally, these results demonstrate the precarity of the handwriting superiority effect.

In another replication attempt, Urry et al. (2021) conducted a pre-registered exact replication of Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014). They hypothesized that handwriting would result in increased conceptual performance over typing. They also predicted that typing would lead to a higher word count and verbatim overlap with the TED Talk than handwriting. To test these hypotheses, 142 participants took either handwritten or typed notes during one of five TED

Talks. Then, after a brief distractor task, they completed a test. Urry et al. replicated the note quantity effect, whereby typed notes contained more words than handwritten ones. Additionally, more verbatim overlap was found between the typed notes and the TED Talks compared to the handwritten ones. However, they did not find an effect of notetaking modality on performance.

The researchers acknowledged, however, some deviations from Mueller and Oppenheimer’s methods. Most importantly, participants completed the experiment in various locations and at different times. Therefore, some participants may have been prey to more distractions than

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 92 others, leaving open the possibility that an encoding advantage for handwriting may still emerge under optimal conditions. Still, if this were true, it would highlight the difficulty of obtaining a handwriting effect in a traditional classroom environment. On the other hand, it may be argued that the ubiquity of online and asynchronous courses since the COVID-19 pandemic confer Urry et al.’s methodology some ecological validity.

Taken together, the failed replications (e.g., Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell &

Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021) suggest that the handwriting superiority effect may not be a not a robust or replicable effect. In theory, it is plausible that handwritten encoding leads to better retention of information compared to typing (Longcamp et al., 2005;

Mangen et al., 2015). However, the mechanism responsible for handwriting’s postulated deeper level of encoding has not yet been clearly identified (e.g., Richardson & Lacroix, in press).

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) notion was that it could be explained by reformulation or paraphrasing of the material presented. However, this argument hinges on the notion that students who handwrite their notes are engaging in some sort of reformulation of the material, such as paraphrasing.

There has yet to be evidence to exclude the possibility that students might be attempting to transcribe the lecture with a pen and paper. Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) thought that their instructions might be to blame for participants’ lack of compliance with notetaking method instruction. While this might be the case, participants’ non-compliance might also be attributed to their inability to adequately paraphrase an ongoing lecture. Research has indicated that students often face difficulties in paraphrasing information, even when they are not under stringent time constraints (Choy & Lee, 2012; Kirkland & Saunders, 1991; Thadphoothon, 2019). Even though paraphrasing can help increase students’ understanding of the material, it

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 93 requires a significant amount of mental effort (Morrison et al., 2015). Augmenting cognitive load with the secondary task of attending to a continuous lecture would only exacerbate this challenge. In fact, using a dual-task paradigm, Rachele (1985) demonstrated that an increased cognitive load via the application of secondary tasks can affect the interpretation of the information’s semantic meaning. When the primary task is paraphrasing information, the complexity of a secondary task, such as attending to and comprehending a demanding academic lecture, can impair students' effectiveness in paraphrasing its contents. Perhaps benefitting from encoding by reformulating the material is something that ought to be reserved for note review periods rather than while attempting to record it during class.

External Storage Benefit of Laptop Notetaking

Notwithstanding their original goals, the most replicable result to emerge from Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) research has been that typing leads to increased note quantity. For instance, Mitchell and Zheng (2019) sought to investigate the difference between handwriting and typing in-class notes on memory and learning in a more ecologically-valid setting: the classroom. As such, 58 participants watched a TED Talk in two groups depending on notetaking modality. Since they participated in a classroom, participants were not required to take notes on the same device they were using to watch the lecture. After 30 minutes of unrelated classroom activities, the participants’ retention of the material was tested using both factual and conceptual questions. Once more, the results did not reveal a handwriting superiority effect. Interestingly, however, participants who took more notes performed better on both conceptual and factual questions of the test. Moreover, verbatim overlap was not found to be a significant predictor of performance. These results might provide indirect support for a laptop superiority effect. To be clear, Mitchell and Zheng did not demonstrate that typing notes led to better performance.

Nonetheless, they did find that typing can increase note quantity, and note quantity can positively predict performance. Thus, it might be the case that encouraging students to record as much information as possible could potentially result in superior performance.

In fact, this was the fundamental premise of Bui et al.’s (2013) argument. Given the speed advantage of typing over handwriting, typists usually take more notes (as confirmed by

Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014; Urry et al., 2021). More notes could logically lead to better review of the material, improving students' study effectiveness. However, while the slower pace of handwriting might limit full transcription of the lecture, it does not necessarily promote paraphrasing, which is necessary to incur encoding benefits. While the complexity of academic lectures may vary, effective notetaking requires students to attend to multiple stimuli (e.g., the lecturer speaking, multimedia presentations, and slide text). Concurrently, they must disregard irrelevant and competing stimuli (e.g., classmates conversing, distraction from peers' computer screens displaying unrelated content, or individuals entering and exiting the room). Moreover, students are required to identify critical information, document it, and decide whether any clarifying questions should be raised. These students are expected to accomplish these tasks while continuously absorbing the information being disseminated, a process that understandably consumes a substantial portion of an individual’s

WM capacity (Paoletti et al., 2012). Paraphrasing during notetaking requires even more WM resources than straightforward transcription. In fact, Bui et al. reasoned that the cognitive demands of in-class notetaking could be reduced by typing because it requires fewer resources than writing. They argued that proficient typists would be able to efficiently transcribe the lecture materials thus eliminating the need to reword or reorganize them. This argument hinges on the assumption that students will review the notes they transcribe. If they do, they could still

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 95 benefit from a deeper level of encoding by actively, and more thoroughly, reorganizing the information during review.

Hence, Bui et al. (2013) aimed to investigate the differences between notetaking modality (i.e., handwrite vs. typing) and method (i.e., paraphrase vs. transcribe) on performance and the role that WM plays in this relationship. To this end, throughout an 11-minute simulated lecture, participants wrote or typed their notes. Moreover, they were instructed to either paraphrase or transcribe their notes. Subsequently, they completed Daneman and Carpenter’s (1980) Reading

Span task to measure WM before completing a free recall and short answer test about the simulated lecture. In a typical Reading Span Task, participants are presented with a series of sentences. They are asked to read each sentence out loud and then remember the final word of each sentence in the series. After a certain number of sentences, they are asked to recall all the final words in the series in the correct order. The task is designed to measure the ability to maintain and manipulate information in WM while simultaneously processing incoming information. In this sense, the Reading Span Task is a dual task because it requires both processing (i.e., reading and understanding the sentences) and storage (i.e., remembering the final words).

Bui et al.’s (2013) results demonstrated that note quantity was higher for typed notes compared to handwritten ones. Moreover, participants who typed their notes recalled more idea units than those who handwrote them. Additionally, those who transcribed their notes recorded more idea units and outperformed those who attempted to paraphrase them on both free recall and short answer. These results revealed a laptop superiority effect. Bui et al. recognized that, even though transcription led to increased performance on an immediate test, reorganizing information during notetaking could lead to deeper encoding, with the effects becoming apparent

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 96 only after a delay. To examine this, they introduced a 24-hour delay in a second experiment.

They hypothesized that if reorganization enhances encoding, then that behavior would lead to better performance on a delayed test than transcription. Their results supported this hypothesis.

The performance of participants who transcribed their notes declined but they remained stable for those who paraphrased them. These results suggested that the performance benefit of transcription typing was short-lived. Although, when participants in Experiment 3 were provided an opportunity to study, transcribers outperformed those instructed to paraphrase their notes even on the delayed test.

To investigate the influence of WM on students’ notetaking ability, Bui et al. (2013) pooled the data from their three experiments and ran correlations. The participants' WM predicted note quantity and test performance for those tasked with note reorganization, but not for those asked to transcribe. Essentially, having higher cognitive capacity could offset the disadvantages related to reorganization in terms of note quantity and test performance. These results suggested that laptops can help students with lower WM capacity leverage the external storage benefit of notetaking. Moreover, this does not appear to come at a great cost of encoding.

Given that both the Reading Span Task used by Bui et al. (2013) and their primary notetaking task heavily depend on participants' literacy skills, their WM measure selection may have confounded the results, potentially overestimating the relationship between WM capacity and notetaking. Turner and Engle (1989) emphasized minimizing overlap between span tasks and primary tasks to avoid artificially inflating correlations. A less literacy-dependent span task, such as Operation Span (OSpan), could offer more precise insights into WM's role in notetaking, possibly refining Bui et al.'s claims about the laptop notetaking benefits for individuals with lower WM capacity. Much like the Reading Span task, the OSpan task interleaves storage and CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 97 processing mechanisms (Shipstead, 2014). However, instead of using sentences, the OSpan requires participants to solve mathematical equations while storing sequences of letters. This task would allow researchers to measure WM by assessing participants’ attention control and retrieval abilities without any overlap with the primary task.

Another limitation in Bui et al.’s (2013) research is that the free recall task was conducted via typing for participants in all conditions. Therefore, transfer appropriate processing (Morris et al., 1977) may explain the improved performance observed among typists. According to this interpretation, the congruence between the method of information encoding and its subsequent retrieval could have enhanced participants’ memory for the material when the test was immediate. Ideally, research that examines the impact of notetaking modality on academic performance should either test modality congruence as an independent variable or eliminate its ability to confound the interpretation of the results with a neutral test (e.g., multiple-choice questions).

The principal goal of this present experiment was to re-examine the impact of notetaking strategies (i.e., modality and method) on students’ ability to retain information in an academic lecture. Additionally, we were interested in whether note attributes, such as quantity and verbatim overlap, predict performance. Another goal was to demonstrate whether individual differences in WM and typing proficiency might influence the relationship between notetaking strategy and academic performance. To this end, several components of Bui et al.’s (2013),

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014), and Morehead et al.’s (2019) research have been integrated into this experiment. First, participants’ WM and typing proficiency were measured before watching a 20-minute simulated lecture. TED Talks have been used as stimuli to represent lectures throughout this line of research. These videos differ from academic lectures, even those

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 98 conducted asynchronously online. As Morehead et al. rightly pointed out, academic lectures are typically created with the purpose of helping students understand facts and concepts. Though

TED Talks are informative, they are designed to entertain rather than prepare viewers for future testing. Hence, a 20-minute neuroscience lecture was created for the purpose of this experiment.

During the lecture, participants handwrote or typed their notes. Within each group, they either transcribed or paraphrased the lecture. Immediately thereafter, they were given the opportunity to create cue cards from their notes. After a 24-hour delay, half of the participants were given 20 minutes to review their cue cards. Then, all of them completed a multiple-choice comprehension test with both factual and conceptual versions of questions. The following hypotheses were formulated.

First, if the instructions that focused on what is expected of participants lead to greater notetaking method instruction fidelity than observed by Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014), then participants’ notes from the transcription conditions should have more verbatim overlap with the lecture transcript than notes from the paraphrase conditions. Second, if typing leads to transcription and handwriting leads to paraphrasing, then verbatim overlap will be higher for typed notes compared to handwritten ones. Third, note quantity should be greater for typed notes compared to handwritten ones. Fourth, increased note quantity should lead to better performance on the test overall. Fifth, if notetaking strategy (i.e., modality and method) differentially impacts students’ factual and conceptual retention, then, participants’ performance on the factual and conceptual questions will differ depending on their modality and method condition. No direction was hypothesized due to the mixed results presented within the literature (e.g., Bui et al., 2013;

Morehead et al., 2019; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014). Finally, it was expected that WM and typing proficiency would impact the relationship between notetaking instruction and CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 99 performance such that decreased WM and increased typing proficiency would allow for a laptop superiority effect overall.

## Method

Participants

Participants were 459 undergraduate students, who were recruited to participate in this experiment using Carleton University’s SONA system. Inclusion criteria ensured that all participants were proficient English speakers with normal or corrected-to-normal hearing and vision. They earned partial course credit in their psychology courses as compensation for participation. A 2 (method instruction: transcribe or paraphrase) x 2 (modality: handwrite or type) x 2 (review group: reviewers or non-reviewers) Between-Subjects design was used.

Therefore, participants were randomly assigned to one of eight conditions.

Materials and Procedure

The procedure for the entire experiment is outlined in Figure 1. It was conducted online using Qualtrics software (Qualtrics, Provo, UT). After first providing informed consent, participants were asked the following demographic information: age, gender, ethnicity, program type and level, and GPA. The following tasks and measures were then given to the participants in the following order.

Figure 1

A Schematic of the Methods

Operation Span Task. The participants completed the OSpan (Unsworth et al., 2005) to measure their WM ability. The task required participants to remember sets of letters while verifying the accuracy of simple mathematical equations. It unfolded as follows. On each trial, the participants first saw an equation (e.g., 5 + 1 = 6). After pressing “Next”, they were presented with a potential answer (e.g., 6) and asked to indicate whether the presented answer was correct by choosing “yes” or “no” on the screen. After pressing “Next” again, they were shown a letter (e.g., “R”) for 2000ms. The participants were asked to memorize the letters in order. At the end of each block, which comprised the presentation of two to five equations with letters, participants were prompted with “???” as a cue to recall all the letters they had seen in that block. To input their answers, they needed to type letters into boxes that corresponded to the serial position of the letters (e.g., “First Letter ____”). Participants submitted their responses by clicking “Next”.

At the end of each block, the screen presented feedback indicating the number of mathematical equations they had correctly solved. All participants were given three blocks of two trials before

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 101 proceeding with the experimental blocks. The actual task comprised 16 randomized and self paced blocks with a combined total of 55 trials.

Typing or Handwriting Proficiency. After completing the OSpan, participants completed either a typing proficiency test or a handwriting proficiency one, depending on modality condition. Typing proficiency was measured using the open-source and online typing test TypingTest.com. As practice, participants first completed one minute of the Medium Text test, which is the site’s default setting. Then, they completed the 3-minute Story Typing test and were instructed to choose “Aesop’s Fables” (i.e., the default Story Typing test). Typing proficiency was measured by the number of words that participants were able to type without errors divided by the number of minutes they were given (see Equation 1.0). It should be noted that words were defined as strings of five characters (Pinet et al., 2022). Only participants in the typing conditions completed the typing proficiency test.

(1.0) Typing prof. = [( Typed characters 5 )− Uncorrected errors] Time (min)

To make the participants’ experience as comparable as possible in both modality conditions, participants in the handwriting conditions completed a handwriting proficiency test that approximated the typing proficiency one. They were instructed to take one minute to practice transcribing an audio recording of Aesop’s Fables using a pen (or pencil) and piece of paper. Then, participants were asked to transcribe the remaining three minutes of the audio recording. The transcript of the audio recording was also presented on the screen and was spoken at approximately 150 words per minute. Handwriting proficiency was not actually measured nor was it analyzed.

Lecture. Next, all participants watched and listened to a 20-minute lecture on The Psychology of Addiction delivered by the first author. This topic was chosen for the target

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 102 audience because it should have been novel yet accessible. It explained how the neural activity in the limbic system translates to the subjective sensation of pleasure that perpetuates addiction.

Moreover, it covered different core features of addiction: withdrawal, tolerance, and reward seeking behaviour. This approximately 2900-word lecture was recorded at an average rate of 150 words per minute (wpm). To optimize the lecture’s quality, audio noise (e.g., hum, clicks) was stripped from the recording using iMovie editing software (Apple, 2022). The lecture video and its transcript are available in the supplementary materials. Participants were provided with notetaking instructions and an example before watching the simulated lecture. The instructions used within this present experiment modified those used by Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) in an attempt to increase their clarity. As such, the following four different notetaking instructions were given to participants.

Handwritten transcription notetaking instructions. “We are doing a study about how information is conveyed in the classroom. We’d like you to take notes on the upcoming lecture to prepare for an upcoming test. While taking notes today, please try to write down word-for word what you hear. This is typically made easier by listening to what the teacher says and writing down every word.”

Handwritten paraphrased notetaking instructions. To maintain consistency across conditions, all instructions were based on the transcription handwritten ones. For the handwritten paraphrased condition, we said: “We are doing a study about how information is conveyed in the classroom. We’d like you to take notes on the upcoming lecture to prepare for an upcoming test.

While taking notes today, please try to write down as many ideas as possible while using your own words. This is typically made easier by using symbols, abbreviations, and jottings.”

Typed transcription notetaking instructions. “We are doing a study about how information is conveyed in the classroom. We’d like you to take notes on the upcoming lecture to prepare for an upcoming test. While taking notes today, please try to type word-for-word what you hear. This is typically made easier by listening to what the teacher says and typing every word.”

Typed paraphrased notetaking instructions. “We are doing a study about how information is conveyed in the classroom. We’d like you to take notes on the upcoming lecture to prepare for an upcoming test. While taking notes today, please try to type as many ideas as possible while using your own words. This is typically made easier by using symbols, abbreviations, and jottings.”

Creating Study Materials. Immediately after watching the 20-minute simulated lecture video, all participants were given 20 minutes to create a stack of cue cards from their notes. They were instructed to create virtual cue cards, by typing within text boxes in Qualtrics marked “front” and “back,” to create study material for an upcoming test. Then, they were provided with a PDF of the cue cards they created and asked to save them to their personal computer. This marked the end of the requirements for day one of the experiment. Before leaving, however, participants were asked to ensure they did not study the material and that doing so would hinder the results of the experiment.

Study Session. After a 24-hour delay, all participants were emailed another link to complete testing, at which point they were asked about how they had if interacted they had interacted the material presented the day before. Then, depending on condition, participants were randomly assigned to the review or non-review conditions. Reviewers were given 15 minutes to study from their cue cards and their lecture notes before testing while Non-Reviewers were not.

Testing. Finally, all participants completed the test. The question bank used to create participants’ tests is presented in Appendix A. The bank comprised 40 multiple-choice questions (e.g., “Which of the following is the area where the terminal buttons of the pre-synaptic cell connect with the dendrites of the post-synaptic cell without touching?”; correct response: “The synapse”; distractor responses: “The axon,” “An action potential,” “The neural junction.”) Two versions existed for each question: factual and conceptual. Factual questions were defined as ones that were mentioned verbatim throughout the lecture (e.g., “Which view of the brain would researchers be examining if they were to slice it from forehead to the nape of the neck?”; correct response: “Sagittal View”; distractor responses: “Inferior View,” “Superior View,” “Limbic

View.”) The content of the conceptual questions matched that of the factual ones, but the conceptual ones were paraphrased from the stimulus lecture (e.g., “Which of the following is the correct terminology for the view of the brain that would allow a researcher to look at the inside of the brain from the side?”) Participants were posed an equal number of factual and conceptual questions. In total, they responded to a subset of 30 questions. Thus, each participant received a unique version of the multiple-choice test, and they had 45 minutes to complete it.

In total, the experiment was conducted over two sessions with a 24-hour delay between them. On the first day, the experiment lasted approximately 60 minutes. On the second and final day, the experiment lasted an additional 60 minutes for reviewers and 40 minutes for non reviewers.

## Results

Data Cleaning

This present experiment required participants to continue with testing after a 24-hour delay. As such, attrition was expected. A total of 115 participants did not complete the testing

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 105 phase. Thus, the attrition rate for testing was 25%, which seems to be low for online experiments with long durations (Sauter et al., 2020). Table 1 shows how many participants (of those who completed both sessions) were included in each group, based on condition. Five participants from the typing condition were excluded because they spontaneously decided to take notes by hand. No participant in the handwriting condition submitted typed notes, however. Therefore, the following analyses were conducted on the remaining 339 participants (i.e., 74% of the original sample).

Demographics

Of the remaining 339 participants, 104 of them identified as Male, 224 identified as

Female, 5 identified as Non-Binary, and 6 either identified as another non-conforming gender or preferred not to say. Their reported ethnicity demonstrated diversity in our sample, with most participants reporting to have European origins (n = 122), Asian origins (n = 71), and African origins (n = 38). Thirty-eight participants reported to have other North American origins and the remaining 68 participants reported to have Aboriginal, Latin, South American, and Middle

Eastern origins. Participants’ ages ranged from 16–49 (M = 19.62, SD = 3.92). Only 48 of them reporting knowing their current GPA, which ranged from 2–11.90 (M = 9.49, SD = 2.07). This makes sense considering most participants were first-year students (n = 252), whereas only a few

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 106 were second- (n = 57), third- (n = 16), or fourth-year students (n = 7). Most of them were from

Carleton’s Psychology program (n = 100). However, many of them were from Carleton’s

Criminology and Criminal Justice (n = 46), Computer Science (n = 26), Health Sciences (n =

21), Cognitive Science (n = 16), and Neuroscience (n = 14) programs. Nine of them reported their major to be “undeclared” and the remaining 92 were from Carleton’s other programs like

Engineering, History, Biology, Business, Linguistics, and other sciences.

Working Memory

Participants’ WM ability was examined using an OSpan task and was calculated using an all-or-nothing unit scoring method (Conway et al., 2005). More specifically, the proportion of recalled letters (in their appropriate serial position) was calculated for participants who achieved at least 85% on the processing task (i.e., the mathematical equations). The number of participants who did not meet this criterion was 39. Next, participants’ WM Score (M = .80, SD = .18) was examined for outliers. Seven participants’ WM scores fell below .26 and were therefore identified as extreme outliers (i.e., +/-3 SD away from the M). Hence, their scores were removed from further analyses while the rest of their data was retained.

To ensure that experimental groups did not differ in their WM Score, a 2 (Modality:

Handwrite vs. Type) x 2 (Method: Paraphrase vs. Transcribe) x 2 (Review: Reviewers vs. Non

Reviewers) Between-Subjects ANOVA was conducted on WM Score. The three-way interaction was not found to be significant, F(1, 285) = 0.16, p = .69. The interaction between Modality and Method was not found to be significant, F(1, 285) = 0.52, p = .47. The interaction between

Review and Method was not found to be significant, F(1, 285) = 0.84, p = .36. The interaction between Review and Modality was not found to be significant, F(1, 285) = 1.23, p = .26. None of the main effects reached significance either: Method, F(1, 285) = 0.05, p = .82; Modality, F(1,

285) = 0.30, p = .59; Review, F(1, 285) = 0.05, p = .83. Therefore, any differences between conditions cannot be explained by differences in WM ability.

Notetaking Instruction Fidelity

Depending on modality and method conditions, participants were given one of four different notetaking instructions. To assess method instruction fidelity, the notes from the two

Method conditions (paraphrase and transcribe) were compared to the lecture transcript. The number of participants who successfully uploaded their notes to Qualtrics was 427. Of these participants, 96 had been excluded from the dataset during data cleaning. Verbatim overlap was defined as the proportion of textual overlap between participants’ notes and the lecture transcript (M = .09, SD = .07). One participant nearly reached 50% overlap, which was an extreme outlier and was therefore removed from analyses. Even after the removal of outliers, the distribution was still skewed. Thus, to meet the assumptions of ANOVA, we performed a log transformation on the verbatim overlap variable, resulting in a normal distribution of the data. Subsequently, a 2 (Modality: Handwrite vs. Type) x 2 (Method: Paraphrase vs. Transcribe) Between-Subjects

ANOVA was conducted on the log transformed verbatim overlap variable. The results are presented in Figure 1. The interaction was not found to be significant, F(1, 285) = 1.57, p = .21.

Neither main effects reached significance: Method, F(1, 285) = 1.77, p = .18 nor Modality, F(1,

285) = 0.04, p = .85. This indicated that there was no significant difference between the verbatim overlap of those asked to transcribe their notes and those asked to paraphrase them. From Figure 1, a potential performance boost for typists transcribing rather than paraphrasing seems to have trended toward significance. Still, no such difference is apparent for those using pen and paper.

Figure 1

Verbatim Overlap for the Different Notetaking Modality and Method Conditions

Note. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Muller and Oppenheimer (2014) argued that their instructions might have been ineffective because typing led to transcription regardless of instruction. To test this claim, an Independent Samples t-test was conducted on verbatim overlap differences between the modality groups: Typing Condition (M = .08, SD = .08) and Handwriting Condition (M = .08, SD = .07).

The results did not reach significance, t(313) = 0.04, p = .49. One could argue that participants’ prior notetaking habits might have impacted their results, however. To rule out this possibility, participants’ self-reported preference for both modality and method were compared using a Chi

Square test. The results are displayed in Table 2 and found that modality preference was unrelated to method preference, X 2 (1, 338) = 2.96, p = .09. Moreover, an Independent Samples t test found that modality preference did not differentiate verbatim overlap; that is, participants who prefer to type their notes (M = .09, SD = .07) were no more likely to transcribe their notes than their peers who prefer to handwrite them (M = .08, SD = .08), t(312) = 1.24, p = .22.

Apparently, participants’ likelihood to copy a lecture word for word is not related to whether they take notes by hand or keyboard.

Many studies have consistently shown that typing leads to higher note quantity (e.g.,

Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014; Urry et al., 2021). To confirm these findings, participants' notes were analyzed for word and character count.

Two participants’ notes had over 120,000 characters, whereby all remaining notes had less than 5,000. Thus, these two participants’ notes were eliminated from analyses. Subsequently, 14 values (i.e., > 3557) were identified as outliers and removed from analyses. Because a very strong positive correlation was found between character count (M = 1591.03, SD = 983.40) and word count (M = 227.44, SD = 149.62), r = .99, p < .001, the former was retained to measure note quantity. To examine the effect of notetaking condition on note quantity, a 2 (Modality:

Handwrite vs. Type) x 2 (Method: Paraphrase vs. Transcribe) Between-Subjects ANOVA was conducted on the character count. The interaction was not found to be significant, F(1, 298) = 0.24, p = .63. However, both main effects were found to be significant: Modality, F(1, 298) = 26.68, p < .001 and Method, F(1, 298) = 6.20, p = .01. As seen in Figure 2, typed transcription resulted in a much higher character count than handwritten and paraphrased, which replicated well-documented results throughout the literature.

Figure 2

Note Quantity Differences Depending on Notetaking Conditions

Note. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Performance Outcomes

The proportion of correct responses was used to measure the participants’ performance on the comprehension test. (M = .55, SD = .18). Overall, performance was similar for factual (M = .54, SD = .19) and conceptual questions (M = .56, SD = .20). To explore the relationships among all variables of interest within this present experiment, a correlation matrix was created that included the variables: note quantity, verbatim overlap, WM, typing proficiency, overall performance, factual performance, and conceptual performance. The results are presented in

Table 3. As expected, overall performance was positively correlated with WM. It was also positively correlated with note quantity and negatively correlated with verbatim overlap. Note quantity was also positively correlated with typing proficiency and WM. Interestingly, while

WM was positive correlated with factual performance, the correlation between WM and conceptual performance merely trended toward significance.

Notetaking modality on performance. It was hypothesized that both method and modality would impact participants’ performance on a comprehension test in relation to the review conditions. Moreover, if notetaking modality and method differentially impact students’ factual and conceptual retention, we expected that participants’ performance on the factual and conceptual questions would differ depending on their modality and method condition. As such, a

2 (Modality: Handwrite vs. Type) x 2 (Method: Paraphrase vs. Transcribe) x 2 (Review: Reviewers vs. Non-Reviewers) x 2 (Question Type: Factual vs. Conceptual) Mixed-Design

ANCOVA was conducted on performance with the covariates WM Score and Note Quantity. The between-subjects variables were Modality, Method, and Review, whereas the within-subject variable was Question Type. The results are presented in Figure 3. The main effect for Question

Type trended toward significance, F(1, 292) = 3.39, p = .07, suggesting that the type of question may have influenced performance. Participants generally did better on conceptual questions (M = 55.86%, SD = 19.37) as opposed to factual ones (M = 54.28%, SD = 19.38). The main effect for Method also trended toward significance, F(1, 292) = 3.05, p = .08. Thus, whether students transcribed or paraphrased their notes might have impacted their performance. None of other main effects or any of the interactions did (all p values > .13). The two covariates were found to be significant: WM, F(1, 292) = 8.15, p = .01 and Note Quantity, F(1, 292) = 29.17, p < .001.

## Discussion

The goal of this present research was to re-examine the effect of notetaking strategy, including both modality (i.e., handwrite vs. type) and method (i.e., paraphrase vs. transcribe), on participants’ memory of a 20-minute neuroscience lecture. Depending on the condition,

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 113 participants were asked to either take notes by hand or with a keyboard. Moreover, they were instructed to paraphrase their notes or to transcribe them. WM, typing proficiency, and note quantity were also measured. After a 24-hour delay, half of the participants were provided time to review their notes. Then, all participants were asked to complete a comprehension test with both factual and conceptual versions of questions. Several hypotheses were examined.

First, it was hypothesized that our instructions would lead to greater notetaking method instruction fidelity than observed by Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014). The results did not fully support this hypothesis; that is, the verbatim overlap between participants’ notes and the lecture transcript did not differ depending on whether they were instructed to paraphrase their notes or to copy exactly what the teacher was saying. This does not preclude the possibility, however, that participants were attempting to paraphrase their notes when instructed to do so. The second hypothesis was based on the core explanation of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s handwriting superiority effect. Because students cannot write as fast as they can type, it may follow that typing would lead to transcription and that writing would encourage paraphrasing. If this true, then typed notes should contain more textual overlap with the lecture transcript than handwritten ones. However, the evidence did not support this hypothesis. Participants who wrote their notes were just as likely to attempt to transcribe them as typists. Moreover, participants’ self-reported notetaking preferences indicated that handwriters were just as likely to attempt to transcribe their notes as typists and that some typists tend to paraphrase their notes.

Given that students struggle to paraphrase information when it is the only task that they must attend to (Choy & Lee, 2012; Kirkland & Saunders, 1991; Thadphoothon, 2019), our results suggest that they might generally lack the working memory ability to paraphrase lecture materials while attempting to record them for later review. Perhaps researchers have been unable

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 114 to replicate Mueller and Oppenheimer’s handwriting superiority effect results (e.g., Kutta, 2017;

Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021) because it hinges on the notion that handwriting results in spontaneous paraphrasing to improve performance via an encoding mechanism. We would suggest that this is rarely the case. To examine whether paraphrasing while lecture notetaking can enhance performance, researchers might need to conduct an intervention study, wherein students are intentionally taught how to paraphrase and can demonstrate that they have gained this skill before testing its effect on memory and comprehension.

Still, one robust effect has emerged: typing and transcription lead to higher note quantity.

Moreover, when provided the opportunity for review, note quantity has been found to positively predict performance (Bui et al., 2013). The formulation of our third and fourth hypotheses stemmed from these results (Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019; Mueller &

Oppenheimer, 2014; Urry et al., 2021). Thus, we expected that note quantity would be greater for typed and transcribed notes compared to handwritten or paraphrased ones, and that increased note quantity would lead to better performance on the comprehension test. Our results did find evidence to support these claims. The typists recorded more information (as defined by character count) than those who took notes by hand. Additionally, transcribers took more notes than paraphrasers. While there was no evidence that typists nor transcribers outperformed handwriters or paraphrasers on the test, note quantity was found to be a predictor of performance, whereby more notes led to better performance. This result has provided evidence to support Bui et al.’s (2013) notion that laptops can provide a more accessible means of notetaking for a subset of students.

Since it is true that people can generally type faster than they can handwrite, taking notes with the assistance of a keyboard would allow students to increase the completeness of their notes. As note quantity has been shown to predict performance, it might be worth advising students to prioritize the external-storage benefit to academic notetaking. Moreover, because it is possible for them to paraphrase their notes while typing, the keyboard would allow them to switch notetaking method when necessary. For instance, students might attempt to paraphrase lectures when they believe the conditions are ideal, such as when they possess prior knowledge of the content material and feel confident in their ability to discern which ideas are important or unimportant. However, due to the less cognitively demanding nature of transcription, these students might switch to this method of notetaking when they feel overwhelmed or pressured to capture all the information, planning to make sense of it all later during review. Naturally, empirical research would be necessary to support these claims. Nonetheless, it might be worth investigating whether strategy-switching in the context of notetaking affects students’ performance.

The fifth hypothesis also concerned performance. Researchers have found that notetaking modality and method impact factual and conceptual retention of information differentially (e.g.,

Bui et al., 2013; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014; Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021).

However, the direction of the results has not been consistent throughout the literature. For example, Morehead et al. found that participants who handwrote their notes performed better on factual questions, whereas Mueller and Oppenheimer found that they performed better on conceptual questions. So, it was expected that notetaking modality (handwrite vs. type) and method (paraphrase vs. transcribe) might differentially impact participants’ performance on the factual and conceptual questions of the current experiment. However, the direction of the effect

CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 116 was not predicted. The results hinted that there might be a slight benefit for participants in the handwriting condition who reviewed their notes for factual questions and not conceptual ones.

However, this effect merely trended toward significance and relied on controlling for both note quantity and WM. It is entirely possible that by the time handwriters review their notes, they have experienced two encoding episodes, which might lead to better recall.

The final hypothesis predicted that decreased WM and increased typing proficiency would allow for a laptop superiority effect to emerge. Because no effect of modality was found, there was no evidence to support this claim. Moreover, while typing proficiency was related to note quantity, it was not found to be related to performance on the test. WM, however, was found to positively predict note quantity and overall performance. It might be that, regardless of notetaking modality or method, students’ WM and typing proficiency would lead to increased note quantity, which would in turn predict higher grades. Overall, the results did not support

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) handwriting superiority effect or provide evidence for Bui et al.’s (2013) laptop superiority effect. When recording to-be-remembered information, it appears modality itself does not affect the retention of factual or conceptual information. This is likely because the degree to which students transcribe their notes does not necessarily depend on whether they are handwriting or typing them. This result is at odds with Mueller and Oppenheimer’s notion that typing leads to transcription.

If typing does not elicit transcription, which was the mechanism driving the handwriting superiority effect, then typing might not be linked to shallow levels of encoding either.

Moreover, if handwriting does not guarantee paraphrasing, it might not be linked to deeper levels of encoding. This might help explain the failed replications within the literature in the last decade (e.g., Morehead et al., 2019; Urry et al., 2021). If a levels-of-processing account is the CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 117 mechanism that explains the effect, it would hinge on the premise that typing leads to transcribed notetaking and precludes paraphrasing. Whether students have the WM capacity necessary to paraphrase information under the cognitive load that is attending to a lecture or classroom activities is open to debate (Choy & Lee, 2012; Kirkland & Saunders, 1991; Morrison et al., 2015; Rachele, 1985; Thadphoothon, 2019). However, since transcription is far less cognitively demanding, most students can benefit from it.

While the encoding episode of any material in the classroom is undoubtedly a helpful step in the learning process, the ability to study these materials creates many more opportunities for learning and understanding. The latter might be more important in the long term. Thus, in support of the external storage hypothesis, if typing increases the quantity of notes that students can generate in the classroom, then this might be a better choice than handwriting. Naturally, if students are to be advised to type their notes, two important considerations need to be taken into account. First, computers offer many distractions that compete for the students’ attention.

Otherwise, if students often fail to listen to their lectures because they engage in unrelated computer-based activities, then writing might lead to more complete notes in a realistic classroom setting. Second, and perhaps obviously, students need to actively review of their lecture materials after creating them to increase their performance on their evaluations (Putnam et al., 2016). If they fail to study, then the quality of the encoding episode in the classroom becomes crucial and any classroom behavior that promotes deeper processing should be privileged irrespective of the notetaking approach.

Limitations and Future Directions

The results of this present experiment and its limitations help to suggest future directions for academic notetaking research. One limitation impacted how interpretable the null effect of CHAPTER 5: THE EXTERNAL-STORAGE BENEFIT OF LAPTOP NOTETAKING 118 review can be. That is, all participants completed the cue card creating session on day one.

Arguably, this can be considered a study episode, which means that Non-Reviewers were not truly non-reviewers. This was done because the programming in Qualtrics did not make it possible to retain the Study Condition data during the 24-hour delay. Replicating this experiment in person would provide a means of ensuring that Non-Reviewers did not also participate in the cue card creating task. Another benefit to conducting this experiment in person is the generalizability of the results to in-person learning and testing. Still, given the ever-increasing prevalence of online asynchronous learning contexts, this research has certainly provided insight into students’ notetaking habits in that context.

Our findings were also aligned with those Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) in that instructions did not significantly alter the verbatim overlap in participants' notes compared to the lecture transcript. It could be that students require more than an instruction set to adequately paraphrase while taking notes during a lecture. It is important to note, however, that verbatim overlap is not a direct measure of paraphrasing (or the absence thereof). It indicates transcription, but researchers might invest time into determining how to measure paraphrasing accurately and efficiently. Our instructions informed participants that paraphrasing was made easier by using symbols, abbreviations, and jottings, which might not have provided a clear paraphrasing definition. If the reformulation of information while notetaking can improve academic performance, it is crucial for future research to establish a shared paraphrasing definition, develop a measuring method, and assist students in mastering this skill.

Educational Implications

Considering the current evidence, promoting handwritten notetaking as an unequivocal way to enhance learning and students’ academic success can be at best described as misleading.

It is crucial for educators to understand that if handwriting leads to superior encoding of information, then these benefits are contingent on students’ ability to paraphrase the information in real-time, a task that requires considerable WM capacity. Instead of advocating for one notetaking strategy over another, it might be more beneficial for educators to encourage students to choose the method that best suits their individual preferences, cognitive abilities, and study habits. To supplement this advice, educators should underscore the importance of active engagement with the material, consistent review, and deep processing of information, regardless of the notetaking method chosen. In conclusion, it is the depth and quality of cognitive engagement, not the method of notetaking, that primarily drives effective learning and memory consolidation. Future research should focus on promoting these practices, rather than prescribing one-size-fits-all strategies.

This increases the rate of cell firing and therefore strengthens the association between drug and pleasure. This results in increased drug-seeking behaviour.

This results in more dopamine available to enter the synapse, thus, increasing drug-seeking behaviour through Hebbian associative learning. |
