CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY

The Role of Notetaking in Online Learning:

A Study of Academic Behaviours and Performance During the COVID-19 Pandemic

Richardson, L. & Lacroix, G.

Carleton University

Author Note

Lindsay D. Richardson, Department of Psychology, Carleton University; Dr. Guy

Lacroix, Department of Psychology, Carleton University.

The efficacy of lecture notetaking in enhancing academic performance is widely recognized, prompting most students to take notes during in-person classes. Nevertheless, Morehead et al.

(2019a) demonstrated a significant reduction in notetaking frequency for learning in an online format. In this study, we surveyed nearly 600 university students during the pandemic-induced shift to online education, focusing on notetaking strategies and a few other academic-related behaviours. We also acquired data on the students’ final grades. Many students engaged in notetaking during their online lectures, mostly choosing to handwrite notes. They believed handwriting aided understanding, yet our analysis linked this modality to lower grades compared to typing. Our study illustrates student perceptions and behaviours during online learning, highlighting the need for understanding notetaking's impact on academic success in the digital era.

Keywords: notetaking, higher education, study habits, online learning

The Role of Notetaking in Online Learning:

The dawn of a new academic year always brings with it a sense of renewal and aspiration.

However, amidst the conventional advice dispensed to incoming students for optimizing learning, such as the importance of class attendance and time management skills, a more recent recommendation has been to take notes by hand instead of using the assistance of a keyboard (e.g., Putnam et al., 2016). Urging students to avoid using their laptop during class seems to have stemmed from Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) widely cited paper: The Pen is Mightier Than the Keyboard. Unfortunately, independent replications have failed (e.g., Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019b; Urry et al., 2021), and others have demonstrated that keyboard notetaking might be beneficial for some students (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Richardson & Lacroix, 2023a). Furthermore, the pedagogical landscape has been undoubtedly reshaped by the COVID-19 pandemic-induced shift to online learning.

Thus, the goals of this study were to shed light on the intricacies of modern academic behaviours, particularly notetaking strategies, to assess their influence on student performance in the context of online learning, and to providing valuable insights for optimizing learning strategies in the digital age. To reach these goals, we will first discuss the traditional notetaking strategies and their perceived benefits, followed by an exploration of the conflicting studies regarding the efficacy of these strategies. Next, we will delve into the evolution of notetaking habits amidst the transition to online learning, setting the stage for our analysis of the study's findings and their implications for teaching and learning in the online educational landscape.

The Benefits of Academic Notetaking

Today, most instructors recognize the value of lecture notetaking and encourage it as an important part of the learning process. However, this now widely-accepted notion was not as prevalent a century ago (Crawford, 1925). In fact, some instructors forbade notetaking in the classroom with the intention of encouraging students to pay closer attention to the lecture and to avoid distractions (Peters, 1972). Additionally, some instructors believed that by not allowing students to take notes, they would be forced to rely on their memory, which would enhance their learning and retention of the information presented during class.

Since then, research has conclusively demonstrated that lecture notetaking is beneficial to students’ academic success (Dunkel & Davy, 1989), which can be attributed to two main notetaking benefits: encoding and external storage (Kiewra, 1989). The encoding hypothesis assumes that students are actively engaging with the material and encoding the information while they take notes in a way that helps them retain it better. Conversely, the external-storage hypothesis posits that the benefit students incur by taking notes during a lecture is because it yields study materials, which can be used for later review. Researchers have long debated the relative benefits of the two in an attempt to discern which benefit is more valuable (e.g., Aiken et al., 1975; Bretzing & Kulhavy, 1981; DiVesta & Gray, 1972; Rickards & Friedman, 1978).

However, Kiewra ultimately concluded that this was unproductive, as it is likely that both contributed to students' academic success. The emphasis should be on notetaking itself, regardless of its specific benefits. His line of argumentation was convincing, but it was formulated at a time when handwriting was the only notetaking method readily available to students. With the rise of educational technology, however, the debate has shifted to the optimal

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 143 notetaking modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) for memory, information retention, and learning, as laptops now enable students to take digital notes.

Accordingly, researchers’ attention has shifted to deciphering the differential impacts of handwriting and typing on students’ ability to encode the information during class as well as their ability to generate quality notes available for later study. If external storage is more beneficial, students should transcribe notes, which would be more effective with the assistance of a keyboard because typing is faster than handwriting. Ultimately, transcription via keyboard notetaking would lead to an increased quantity of information for review. However, if encoding is more valuable, students should take notes by re-organizing the information in some way. This could be done by paraphrasing, concept mapping, or using symbols and jottings instead of full sentences. Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) argued that pen and paper facilitate this process, but no empirical evidence has shown that typing prevents paraphrased notetaking (Richardson & Lacroix, 2023a).

The Modality Debate

Nevertheless, the advice often provided to students is to leave the laptop at home (e.g.,

Putnam et al., 2016). One reason students ought to head this advice is that computers come equipped with many distractions. For example, social media, email notifications, learning management system access, unfinished assignments in the documents folder are all temptations that may capture a student’s attention during a lecture. However, it is essential to recognize that distractions can still occur even without a computer competing for students' attention. Mind wandering, a common occurrence in classrooms, can happen irrespective of whether students have laptops. Interestingly, Kane et al. (2017) found that some students experienced less mind wandering when they took notes during class, suggesting that notetaking itself could alleviate

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 144 students' tendency to mind wander. Additionally, Risko et al. (2013) found that students who utilized computers in the classroom demonstrated decreased tendencies of mind wandering. The evidence suggested that the time students spent distracted by their computer essentially replaced the time they would have allocated to mind wandering. Hence, students' access to computers during class might not provide them with significantly more means of disengaging from the lecture. Recognizing the distracting nature of electronics and the public's desire to avoid distractions in favor of maintaining focus and optimizing productivity, technology companies, like Apple, have included features that allow users to easily program focus modes to turn off certain notifications. Still, even if computer-related distractions can be prevented, it may still be preferable to take notes by hand.

In fact, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) argued that keyboard notetaking was detrimental to students’ performance over-and-above distractions. They aimed to examine the impact of notetaking modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) on learning outcomes. Specifically, they investigated how these different methods of notetaking affected memory, comprehension, and information processing during lectures, and whether the benefits of one modality outweighed the other in terms of academic performance. As such, Mueller and Oppenheimer conducted a series of experiments where participants watched TED Talks and took notes either by hand or using a laptop. Participants' comprehension was then tested. The results demonstrated that students who took notes by hand performed better on conceptual questions, which demonstrated a deeper understanding of the material. Conversely, the participants who used laptops tended to transcribe the lecture content verbatim, which did not benefit their learning as much. The researchers concluded that handwriting notes led to better encoding and processing of information, resulting in improved learning outcomes compared to typing. These claims hinged

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 145 on the assumption that the encoding benefit to notetaking outweighs the external storage benefit.

They also assume that students can adequately paraphrase the information transmitted to them during a lecture.

However, Bui et al (2013) and Richardson and Lacroix (2023a) argued that paraphrasing incoming information during a lecture or class can be an intense, resource-demanding, and difficult process compared to transcription. To successfully record the relevant information in a way that would elicit deeper encoding, students must continuously attend to the lecture (which might include multimedia, text, and a talking head), comprehend the information, re-organize it into their own words, and write it down. It is completely plausible that some students might not have the skills nor the working memory capacity necessary to execute this cognitively demanding task.

In fact, this was Bui et al.’s (2013) chief motivation for investigating the relative benefits of handwriting and typing on memory and learning. They argued that computers could equalize learning opportunities for students by lessening the cognitive load associated with notetaking. To this end, they conducted an experiment in which participants were tasked with watching video lectures. Participants took notes using either pen and paper or a laptop, and they either paraphrased their notes or transcribed them. The results indicated that both the method of typing and the practice of transcription yielded more complete notes compared to their counterparts.

Interestingly, the act of typing emerged as a superior method in terms of performance outcomes relative to handwriting. This finding, Bui et al. posited, reinforces the idea that computers can indeed offer a route for students to bypass the stringent cognitive demands associated with academic notetaking.

Richardson and Lacroix (2023a) provided additional support for Bui et al.’s (2013) line of argumentation in response to some unsuccessful attempts to replicate Mueller and Oppenheimer's (2014) results (e.g., Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019;

Morehead et al., 2019b; Urry et al., 2021). They re-examined the influence of notetaking strategy on academic performance for which 465 participants were given an Operation Span task to measure their working memory and then asked to watch a realistic neuroscience lecture online.

The instructions required them to handwrite or type lectures notes and to transcribe or paraphrase them. They were then given 20 minutes to create cue cards from these notes. After a 24-hour delay, half of the participants were given an additional 20 minutes to study from their cue cards, and the remaining half were not. The experiment concluded with a multiple-choice comprehension test. The results demonstrated that performance on the test was predicted by working memory and note quantity, rather than notetaking modality or method. Furthermore, the notetaking method instruction did not impact the degree of transcription, nor did the chosen modality. Thus, evidence was found to suggest that students may attempt to transcribe notes with pen and paper, while others may opt to paraphrase using a keyboard. In light of these findings,

Richardson and Lacroix advocated for a focus on the external-storage benefit of academic notetaking, assuming students plan on reviewing the material after class.

The prevalence of notetaking in traditional versus online classrooms suggests that students may already be prioritizing the external storage benefits of notetaking over encoding. To explore this topic, Morehead et al. (2019a) surveyed 577 students, examining their notetaking habits in relation to their academic experiences. The results showed that almost all students take notes during in-person lectures, but a significant drop occurs when the learning environment shifts to online. Only 49% of the respondents took notes for online lectures. This important

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 147 difference occurred despite 58% of students recognizing notetaking to be an essential academic practice. Morehead et al. concluded that students may be adapting their notetaking strategies based on the unique contexts of different learning environments. Interestingly, 67% of those who opted out of notetaking for online courses viewed notetaking as unnecessary due to the availability of online materials such as recorded lectures or slides. This might suggest that students see the primary advantage of academic notetaking to be acquiring material for future review, thereby emphasizing the aspect of external storage. However, despite the valuable insights provided by Morehead et al.'s study regarding notetaking strategies, it is worth noting that it focused primarily on notetaking behaviour and did not provide a comprehensive examination within the broader academic context. To gain a more holistic understanding, it would be beneficial to explore notetaking strategies in conjunction with other academic-related behaviours, such as class attendance and metacognitive beliefs, a more holistic understanding can be achieved. Additionally, the absence of performance outcome data in their study limited their ability to demonstrate the direct impact of notetaking strategies on students' academic performance. Investigating notetaking strategies alongside various academic indicators and gaining insights into their actual impact on students' overall academic success would be valuable for educators seeking to optimize learning in a post-pandemic and digital educational landscape.

Academic Behaviours

Certain student behaviours have been demonstrated to play a substantial role in determining overall performance and achievement, including class attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognition. Among these, class attendance has been particularly highlighted. For instance, numerous studies have found evidence that class attendance is positively related to final grades (e.g., Jenne, 1973; Kassarnig et

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 148 al., 2017; Marburger, 2001; Moore, 2003). While the relationship between attendance and high grades might seem self-evident, the prevalence of absenteeism among students suggests that the importance of consistent attendance is not always recognized (Friedman et al., 2001).

Shifting focus to study habits, it is interesting to note that researchers have found only weak and unreliable correlations between the time spent studying and academic performance (e.g., Allen et al., 1972; Beer & Beer, 1992; Wagstaff & Mahmoudi, 1976). This suggests that it is not merely the amount of time spent studying, but the quality and strategies of study that are crucial. For example, if students are spending extensive hours passively re-reading notes or re watching lectures, they are not actively engaging with the material. Conversely, a student who spends less time studying but employs evidence-based techniques such as practice testing (Karpicke & Roediger, 2008) and spaced practice (Carpenter et al., 2012) is likely to achieve better performance results.

Unfortunately, advocating for effective study strategies often meets resistance from students (Brown et al., 2014). This reluctance typically stems from their perception of these strategies as labour-intensive and time-consuming, especially when contrasted with seemingly effortless methods like passive re-reading and highlighting of lecture notes. What students might be underestimating is passive study habits’ ability to inadvertently undermine their academic performance, as it lulls them into a false sense of preparedness for evaluations. Dunlosky et al.

(2013) contended that students' learning regulation could be enhanced with empirically supported study techniques, drawing on research from cognitive and educational psychology.

Their comparative analysis of ten learning techniques (e.g., distributed practice, highlighting and underlining, practice testing, and re-reading) revealed that distributed practice and practice testing held the highest utility. In contrast, passive techniques like highlighting and re-reading

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 149 were ranked the lowest. Despite the popularity of passive study techniques like re-reading (Hartwig & Dunlosky, 2012), they offer a superficial level of encoding compared to active techniques such as practice testing (Schellenberg et al., 2011). A similar argument can be made for lecture re-watching.

In fact, Palmer and Persky (2019) explored this notion by examining the differential effectiveness of lecture re-watching and practice testing. Their study involved approximately 100 students who, one-week after viewing a lecture, either re-watched the lecture or responded to prompts. Comprehension for half of the participants was immediately tested and they all took a final test one week later. While there were no performance differences on the delayed test, re watchers performed worse on the immediate test compared to their peers who employed information retrieval as a study method. Consequently, Palmer and Persky strongly encouraged students to favour practice testing over lecture re-watching. Martin et al. (2018) reached a similar conclusion as they found no performance benefits of lecture re-watching. Additionally, their results suggested that re-watching can increase mind wandering. These findings underscore the notion that despite its growing popularity amidst the shift to online learning, lecture re-watching might not be the most effective study strategy. Instead, engaging in active learning methods such as practice testing seems to hold more promise in enhancing immediate comprehension and potentially mitigating mind wandering, thus warranting further emphasis in academic advisement.

While these findings highlight the importance of active study methods over passive ones it is equally important to consider the role of other skills that factor into academic success. Even though class attendance and study habits are contributors to student success, other skills also play an important role, including organizational and attentional abilities, for example. Students who

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 150 invest time organizing their semesters typically have higher GPAs (Dickenson & O'Connel,

1990) and meeting externally-imposed deadlines is crucial for success in higher education (Morgan, 2003). Staying organized enables students to offload working memory, enhancing their ability to attend to coursework and focus on academic productivity. This may explain why students with attention deficits often struggle with learning (e.g., Langberg et al., 2008). These skills can increase the likelihood of academic success regardless of lecture style and course context. However, throughout post-secondary education, it is likely that students will be met with many different styles of teaching, learning expectations, and environments. Thus, one of the key contributors to academic success is one’s ability to adapt.

Adaptability, as a trait, has been found to play a crucial role in the success of students in higher education (Feraco et al., 2023). Martin et al.'s (2012) Adaptability Scale is a comprehensive tool designed to evaluate adaptability in students. More specifically, it assesses students' ability to adjust their thoughts, behaviours, and emotions to a range of changing situations and demands in their educational environment. The scale demonstrated robust psychometric properties that established its value as an assessment tool, particularly in the context of higher education. The scale exhibited high internal consistency, with a Cronbach's alpha of .90, as well as both content and construct validity.

Overall, Martin et al. (2013) established that the Adaptability Scale is highly suitable for research in education settings. For instance, they found a direct correlation between the scores on the Adaptability Scale and academic outcomes in a sample of 969 high school students. Students who scored higher on the adaptability scale tended to perform better academically, indicating that adaptability was not just a desirable trait, but a predictive one for academic success. Martin et al. (2022) further identified adaptability as a vital attribute for successfully navigating

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 151 disruption, particularly in the context of higher education during the COVID-19 pandemic. This study found that students' adaptability was significantly associated with higher levels of academic confidence and greater academic participation, aspirations, and enjoyment. It also demonstrated that adaptability is a modifiable trait, providing direction for efforts to boost students' adaptability during periods of academic disruption and other challenges. This research not only emphasized the importance of adaptability for student success but also suggested ways to nurture this trait, such as teaching students how to recognize and navigate potential disruptions, and how to adjust their thoughts, behaviours, and emotions in response to them.

By helping students adjust their learning strategies in response to new challenges, being adaptable can facilitate metacognitive processes. Similarly, having strong metacognitive skills can enhance adaptability by enabling students to reflect on, and learn from, their experiences of coping with change. Metacognition—one’s ability to reflect and regulate one’s own thinking and learning processes—has been shown to influence post-secondary students’ academic success (Schraw et al., 2006). Metacognitive skills enable students to plan, monitor, and evaluate their learning strategies, which in turn helps them to optimize their study habits and approach tasks more effectively. By recognizing their strengths and weaknesses, setting appropriate goals, and adjusting their strategies accordingly, students with strong metacognitive skills can improve their understanding, retention, and application of knowledge, ultimately leading to better academic performance. By examining these factors in conjunction with notetaking strategies, the efficacy of various approaches can be better understood and provide more tailored guidance to students seeking to optimize their learning experience.

Thus, the goal of this research was to examine the influence of notetaking strategies on students’ learning while considering other behavioural predictors of academic success: class

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 152 attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognition.

Two cohorts of Carleton University students from online introductory psychology courses (PSYC 1001) were recruited during the fall semester for an academic experience survey. The first cohort was surveyed in 2020, the initial year of fully online post-secondary education due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and the second in the fall 2021. The full questionnaire, presented in

Appendix A, consisted of six subscales: notetaking, adaptability, classroom attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, and metacognitive beliefs. The notetaking subscale was a modified version of Morehead et al.’s (2019a) questionnaire, while the adaptability subscale was an adapted version of Martin et al.’s (2013) Adaptability Scale. The other questions were created for this study. With the intention of effectively capturing students’ adaptability within their first semester of university, the questionnaires were administered at three separate intervals throughout their completion of PSYC 1001: during the first two weeks of class (T1), the last two weeks (T2), and after receiving their final grade (T3). Students’ PSYC 1001 course grades were collected from instructors, avoiding potential bias and consequences of self-reported grades, such as the increased probability of Type II error (e.g., Sticca et al., 2017).

This study was primarily exploratory but proposed a series of hypotheses. First, if handwriting enhances performance via additional encoding, then students who paraphrase notes using pen and paper should outperform those who type notes. Second, if typing increases performance due to the benefits of external storage, then students who transcribe notes using a keyboard should outperform those who handwrite notes. Third, if typing necessitates transcription and prohibits paraphrasing, then students typing notes should be more inclined toward transcription, and less included toward paraphrasing, compared to those writing notes.

Finally, notetaking strategies were hypothesized to predict students' final grades in a regression

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 153 model, even after controlling for other academic predictor variables such as attentional skills, adaptability, study habits, organizational skills, and metacognition.

## Method

Participants

Participants were 576 undergraduate students from two fall term cohorts (i.e., 2020 n = 197; 2021 n = 379) enrolled in PSYC 1001 at Carleton University. Within both cohorts, students were recruited across multiple sections of the course that were taught by four different instructors. Table 1 presents the sample sizes taught by each instructor within each cohort. All courses were delivered online by Faculty Instructors or Contract Instructors at Carleton

University. Class sizes ranged from 121–390 (M = 288.38, SD = 75.67). Participants earned course credit as compensation for their participation in the T1 and T2 portions of the study.

Moreover, those who completed the final T3 portion of the study earned an entry into a draw for an Apple iPad. There was a winner from each cohort. This study was conducted in compliance with ethical standards and guidelines for research involving humans. Approval for this research was obtained from Carleton University’s Research Ethics Board (CUREB) prior to the commencement of the study. All participants provided informed consent, and their confidentiality and anonymity were strictly maintained throughout the research process.

Materials

The instructions and questions for all three surveys are presented in Appendix A (i.e., T1,

T2, and T3). To avoid redundancies and to reduce the potential of participant non-compliance,

T2 and T3 were condensed versions of T1. Additionally, T2 and T3 were designed to be more specific to PSYC 1001. As such, T1 comprised 103 questions, while T2 and T3 contained 58 and 41 questions, respectively. Eight demographic questions were included, probing age, gender,

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 154 ethnicity, program type and level, and GPA. With the exception of gender, which was asked only at T1, these demographic questions were also included at T2 and T3. However, GPA was only asked at T2 and T3.

Notetaking experience questions. Questions 11 through 43 were modelled after

Morehead et al.’ (2019a) questionnaire, which examined participants’ notetaking strategies.

These questions were used to probe participants’ likelihood, preference, and perceived ability to handwrite and type their notes. They also asked about participants’ likelihood, preference, and perceived ability to paraphrase, organize, and transcribe their notes. Finally, participants’ perception of the quality of their notes was probed. To ensure consistency in our data collection, we modified Morehead et al.'s original question formats, which included Yes/No and True/False options, to a 5-point Likert scale format. This change necessitated the transformation of some of Morehead et al.'s original questions into statements compatible with our agree-to-disagree (or always-to-never) Likert scale responses. Furthermore, in our version of the questionnaire, we omitted the open-ended response options that Morehead et al. included, which had asked participants to “please explain” their answers.

Academic Performance Indicators. For the purposes of this present research, individual academic-related behaviours have been categorized as class attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attention skills, adaptability, and metacognition. Therefore, 60 questions provided scores for these behaviours. Participants were asked about their class attendance frequency and various study habits, including active studying, time devoted to studying, and their intentions and actual behaviours regarding lecture re-watching. The section on organizational skills assessed participants’ skills regarding time management, ability to meet deadlines (e.g., assignments), and their typical completion of required readings. To probe attentional skills,

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 155 participants were asked about their tendency and preference to study in quiet places and avoid distractions. Their behaviour during class regarding avoiding distractions and ability to concentrate were also assessed.

Questions 85–93 were directly from Martin et al.’s (2013) Adaptability Scale. To tailor questions specifically to the higher education context, additional questions (94–99) were included (e.g., “I am able to revise the way I learn to help me through new classes”; “I am able to change my study habits to match the different courses I take”). The section assessing metacognitive beliefs delved into participants' understanding of the relationship between specific academic behaviours and their grades. Consequently, participants were prompted to reflect on the potential impact of modifying academic behaviours on their grades (e.g., “I believe I would achieve higher grades if I changed my notetaking strategies”). This section also included questions to gauge participants' perceived value of various academic behaviours, such as the importance of reading the textbook before class (e.g., “I think reading the textbook before class is important”). For a more comprehensive evaluation, questions were also included about participants’ views on the necessity of class attendance for good grades (e.g., “Class attendance is really important for good grades”), and they were asked to assess the relative significance of different academic activities, including completing pre-class readings, lecture attendance, notetaking methods, study skills, organizational capabilities, and intelligence.

Compliance check questions. Five compliance-check questions were used to ensure that participants were completing the questionnaire attentively. They were randomized throughout the questionnaire along with all other items. These questions stated “paying attention and reading the instructions carefully is critical, if you are paying attention, please …” which was followed by one of several options (e.g., “do not respond” or “choose sometimes”). Therefore, these

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 156 questions were designed to detect inattentive participants who were expected to select incorrect answers often and thus be classified as non-compliant.

Pandemic-related questions. Finally, three questions were included to gauge students’ readiness and willingness to take part in online courses, despite the necessity brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic. As such, participants were asked to rate the degree to which they agreed with the following statements: “I feel prepared to take part in an online course”; “I would have been taking this course online anyway”; and “I much prefer online learning compared to face-to face classes.”

Subsequent survey differences. For T2 and T3, the only recurring demographic question was regarding participants' majors. Questions about handwriting legibility and typing proficiency were excluded, as were redundant items (e.g., “I skip classes”). The Adaptability Scale was narrowed down to cover only academic adaptability. Questions specific to PSYC 1001 were adjusted appropriately (e.g., “I review my notes” from T1 changed to “have you started reviewing your notes for PSYC1001?” for T2), and only two of the five compliance questions were retained. The same condensing approach was applied to T3 as T2.

Academic Success

Academic performance was defined by students’ final grades within their PSYC 1001 course. As such, students were asked to consent to the release of their grades. Moreover, the instructors of PSYC 1001 also consented to releasing students’ grades within their classroom. All instructors from every section of PSYC 1001 at Carleton granted consent, ensuring that no section was ineligible to participate in the study. Due to the large size of PSYC 1001 classes, the most efficient method of evaluating learning is through multiple-choice exams and midterms.

Therefore, mostly recognition-based evaluations contributed to the final grades for the course.

Procedure

This study was conducted entirely online using Qualtrics (Qualtrics, Provo, UT).

Participants were recruited to participate in this research through Carleton University’s SONA system. They were informed that the goal of the study was to examine students’ common academic behaviours throughout the progression of a semester in university. Upon registration, the SONA system provided participants with a link to complete T1. They were emailed subsequent links to complete the survey at T2 and T3, however. After first providing informed consent, participants were asked to also consent to the release of their grades. Then, they were asked the demographic questions before completing the questionnaire. Throughout the survey, participants were asked to respond to several statements using a 5-point Likert scale. Three different scales were used: agreement, frequency, and affect. The agreement scale ranged from 1 (Strongly Disagree) to 5 (Strongly Agree). The frequency scale ranged from 1 (Never) to 5 (Always). Finally, the affect scale ranged from 1 (Least Affected) to 5 (Most Affected). In total,

T1 lasted about 50 minutes, whereas T2 and T3 took about 20 and 15 minutes to complete, respectively.

## Results

Data Cleaning and Screening

In line with Meade and Craig (2012), participant non-compliance was defined as incorrect responses to two or more of the compliance-check questions. Non-compliant participants were removed from the analyses (n = 22). Moreover a few (n = 10) participants were removed because they were missing more than 80% of the data. The data were also examined for potential errors. The Likert Scale data did not contain any impossible numbers (i.e., greater than 5). However, one participant listed their hours studied as 9000. This was considered a typo and CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 158 the data point was removed. Next, the data was examined for careless or insufficient effort in responding following methods outlined by Curran (2017). As such, minimal variability in responding (i.e., string responding) was used to present a case for careful examination and potential elimination from the dataset. This resulted in the removal of 21 participants. In total, 53 participants (9%) were removed from the dataset. No trends were found in the differences between participants who were removed and those who were retained.

Attrition. Given the requirement for participants to respond at three distinct intervals, attrition rates were also examined. Out of the initial 523 participants at T1, 266 returned for T2, and only 148 of them completed T3. Thus, the attrition rate was 71%, which was much higher than expected. This may be attributed to the incentive structure offered via Carleton University's

SONA system, whereby students earn course credit for research participation. Specifically, students enrolled in Introductory Psychology courses can earn up to 4% toward their final grade for participating in research via the SONA system. In our study, students were awarded 1% after completing T1. Moreover, T2 was scheduled at the end of the term when students might have reached this 4% limit. Thus, there may not have been sufficient motivation for students to continue with the study. Furthermore, the T3 incentive (i.e., a chance to win an Apple iPad) might not have been compelling enough to encourage participation amidst the demands of a new semester.

In addition to the attrition, the dataset contained a substantial proportion of randomly missing data (32% of values). The combined effect of attrition and missing data for T2 and T3 compromised the interpretability of the dataset. Consequently, subsequent analyses were conducted on T1 data exclusively, which comprised a total of 523 participants after data cleaning. Data for T2 and T3 are available in Supplementary Materials for reference.

Demographics

Of the remaining 523 participants (180 from 2020 and 343 from 2021), 130 identified as male, 388 as female, and 5 as either non-binary or genderfluid. These participants ranged in age from 17–71 years old (M = 19, SD = 4.36). Most of them reported to be of European (42%),

Asian (19%), and African origins (12%). The remaining participants reported to be of North

American Aboriginal (5%), Latin (2%), Middle Eastern (7%), South American (2%), Caribbean (1%), or other North American origins (10%). Additionally, most participants’ first language was

English (82%).

Many participants were Psychology majors (27%). However, several students were enrolled in the Humanities (21%), Computer Science (9%), and Cognitive Sciences (7%). The remaining participants were from one of the many programs offered at Carleton University, including Health Sciences, Business, Law, Biology, Media and Communication, Childhood and Youth Studies, industrial Design, Neuroscience, Social work, and Biomedical Sciences. Most of them were first-year students (85%). However, 10% were in their second year, 3% were in their third year, and 2% were in their fourth year. While most students either did not know their current GPA (66%) or did not report it (24%), of those who did report it (10%), GPAs ranged from 3–12 out of 12 (M = 8.93, SD = 1.91).

Considering all students were forced into online learning despite their learning modality preference, we explored the impact of participants’ preference on the relationship between their behaviours and performance. As such, participants’ online learning preference score was determined by the number of questions to which they agreed or strongly agreed with the three pandemic-related statements. Participants with a score of two or three were considered to prefer online learning (n = 139), whereas the others were considered to prefer more traditional formats,

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 160 such as face-to-face (n = 384). As demonstrated in Figure 1, participants’ preference toward online learning differed in 2021 compared to the 2020 cohort, X 2(1, n = 523) = 8.71, p = 003.

Compared to participants recruited in 2020, those who were surveyed one year later clearly exhibited a preference for in-person learning.

Academic Success as Defined by Final Grades

Even though 523 participants’ data were retained in the analyses, final grades were collected from only 413 (79%) of them. Moreover, two participants’ final grades were less than 3%, whereas the rest ranged between 14.98–100% (M = 71.58, SD = 17.67). One of them was reported to have withdrawn from their course, whereas the other received 2.86%. Additionally, three participants’ final grades were found to be extreme outliers, since they were less than 18.74% (i.e., 3 SD from the M). All five of these grades were removed from further analyses. As such, all analyses pertaining to final grades relied exclusively on the remaining 408 participants, whose grades ranged from 19.57–100% (M = 72.33, SD = 16.42).

Figure 2 shows that participants' final grades were negatively skewed. All main analyses were conducted using both actual final grades (as a percentage) and log-transformed grades, with no differences found. Thus, the results reported are based on the actual final grades. Nonetheless, the distribution of data that is demonstrated in Figure 2 suggests that there might be three distinct groups of students in our sample: students who earned between an A- and an A+ (A Students; n = 142); those who earned a grade between C- and B+ (Passing Students; n = 182); and those who earned between F and D+ (i.e., “DFW Students”; n = 84).

Individual Differences in Academic-Related Behaviours

This study examined students' individual differences in specific academic behaviours known to impact their final grades: notetaking strategies, class attendance, study habits,

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 161 organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognition. Tables 2 and 4–12 present participants’ responses to all survey questions pertaining to these behaviours. Since the questions were posed using a 5-point Likert scale, the means and standard deviations are presented for each question. A mean closer to 5 indicates a higher level of agreement (for the Strongly Agree-to-Strongly Disagree scale); higher frequency (for the Always-to-Never scale); or greater degree of affect (for the Most-to-Least Affected By scale). Additionally, all Likert scale variables were recoded into new binary ones, whereby 1 represented those who chose “5” (Strongly Agree, Always, or Definitely Affects) or “4” (Agree, Most of the time, Probably affects) and all other values were coded as 0. Hence, the percentage column in each of the Tables 2 and 4–12 represents the percentage of participants who chose these options.

Notetaking. The primary focus of this present study was to document students’ notetaking strategies alongside other academic-related behaviours to determine whether their strategy influenced their final grades over-and-above well-known success indicators. As such, the prevalence of notetaking modality (i.e., handwriting vs. typing) and notetaking method (i.e., transcription vs. paraphrase) was quantified for all participants. The responses to questions related to notetaking are presented in Table 2. Many students reported that they take notes to aid their learning (93%) and that doing so helps them learn by writing as the teacher is speaking (75%). Still, some students admitted that they take notes because it is expected (49%) and because they must take exams (86%).

The notetaking prevalence in this sample (84%) was lower than previously documented for in-person courses, but higher than for online courses (Morehead et al., 2019a). Additionally, 85 participants chose “never” when asked “how often do you take notes during lectures?” Still, 348 participants chose to respond to the prompt “If you are not taking notes, why not?” Some of CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 162 them reasoned that it impedes their ability to attend to the lecture (18%) and a few reported that they did not have to take notes because they could re-watch the lecture (9%). However, Figure 3 shows that their level of agreement with “I do not take notes because I can re-watch the lecture later” did not predict their likelihood of actually doing so. Moreover, a chi-square test of independence presented in Table 3 demonstrated that participants’ likelihood of taking notes was not related to their tendency to re-watch the lectures as a form of studying, X 2 (1, 575) = 1.40, p = .27. Note, however, that this is based on a very small proportion of our sample (n = 85).

Notetaking Strategy. In addition to the general trends in students’ notetaking behaviours, we examined their notetaking strategy, as defined by modality (handwriting vs. typing) and method (paraphrasing vs. transcription). Table 4 presents participants' responses to questions about notetaking modality. Most participants (77%) indicated that they typically handwrite their notes during lectures, with 66 percent of them using a pen or stylus. In contrast, less than half (45%) reported that they typically type their notes, with 41 percent primarily using a keyboard.

When queried about their preferred notetaking method, 70 percent favored handwriting, while 35 percent preferred typing. Notably, participants reported to have decent handwriting skills; that is, 71 percent claimed to be able to write quickly and 81 reported to have legible handwriting.

Conversely, fewer participants (58%) self-identified as proficient typists. Participants' abilities to keep pace with the lecture was lower for when they type their notes (43%) compared to when they choose to handwrite them (58%). Moreover, the quality and comprehension associated with each method showed a marked discrepancy. Participants predominantly claimed better quality notes from handwriting (65%) compared to typing (38%). Similarly, a more substantial proportion reported enhanced understanding when handwriting notes (72%) as opposed to typing (30%).

Participants’ responses to questions pertaining to notetaking method are presented in

Table 5. Most of them claimed that they try to organize their notes as they take them (92%) and that they like to use their own words (65%), whereas less than half of them (46%) reported that they try to copy what the teacher says as they take notes. Interestingly, a substantial number of participants claimed to be proficient at using shorthand to take notes (64%). When taking notes during readings, participants seem to use both notetaking methods: paraphrasing (70%) and transcription (77%).

To determine whether notetaking method depended on notetaking modality, composite variables for these two constructs were computed. Participants’ likelihood of handwriting (as opposed to typing) their notes was computed using the mean of Q17 and reverse-coded Q16 (M = 3.06, SD = 1.35). Similarly, participants’ likelihood of organizing (as opposed to transcribing)

their notes while notetaking was computed using the mean of Q18 and reverse-coded Q19 (M =

3.20, SD = 0.68). Moreover, a median split was conducted to create new groups: Handwriters (n = 300), Typists (n = 223), Transcribers (n = 121), Paraphrasers (n = 402). Interestingly, a chi square test of independence found that the likelihood of transcribing or paraphrasing notes was dependent on the likelihood of handwriting or typing them, X 2(1, n = 523) = 6.77, p = 01. Figure 4 shows that the likelihood of typists transcribing or paraphrasing their notes was almost equal, whereas those who take notes by hand were more likely to paraphrase their notes than transcribe them.

Other Academic Related Behaviours. Throughout the exploration of student study practices and habits, the data demonstrated several key findings about students' academic behaviours. First, Table 6 showcases participants’ responses to queries concerning their note review practices. The data indicated a general preference or tendency among students for passive

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 164 review methods, such as re-reading (93%) and highlighting (76%), over more active strategies like generating flashcards (40%) or formulating questions (27%). A notable trend was that the majority of students tended to review their notes in preparation for assessments (97%), rather than doing so after lectures (35%). Nonetheless, a subset of students reported engaging in consistent note review throughout the semester (77%). In addition to note reviewing, the tendency of note borrowing was explored, revealing that it is relatively uncommon (2%). It seems as though the majority of students predominantly relied on their own notes for studying.

For courses taught online, wherein lecture recordings are pervasive, another method for reviewing the material after class and prior to assessments is to re-watch the lectures. Much like re-reading notes (or the textbook), re-watching lectures is a passive method of review. So, we aimed to examine whether students who relied on re-watching lectures as a study strategy performed worse than those who utilized a more active method of review: testing themselves. To this end, a 2 (Rewatcher vs. Non-Rewatcher) x 2 (Self-Tester vs. Non-Tester) Between-Subjects

ANOVA was conducted on Final Grades. The results are presented in Figure 6. While the interaction effect did not reach significance, F(1, 378) = 0.92, p = .33, both main effects did: Re watching, F(1, 378) = 6.78, p = .01 and Self-Testing, F(1, 378) = 5.30, p = .02. Participants who re-watched their lectures performed significantly worse in the course than those who did not.

Additionally, those who used self-testing as a study strategy outperformed their peers who did not. Moreover, the best performance was achieved by those who did not re-watch the lecture and who utilized self-testing and the worst performance was observed in those who did re-watch the lecture and who did not utilize self-testing.

Shifting focus to class attendance, Table 7 presents participants’ responses to questions on this topic. The data revealed an overwhelming majority of students consistently attend their

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 165 online classes (99%). In fact, only one percent of our sample admitted that they skip class and three percent reported that they are not missing important information should they choose to do so. Table 8 continues the investigation into study habits. The responses indicated a strong consensus among students that studying is necessary, as a mere two percent agreed with the statement “I don’t need to study.” Moreover, most participants expressed the belief that they dedicate substantial time to studying (82%).

Moving towards organizational skills, Table 9 demonstrates that most students employed planners to structure their semester (78%) and claimed to be punctual in completing assignments 97%). Some of them even reported that they tend to finish their assignments early (42%), whereas very few of them admitted to missing deadlines (1%) or relying on others for reminders of upcoming deadlines (4%). Interestingly, almost as many students who reported that they need to work on their time management skills (70%) also reported good time management skills (50%). In the domain of attentional skills, Table 10 demonstrates that a significant proportion of students who reported being focused during class (76%) and some turn off their social media during class (45%). Unfortunately, many students admitted to being on their phone during class (59%), and that they do not put their phone on silent when doing schoolwork (43%). Lastly, participants responses to questions pertaining to adaptability and metacognitive beliefs are presented in Table 11 and Table 12, respectively. Generally, participants seem to be adaptable in their ability to take part in different types of classes. However, fewer students claimed to be able to have the ability to control emotions when uncertainty arises. When asked to reflect upon what they could change to earn better grades, many students reported that they need to work on their notetaking skills (75%). However, most students reported needing to work on focusing (85%)

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 166 and avoiding distractions (87%). Very few students claimed that there was nothing they could do to change their grades (8%).

Next, we explored participants’ metacognition surrounding their notetaking strategies. As such, a 2 (Modality: Handwrite vs. Type) x 2 (Method: Paraphrase vs. Transcribe) Between

Subjects ANOVA was conducted on participants’ level of agreement with the statement “I believe I would achieve higher grades if I changed my notetaking strategies.” The results demonstrated that notetaking modality and method impacted participants’ likelihood of agreement. While the interaction did not reach significance, F(1, 519) = 1.15, p = .28, both main effects did: Modality, F(1, 519) = 6.73, p = .01; Method, F(1, 519) = 5.30, p = .02. These results are complimented by the data presented in Figure 5, which suggests that transcribers believe they could enhance their notetaking strategies.

Before computing composite variables to represent the different academic behaviour factors, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was conducted to determine whether the Likert scale survey data qualified for data reduction. The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy was .732, indicating that the sample size was suitable for PCA. Additionally,

Bartlett's test of sphericity was significant, χ²(9316) = 25,076.133, p < .001, indicating that the data were suitable for reduction using PCA. The Scree plot indicated that the appropriate number of components to retain was somewhere between 5–10. Based on Keiser’s criterion and the Scree plot, the number of components to retain was chosen to be 10. Next, the PCA was conducted using a Direct Oblimin rotation, allowing the factors to freely correlate with one another. Some anticipated components emerged naturally from the data; for instance, one factor consisted primarily of items investigating students' metacognitive strategies, while another factor was dominated by items related to their notetaking modalities. However, despite re-running the CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 167 analysis with a reduced number of retained factors, the resulting factor loadings did not entirely align with the theoretical components we had initially postulated.

Thus, reliability analyses were conducted to assess the internal consistency of the items theorized to constitute each construct (as represented in Tables 2 and 4–12). Items that lowered the Cronbach's alpha level of the composite variable were excluded from the final composite.

Likewise, items that did not correlate significantly with their respective composite scores were also removed to ensure construct validity. The final composite variables, as presented in Table 13, encompassed nine constructs: active study habits, passive study habits, class attendance, notetaking, handwriting, adaptability, attentional skills, organizational skills, and metacognitive beliefs. Descriptive statistics for these composite variables, along with the bivariate correlations between them, are provided in Table 14. Two of the nine constructs were found to have correlated with students’ final grades: metacognitive beliefs and class attendance. Many other correlations were found, however. Notably, adaptability was found to be related to both attentional and organizational skills. Moderate correlations were also found between adaptability and active study habits, passive study habits, class attendance, and notetaking.

Academic Behaviours’ Impact on Academic Success

To examine which variables predicted final grades, a backward stepwise regression analysis was conducted on participants’ final grades. Initially, 13 variables were entered into the model: hours spent studying, HSGA, age, GPA, and the 9 constructs (i.e., active study habits, passive study habits, notetaking, handwriting, class attendance, adaptability, attentional skills, organizational skills, and metacognitive beliefs). Through the backward elimination process, only one variable was retained in the final model: handwriting. The final model was statistically significant, F(1, 19) = 6.49, p = .02, and accounted for 26% of the variance in the dependent

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 168 variable (R² = .26, adjusted R² = .22), indicating that the predictor in the model contributed to explaining a modest proportion of the variation in participants’ final grades. Within the final model, handwriting was a significant negative predictor of final grades (β = -10.80, p = .02), indicating that an increase in participants’ likelihood of handwriting as opposed to typing was associated with a decrease in final grades. All excluded variable are presented in Table 15.

Surprisingly, handwriting, which initially did not show a significant relationship with final grades in bivariate correlations, exhibited a suppression effect when considered alongside the other predictor variables. This suppression effect suggests that the presence of the other variables enhances the predictive power of handwriting on students' final grades. Despite not showing a significant relationship independently, handwriting's influence in the regression model revealed its predictive value when combined with other academic behaviour constructs. This finding underscores the complex interplay between academic behaviours and their combined impact on academic success.

## Discussion

The goal of this study was to examine the effect of notetaking strategies (i.e., modality and method), alongside other academic-related behaviours, on students’ performance (as defined by their final grades). Two cohorts from PSYC 1001 at Carleton University participated in an academic experience survey. The data was collected during the first (2020) and second year (2021) of compulsory online courses caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Participants provided insights into their online learning experiences, notetaking behaviours, and other predictors of academic success, such as class attendance, study habits, and metacognitive beliefs. As a result of the pandemic-induced isolation restrictions, both instructors and students had to adapt to online learning. Therefore, the findings presented represent a unique and homogeneous sample

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 169 of students who were all obligated to complete their courses online. Notably, the data indicated that students generally held a distaste for online learning, particularly in the context of an abrupt emergency shift from traditional face-to-face courses to online formats and oftentimes without pedagogical redesign.

This distinctive sample presents a rare opportunity to investigate students’ behaviours, including the notetaking strategies they employ, in online courses despite their preference for course format (i.e., online vs. face-to-face). It is important to note that the data presented represent students’ perception of their own behaviours during the first few weeks of class, when their motivation and self-efficacy might be heightened due to a fresh start effect (Dai et al., 2014).

Academic Performance Indicators

To support educators in developing timely interventions, researchers strive to identify behavioural indicators associated with positive and negative academic outcomes (Yao et al., 2019). This survey explored nine factors hypothesized to influence students' academic performance: active study habits, passive study habits, notetaking, handwriting, class attendance, adaptability, attentional skills, organizational skills, and metacognitive beliefs. When examined in isolation, metacognition and class attendance showed a positive correlation with academic performance. Moreover, the data revealed that students recognized the impact of their behaviours on their success, as very few believed that there was nothing they could do to change their grades. Many students acknowledged the need to enhance their attentional skills, specifically focusing and avoiding distractions. Interestingly, while several students reported being focused during class, a considerable number admitted to using their phones or engaging with social

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 170 media. This suggests that students were aware of the potential negative impact of such behaviours on their academic success, despite occasionally engaging in them during class.

One behaviour many of the participants understood to be conducive to success was the use of planners to organize the semester (as opposed to relying on others to be reminded of upcoming deadlines). Many students reported adopting planners for this purpose, and a vast majority claimed to be punctual in completing assignments. These findings may also be considered in the context of the fresh start effect, suggesting that students' perceptions of their behaviours at the beginning of the course may be influenced by high motivation to succeed.

Interestingly, a significant number of students acknowledged the need to improve their time management skills. This might present evidence of a social desirability effect, whereby students claim to behave in ways they understand are conducive to learning and success in their courses.

If students were responding in a socially desirable manner, they might be unaware of certain inefficient study habits. Our findings revealed a prevailing tendency for passive review methods, with almost every student reporting re-reading and highlighting their notes as their primary review approach. In contrast, less than half of the participants reported using flashcards, and only a few claimed to generate questions from their notes. Hence, despite acknowledging the necessity of studying and dedicating substantial time to it, students often opted for less effective study techniques when preparing for assessments. An interesting finding was the prevalence of re-watching lectures as a passive review method. Given that all participants were enrolled in online courses, they likely had the opportunity to review recorded lectures. Students who frequently re-watched lectures performed worse in their courses than those who did not engage in this practice. Furthermore, when compared to the more active method of self-testing, participants who relied on re-watching lectures without utilizing self-testing performed

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 171 significantly worse in their courses. These results replicated previous research (Martin et al., 2018; Palmer & Persky, 2019) and they underscore the importance of encouraging students to adopt more effective study strategies, such as self-testing, to optimize their academic performance.

Academic Notetaking in Online Courses

To adequately prepare for effective review sessions, students would need to ensure that the notes they take during class are of high quality. Many of the students in our sample claimed to require improvements in their notetaking skills. The strategies employed by students when taking notes during class play a crucial role in determining the quality of their notes. Since these notes are instrumental in preparing for effective review sessions, their notetaking strategies could indirectly impact their academic performance. While notetaking, when analyzed individually, was not found to be related to academic performance, its significance became apparent when considered alongside other predictor variables. Notably, notetaking modality emerged as a significant predictor, revealing an inverse relationship between students' preference for handwriting their notes and their grades. This provides partial support for our fourth hypothesis that, after controlling for other academic predictor variables, notetaking strategies would predict students' final grades.

Participants' motivation to take notes during class appears to be rooted in their belief that this practice enhances learning, as they can capture information while the teacher speaks. This sentiment is emphasized by the majority agreeing that they would not take notes if not required for exams, indicating a perceived connection between notetaking and improved learning outcomes. Nonetheless, some participants admitted to refraining from notetaking in their online courses. However, notetaking prevalence rates were lower than that reported by Morehead et al.

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 172 (2019a) for in-person learning but higher than what was reported for online learning. This makes sense if the distinction in sample characteristics between the two studies is considered. Morehead et al.'s research was conducted before the pandemic, and their sample consisted of students who had experience with both online and in-person courses. In contrast, our survey focused exclusively on participants involved in online learning, irrespective of their learning format preference. As a result, the prevalence rates of notetaking in our study encompassed students who may not have voluntarily opted for online courses. This distinction in samples highlights the need to consider the broader context when comparing the prevalence rates of notetaking behaviours between the two studies. For instance, it is possible that the students who opt out of notetaking in both online and in-person courses are consistently the same, while the majority of students, forced to complete their courses online, continue to adhere to notetaking practices.

The factors influencing students' decision to opt out of notetaking in online courses remain unclear. Morehead et al. (2019a) suggested that students might forgo notetaking in online contexts due to the availability of lecture material online. However, our survey findings present a different picture, as only a small fraction of participants cited re-watching lectures as the reason for not taking notes. Surprisingly, a significantly larger number of students reasoned that notetaking hinders their ability to focus on the lecture. Strikingly, the likelihood of re-watching lectures did not show any association with whether students engaged in notetaking. These findings suggest that additional factors play a role in students' decision-making regarding notetaking in online courses. Consequently, future research should explore the diverse reasons behind students' decision to opt out of notetaking, potentially discerning whether these reasons differ between in-person and online formats. Such investigations could shed light on how

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 173 educational contexts influence students' attitudes and behaviours towards notetaking and help educators devise strategies to promote effective learning practices in both learning environments.

Notetaking Method. Our findings do offer some support for Morehead et al.'s (2019a) assertion that students exhibit flexibility in their notetaking strategies. Notably, when students were asked about their notetaking methods during required readings, a considerable number reported using both transcription and paraphrasing approaches. This suggests that students can effectively switch strategies based on factors such as time constraints and familiarity with the material. For instance, it is plausible that students perceive less time pressure when taking notes from a text compared to a live lecture, which grants them greater control over the pace of information processing. This increased sense of control may lead to a greater ability to dedicate time and cognitive resources to re-organizing the material, rather than resorting to mindlessly transcribing it. In contrast, the fast-paced nature of lecture notetaking, where students are exposed to a substantial amount of information, may necessitate a more immediate focus on transcription, leaving re-organization for later when they have additional time and cognitive resources available.

This distinction in perceived time pressure and cognitive demands could explain the variation in notetaking strategies between these different learning contexts. However, a key finding in this present study was that, while taking notes during class, most students preferred using their own words and making attempts to re-organize the information they encountered.

Surprisingly, less than half of the participants reported relying on transcription. Those who did also believed that they could improve their notetaking strategies. This intriguing aspect of students' notetaking methods warrants further exploration in future research. Investigating the factors influencing students' strategy selection during different learning contexts, such as lectures

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 174 versus readings, could provide valuable insights into how learners optimize their notetaking techniques for various learning situations, lending more support to Morehead et al.’s (2019a) argument that students are flexible in their notetaking strategies. Understanding the cognitive processes underlying these notetaking methods can inform educators on how to encourage and support students in developing more effective and efficient notetaking habits, ultimately enhancing their learning outcomes.

Examining notetaking method prevalence rates in isolation might suggest that paraphrasing is more commonly employed than transcription. However, delving deeper into the relationship between notetaking method and modality reveals a more nuanced perspective.

Specifically, our findings indicated that typists are equally likely to engage in both paraphrasing and transcription of their notes. This refutes the third hypothesis that typing necessitates transcription and prohibits paraphrasing. In contrast, those who prefer handwriting their notes are more prone to paraphrasing. Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) argued that notetaking on laptops could be detrimental to student performance due to the ease of transcription and resulting shallow encoding. Richardson and Lacroix (2023a) contended that handwriting might not necessarily be superior, as handwriters might also engage in transcription without effectively paraphrasing. The results of this present study partially corroborates Mueller and Oppenheimer’s results but also suggests that typing does not invariably result in transcription, providing some support for Richardson and Lacroix’s claim. However, these results do not provide insights into the quality or efficacy of the participants' paraphrasing skills. Nor does it illuminate students’ notetaking skills more broadly.

Notetaking Modality. For notetaking modality in online courses, we examined prevalence, preference, perceived quality, and its impact on understanding. The results showed

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 175 that handwriting was the more common modality chosen for taking lecture notes compared to typing. This prevalence of handwriting in online courses might be attributed to the challenges of watching lectures and taking notes on the same device, especially for those without secondary monitors or larger screens. However, when considering participants' notetaking modality preferences, a different picture emerges, as the majority of them expressed a preference for handwritten notes, while less than half favored typing.

Participants claimed to possess better handwriting skills, including legibility and speed, compared to their self-reported typing proficiency. They also believed that handwriting allowed them to keep up better with the lecture pace and resulted in better quality notes, ultimately enhancing their understanding of the material. These findings might suggest that students hold preconceived notions about the benefits of handwritten notetaking for information retention and learning when compared to typing. It is possible that the research conducted by Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) may have directly or indirectly (e.g., Putnam et al., 2016) influenced students' understanding of how handwriting impacts their learning compared to typing. It is plausible that students are deliberately choosing to handwrite their notes based on the expectation that this practice will lead to increased encoding and better academic performance.

However, this reliance on handwritten notes might not be suited to all individuals and could potentially lead to detriments in performance (Bui et al., 2013).

The negative relationship observed between handwritten notetaking and final grades raises important questions about the alignment between students' beliefs about notetaking strategies and their actual performance. If students feel compelled to handwrite their notes due to perceived benefits, despite their compatibility with typing, they may inadvertently be hindering their own academic success. As educators and researchers, understanding these nuances is

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 176 essential to guiding students towards effective notetaking practices that cater to their individual learning needs and optimizing their academic success.

Encoding Versus External-Storage. Kiewra’s (1989) perspective on the likely combined influence of both encoding and external storage in the performance benefit of notetaking during class was formulated prior to the widespread adoption of technology in educational settings. As the pedagogical landscape has evolved to encompass more digital learning, particularly in the post-pandemic era, it has become increasingly crucial to discern the relative impact of these notetaking benefits on academic success. If encoding is found to be more beneficial to students' success, it would suggest that students should be advised to take notes in a way that promotes a deeper level of encoding, such as re-organizing the information through paraphrasing. On the other hand, if external storage proves to be more advantageous, students should adopt notetaking methods that allow them to record as much information as possible, making transcription a viable approach (provided students review their notes, of course). This research aimed to contribute valuable insights into the ongoing encoding versus external storage debate, providing actionable recommendations to optimize notetaking practices in the digital educational landscape.

As such, it was hypothesized that if handwriting enhances performance via additional encoding, then students who paraphrase notes using pen and paper should outperform those who type notes. Similarly, we expected students who transcribed their notes using a keyboard to outperform those who handwrite notes, which would provide evidence that typing increases performance due to the benefits of external storage. Surprisingly, our results did not support either hypothesis, suggesting that a one-size-fits-all approach is not reasonable. The suppression effects observed in our regression model highlight the intricate relationship between notetaking

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 177 and academic performance. It is evident that the relative impact of encoding and external storage depends on a multitude of factors. As per Kiewra's (1989) perspective, students ought to be advised that both these benefits contribute to academic success. Instead of adhering rigidly to one method, students should leverage whichever notetaking strategy works best for their unique skill set and learning approaches. By recognizing the complexity of this interplay, our study calls for further exploration into individual differences and learning preferences, offering valuable insights to optimize notetaking practices in the dynamic digital educational landscape. With a more nuanced understanding of notetaking's impact on academic success, educators can tailor interventions to better support students' learning needs.

Limitations and Future Directions

While this study provides valuable insights into students' academic-related behaviours and notetaking strategies, it is essential to acknowledge certain limitations that may impact the interpretation of the results and offer directions for future research. One limitation is the lack of variability in self-reported attendance rates, with very few participants admitting to skipping class. This near-perfect attendance rate in the sample limits the ability to fully explore the relationship between online course attendance and academic performance. Future research should consider investigating whether class attendance patterns differ significantly between online and in-person courses. This exploration could shed light on the potential impact of online learning formats on students' attendance behaviours and its subsequent influence on academic outcomes.

Another potential limitation is the possibility of social desirability bias in participants' responses. While we did not include specific measures to assess social desirability throughout the survey, future researchers conducting similar investigations on academic-related behaviours

CHAPTER 6: THE ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE SURVEY 178 should consider incorporating social desirability measures to obtain a more accurate understanding of participants' responses. By addressing this bias, researchers can ensure that the reported behaviours and beliefs are more aligned with participants' genuine experiences and attitudes, enhancing the reliability of the findings.

Furthermore, an interesting finding was that many participants claimed to be proficient in using shorthand or perhaps some modernized version that involves jottings and symbols instead of longhand. However, the research on shorthand notetaking and its impact on academic performance is limited. A potential direction for future research is to explore the efficacy of shorthand or symbol-based notetaking methods in enhancing comprehension and information retention. Since concepts are better remembered when they are drawn compared to when they are handwritten (Wammes et al., 2016) or typed (Richardson & Lacroix, 2023b), students might benefit from drawing their notes. Investigating whether students can be effectively taught to draw their notes and whether this leads to improved understanding and recall could provide valuable insights into innovative notetaking strategies for academic success.

Overall, this study serves as a foundation for future investigations into academic performance indicators, notetaking strategies, and their impact on learning experiences. By addressing these limitations and exploring new avenues of research, educators and researchers can continue to refine and optimize strategies to support students' academic success in the evolving landscape of education in a post-pandemic and digital era.

APPENDIX A: ACADEMIC EXPERIENCE QUESTIONNAIRE

For both cohorts, this survey was given to both in-class and online participants at three separate time points (T1, T2, and T3). T1 was administered during the first two weeks of class; T2 during the last two weeks of class; and T3 was completed after students received their final grades. Thus, included in this Appendix are instructions and questions for all three surveys: T1, T2, and T3.
