CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION

The debate regarding the optimal method for notetaking in academic settings is not a new one. With the rise of technology in the classroom, there has been a growing recommendation for students to shift from computers back to traditional pen-and-paper methods (e.g., Putnam et al., 2016). This viewpoint has been extensively supported by researchers (e.g., Blankenship, 2016;

Fried, 2008; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay &

Lauricella, 2011; Kodaira, 2017; Kraushaar & Novak, 2010; Manzi et al., 2017; Muller &

Oppenheimer, 2014; Skolnick & Puzo, 2008; Sovern, 2013; Yamamoto, 2007), primarily predicated on the notion that computers introduce a greater likelihood of distraction than pen and paper. The empirical research conducted by Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) further fortified this stance by presenting evidence that handwritten notes could lead to better encoding than typed ones regardless of the distractions offered by computers. Thus, even if computers did not offer additional distractions to students during class time, they could still thwart students’ academic success by other means. However, Morehead et al. (2019a) challenged this conclusion, suggesting that this it might be premature, particularly following their unsuccessful attempt at replicating Mueller and Oppenheimer’s results. Additionally, some research indicated potential benefits associated with typed notetaking (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Kutta, 2017), thus demonstrating the complexity and nuance of this academic issue.

The intricacies of notetaking strategies and their implications on academic performance are deeply tied to the fact that the benefits derived from lecture notetaking are multifaceted.

Specifically, these advantages can be distilled into two distinct but intertwined benefits: encoding and external storage. Each enhances learning outcomes, yet the extent to which a student can capitalize on these benefits may greatly depend on their notetaking strategy. For CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 236 instance, paraphrased notetaking, regardless of modality, may enhance the encoding benefit, and this process might be particularly facilitated by handwriting. Conversely, the external storage benefit, which relies on creating a more comprehensive set of notes, may be amplified by typing due to its inherent speed advantage. However, this relationship is further complicated by other factors including working memory, typing proficiency, lecture speed, students’ familiarity with the material, whether the notes will be reviewed, students’ study habits, and more.

Bui et al. (2013) explored how individual factors such as working memory and note quantity correspond with and potentially influence notetaking strategies. They discovered that transcribing notes, which increased note quantity, could enhance immediate recall performance, especially for typists. Kutta (2017) corroborated this finding, suggesting that students with lower working memory capacity and proficient typing skills could derive performance benefits from typing their lecture notes. Conversely, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) offered a mechanistic explanation, stating that typing, even without the distractions of the computer, led to poorer performance on conceptual test questions compared to handwriting. This was attributed to the tendency for typists to transcribe notes verbatim, which is a less effective encoding strategy.

Building on the intriguing findings and the subsequent theoretical debates in the field of academic notetaking, the current research set out with distinct objectives. Its primary goals aimed to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the complex interplay between notetaking strategies, learning, and academic performance. The ensuing sections delineates the unique objectives of each study, summarizes their pivotal findings, and aligns their results within the overarching research framework that guided this investigation.

Exploring Modality Differences in Encoding on Recall Performance

The primary objective of the initial experiments, presented in Richardson and Lacroix’s (2023c) research, was to evaluate the potential impact of the mechanistic, cognitive, and kinaesthetic differences underpinning the processes of typing and handwriting on performance outcomes. Prior studies have underscored the existence of these differences across diverse encoding strategies (e.g., Longcamp, 2005; Mangen et al., 2015). Moreover, research by

Wammes et al. (2016) has revealed a tangible encoding benefit for recall when concepts were drawn as opposed to handwritten. To investigate these research objectives, participants engaged in an encoding task wherein they were instructed to draw, write, or type words that sequentially appeared on a screen. Following a 3-minute distractor task, participants were surprised with one minute of free recall to remember as many words as possible in whatever order they chose.

The first experiment implemented a within-subjects design, randomizing the order of the encoding strategies during the task, and thus exposing all participants to each encoding modality.

The findings replicated Wammes et al.’s (2016) drawing effect, demonstrating a significant advantage in recall for words that were drawn over those that were handwritten. Moreover, words that were drawn also demonstrated a superior recall rate compared to those that were typed. Interestingly, no differences were observed in the recall performance between words that were encoded via handwriting versus typing. Hence, no evidence was found to support a handwriting effect, where words encoded by hand are recalled more effectively than those typed.

The results from this experiment may, in part, be understood through the lens of the distinctiveness effect, which is a memory enhancement for stimuli that stand out or are unique in some way (von Restorff, 1933). It is worth noting that Wammes et al. (2016) suggested the drawing effect they observed might be driven by this distinctiveness, meaning that the CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 238 heightened recall for words in the drawing condition may not stem from the act of drawing itself, but from the distinctive nature of such stimuli. When considering our findings, the distinctiveness of drawn words might have overshadowed any handwriting effect. If we consider participants' recall capacity as finite, then the pronounced memorability of drawn words, attributed to their distinctiveness, could occupy the majority of this capacity, leaving negligible space to discern differences between typed and handwritten words.

To address this hypothesis, a between-subjects design was employed in the second experiment. The methodology was the same as the first experiment, except that the participants only used one of the three encoding strategies. The results from this study presented a compelling contrast to the first experiment, revealing a significant difference between handwriting and typing groups. More specifically, participants in the handwriting condition remembered more words than did their typing peers. The evidence from the second experiment supported the hypothesis that handwriting could indeed be more conducive to memory. This finding aligns with the theories of embodied cognition, which posit that interactions between the human body and the environment influence cognitive functioning, beyond just the confines of the brain (Stolz, 2015).

Despite these promising results, the first experiment's inability to detect a handwriting effect necessitated a closer investigation, leading to the conception of the third experiment. The potential explanation revolved around the idea that the distinctiveness of drawn words in the first experiment could have inhibited the manifestation of a handwriting effect. To test this notion, a third experiment was designed to isolate the variables, limiting encoding options to writing and typing only. The methodology for this experiment was the same as the first, including the use of a within-subjects design, except that the drawing condition was not included. These results failed

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 239 to demonstrate an effect of modality on memory. The absence of the drawing condition did not lead to significant differences in recall between handwritten and typed words, echoing the results from the first experiment. The findings suggested that a handwriting effect might only be achievable when participants are not alternating between encoding strategies within a single experiment, a condition satisfied by the between-subjects design of the second experiment.

Re-Examining Lecture Notetaking Strategy on Academic Performance

The goal of Richardson and Lacroix’s (2023b) simulated lecture experiment was to revisit the influence of notetaking modality (i.e., handwrite vs. type) and method (i.e., paraphrase vs.

transcribe) on students’ performance on a subsequent comprehension test. This re-examination considered factors such as working memory, note quantity, and typing proficiency. Previous studies presented a mixed picture on this matter. While Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) discovered a handwriting superiority effect, several attempts to reproduce this effect did not yield similar results (e.g., Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019a; Urry et al., 2021). Additionally, certain researchers have suggested a laptop superiority effect (e.g., Bui et al., 2013; Kutta, 2017). Nonetheless, one consistent finding across these studies has been that typing leads to increased note quantity and that increased note quantity is related to increased performance outcomes. To delve deeper into this question, Richardson and Lacroix employed a methodology that combined Bui et al.’s research model and relevant aspects of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s methods.

In this experimental setup, participants were randomly assigned to one of eight groups: notetaking modality instruction (handwriting vs. typing), notetaking method instruction (paraphrasing vs. transcribing), and material review (review vs. no review). After the participants provided informed consent, they completed an Operation Span Task (Unsworth et al., 2005),

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 240 which gauged their working memory capacity. Next, participants in the typing conditions completed a typing proficiency task. To ensure parity across conditions, participants in the handwriting groups undertook a comparable task for handwriting. Once participants were given the notetaking instructions pertaining to their condition, they watched a 20-minute simulated lecture on the psychology of addiction. The lecture was immediately followed by a 20-minute window for participants to create cue cards based on their notes. A 24-hour delay ensued, after which participants returned for testing. Half of the participants were given 20 minutes to review their notes and cue cards immediately prior to testing, while the other half proceeded without this review period. Finally, all participants completed a multiple-choice comprehension test that encompassed both factual and conceptual questions.

The findings contradicted Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) assertion that a handwriting superiority effect emerges due to spontaneous paraphrasing while notetaking. Participants who handwrote notes were found to be just as likely to transcribe the lecture verbatim as those who typed their notes. This suggests that students may not have sufficient working memory capacity to paraphrase lecture content while simultaneously attending to the lecture. However, aligning with prior studies, typing and transcription did lead to a greater volume of notes, and this increased note quantity was a positive predictor of performance when participants were provided an opportunity to review. Hence, typists and transcribers generated more content in their notes compared to those who handwrote or paraphrased them. Although there was no clear indication of typists or transcribers performing better on the test than handwriters or paraphrasers, the findings did once again reveal a correlation between note quantity and performance.

Consequently, while notetaking modality may not directly impact information retention, it plays a significant role in determining note quantity, which in turn affects performance.

The insights from Richardson and Lacroix’s (2023b) experiment helped to integrate the understanding of notetaking strategies into a wider context of students' overall academic experiences. The research unveiled the complexity of notetaking and emphasized the roles of note quantity, review opportunities, and working memory capacity in learning outcomes. In light of these findings, the research naturally progressed to further delve into the effect of notetaking on performance, this time taking into account other academic-related behaviours known to influence students' academic performance. This progression of research was set to provide a more comprehensive understanding of notetaking, not as an isolated academic tool, but as a part of a multifaceted student academic experience. Hence, an Academic Experience Survey aimed to understand how notetaking strategies, together with other academic behaviours, impacted students’ performance in an online learning environment—an increasingly relevant context due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This study signifies an important step in the quest to understand and optimize student learning processes and academic performance within varied and dynamic educational settings.

Considering Students’ Broader Learning Experiences

Richardson and Lacroix’s (2023a) study aimed to examine the impact of notetaking strategies defined as modality (handwriting vs. typing) and method (transcribing vs.

paraphrasing), on students’ final grades. This exploration was set within the broader context of crucial academic-related behaviours known to influence student success, including class attendance, study habits, organizational skills, attentional skills, adaptability, and metacognition.

The authors recognized the complexities of academic success, particularly amid the shift to online education spurred by the COVID-19 pandemic. Their research sought to capture the realities of digital learning, where notetaking might interact with other academic behaviours to

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 242 impact performance. To this end, a comprehensive survey was administered to nearly 600 university students during the pandemic-induced transition to online education. This survey was designed to collect data on students' notetaking strategies and various other academic behaviours.

Additionally, with students' consent, final grades were obtained from their instructors, providing a real-world and practical measure of academic success. This approach facilitated an in-depth exploration of the intricate correlations between academic behaviours, notetaking strategies, and academic performance in the digital learning environment.

Richardson and Lacroix's (2023a) findings revealed correlations between notetaking strategy, other academic behaviours, and academic performance in an online learning environment. Notably, the researchers observed a prevailing trend among students to handwrite their notes during online lectures, a choice primarily driven by a perceived enhancement in comprehension. However, this modality was surprisingly linked to lower final grades compared to typing. Investigating beyond notetaking strategies, Richardson and Lacroix found that class attendance and metacognition significantly influenced academic performance. Students' self reflection on potential areas of improvement highlighted the need for better notetaking skills, improved focus, and strategies to mitigate distractions. The study also illuminated students' reliance on passive review methods, such as re-reading notes or re-watching lectures. Few students reported using more active study techniques like flashcards or generating questions from their notes. Yet, it was these self-testing strategies that were indicative of higher performance.

Interestingly, the researchers' findings did not support Morehead et al.'s (2019b) claim that online learners reduce notetaking due to the availability of lecture recordings. The data showed no significant relationship between students' likelihood to take notes and their inclination to re-watch lectures. Very few students indicated that they abstained from notetaking because of CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 243 the accessibility of these recordings. Overall, students' perceptions did not align with their performance outcomes. While handwriting and paraphrasing were commonly perceived as superior for learning, this belief did not manifest in their academic grades. Richardson and Lacroix's (2023a) study thus underscores the complex interplay between academic behaviours, notetaking strategies, and performance, and prompts further investigation into the discrepancy between students' beliefs and their actual performance.

Reflecting on the broader goals of the research, these findings add depth to the field’s understanding of how notetaking strategies and other academic behaviours influence student success in online learning, which has become increasingly relevant in recent years. More importantly, the results highlight the necessity to consider the influence of notetaking strategy in the context of a multitude of other academic-related behaviours. These are important considerations for students and educators alike, as they seek to optimize learning strategies in the digital era. This research journey, from examining the nuances of notetaking in different modalities and methods to understanding it within the broader spectrum of academic behaviours, sets the stage for a discussion on the research as a coherent whole.

This research endeavour began with an ostensibly straightforward question: Which academic notetaking modality—handwriting or typing—better facilitates memory and learning?

This question might suggest a simple experimental design: manipulate the notetaking modality during the encoding phase and subsequently examine differences in memory during the transfer phase—a typical design in cognitive psychology research. However, as the existing body of literature elucidates, such a seemingly uncomplicated research question unravels layers of complexity. Yet, an array of scholarly articles (e.g., Putnam et al., 2016) and educational blogs

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 244 (e.g., Campos, 2022) conclude with a unified proclamation: laptops should be abandoned in favour of handwriting. The endorsement of handwritten notes frequently stems from Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) research, positing that handwriting facilitates a deeper level of encoding and consequently, enhanced memory. Yet, it is imperative to recognize that this is only part of the puzzle. Notetaking's effectiveness also relies on its external storage benefit, which necessitates the creation of quality notes and their subsequent review. Furthermore, Mueller and Oppenheimer’s work has seen recent challenges with multiple independent researchers unable to reproduce their findings (Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019a; Richardson & Lacroix, 2023a; Urry et al., 2021).

Achieving the encoding benefit from notetaking demands significant cognitive resources, as students are required to paraphrase incoming information (Bui et al., 2013; Richardson &

Lacroix, 2023a). Should handwriting notetakers find themselves unable to paraphrase while maintaining lecture attentiveness, they may produce lower quality notes. In contrast, proficient typists, with the advantage of speed, can lean towards transcription when necessary, ensuring comprehensive notes for review. Therefore, understanding notetaking’s dual purpose, encoding and external storage, and the potential trade-offs between the two is fundamental to fully comprehend the impact of notetaking modality on academic performance.

Hence, this present research set out to revisit the central question of whether students should handwrite or type their class notes. Initially, it assessed the differential impacts of mechanistic, cognitive, and kinaesthetic aspects of each modality, aiming to determine if the act of handwriting itself confers superior memory benefits over typing. The investigation then progressed to study notetaking strategies, defined by modality and method, in a realistic yet controlled setting, taking into account note quantity, typing proficiency, and working memory.

Subsequently, the exploration extended to students' notetaking strategies in conjunction with other academic-related behaviours known to enhance student performance, including class attendance, study habits, adaptability, attentional skills, organizational skills, and metacognition.

This successive approach facilitated a logical progression from addressing a fundamental research question to situating that question within the wider educational context.

Summary of Key Findings

This body of research rigorously investigated the impact of notetaking strategies on academic performance, delving into the complex interplay between these strategies and other influential academic behaviours. The synthesis of this multifaceted evidence provides an answer to the initial research question while acknowledging the inherent complexities of the educational environment. Importantly, the findings lend credence to Morehead et al.’s (2019a) assertion that

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) handwriting superiority effect may have been prematurely accepted. If a benefit of handwriting over typing does exist, it is not attributed to the mechanistic, cognitive, or kinaesthetic differences between these two modalities, as shown by Richardson and Lacroix (2023c). This suggests that any perceived advantage of handwriting might be due to the potential for deeper encoding associated with paraphrasing. However, data from Richardson and Lacroix (2023b) indicated that the method of notetaking is unrelated to its modality, suggesting that a student's ability to paraphrase lecture notes does not depend on the notetaking modality the choose. Furthermore, consistent evidence throughout this research endeavour highlights computers as more effective tools for producing reference materials for review. Ultimately, this research both contributes to the growing body of work challenging Mueller and Oppenheimer’s results and offers a plausible explanation for these contested replications: method and modality emerge as notetaking strategies that are not mutually exclusive.

Unfortunately, the belief in handwriting as a superior notetaking method appears to be widespread among students. The majority report favouring handwritten notes, asserting that this approach enhances their understanding of the material (Richardson & Lacroix, 2023b). When comparing these findings with Morehead et al.'s (2019b) survey, where participants' self-reported notetaking modalities were evenly split, two plausible explanations emerge. Either there has been a recent revival in the preference for handwritten notes among students, or the shift to online learning has inadvertently prompted a greater reliance on handwriting as compared to typing.

The rise in handwritten notetaking observed was also paired with an unexpectedly high prevalence of both paraphrasing and shorthand methods. Notwithstanding the challenging nature of paraphrasing during a live university lecture, a significant number of students reported that they rephrase the material in their own words while taking notes. Intriguingly, irrespective of whether students use a shorthand-like method for notetaking, they report doing so. This may indicate a pervasive belief that handwriting notes, particularly when paraphrased or in shorthand, could enhance memory retention and performance in evaluations, a sentiment resonant with

Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) argument. However, the evidence drawn from students' final grades does not necessarily support this assumption. In fact, an inverse relationship was found between the propensity to handwrite notes during class and final grades.

The evidence seems to suggest that students can harness the encoding benefit through any notetaking modality, making the external storage benefit a decisive factor in the choice between handwriting and typing for notetaking. Although the handwriting superiority effect proposed by

Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) remains un-replicated by several researchers (Kutta, 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Mitchell & Zheng, 2019; Morehead et al., 2019a; Richardson & Lacroix, 2023a; Urry et al., 2021), one consistent finding emerges: typing fosters an increase in note volume,

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 247 which correlates with improved academic performance. This, however, presupposes that students review their notes, and the efficacy of the external storage benefit ultimately depends on the strategies employed during note review. Regrettably, students typically resort to ineffective study strategies when reviewing their notes (Dunlosky et al., 2013; Hartwig & Dunlosky, 2011;

Richardson & Lacroix, 2023b). For instance, Richardson and Lacroix found that students frequently defaulted to passively re-reading their notes or re-watching lectures. Active interaction with their notes, when it occurred, often involved only highlighting or underlining, still representing passive forms of review. Therefore, instead of guiding students towards a particular modality for notetaking, perhaps educators and researchers should emphasize active note review and advocate strategies that enable students to optimally utilize the notes they have prepared, regardless of the chosen modality.

In conclusion, this body of research contributes to the ongoing debate on the effectiveness of handwriting versus typing as notetaking strategies within education. It has established that the encoding benefits of either modality do not differ significantly (Richardson & Lacroix, 2023c), and that the choice of notetaking method does not directly influence a student's academic performance. Instead, the evidence highlights the potential of computers as superior tools for generating reference materials for review, and it underscores the positive impact of note quantity, an advantage associated with typed notes, on academic performance. However, the effectiveness of this external storage benefit hinges greatly on the students' note review strategies, highlighting the need for effective strategies in this area.

These findings prompt a reassessment of prevalent beliefs in the field, potentially catalysing a shift in educational advice from emphasizing the modality of notetaking to focusing more on the methods used in reviewing notes. Such a shift could lead to more effective

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 248 educational outcomes. Although the handwriting versus typing debate remains unresolved, this research emphasizes the need to approach the issue through a multi-faceted lens, considering not only the act of notetaking but also the strategies employed in processing these notes. The impact of this research extends beyond the field of education, potentially influencing how information processing is approached in various contexts, and thus provides significant contributions to the broader cognitive science community. Ultimately, it is not just about how information is written down, but how it is effectively assimilated and utilized that matters most.

Limitations and Future Directions

The present research, conducted during a global pandemic, took a step in understanding the impacts of various notetaking strategies on academic outcomes. However, like any comprehensive study, it has limitations and offers directions for future investigations. For example, COVID-19 restrictions necessitated online testing for these studies, impacting the researchers' ability to control for potential confounding factors and ensured compliance with the experiment instructions. Most importantly, these conditions pose challenges for generalizing the findings to traditional in-person learning environments. However, a silver lining presents itself in the form of the survey's sampling advantage; specifically, the ability to draw from a homogenous pool of students who found themselves navigating the necessities of online course participation.

Throughout the literature, including the research presented in this dissertation, there is an implicit assumption that students have the capacity to paraphrase information concurrently with attending to an ongoing lecture. Nevertheless, a reliable and standardized measurement of paraphrasing remains conspicuously absent in research methodologies. Current practices tend to utilize verbatim-overlap as a surrogate metric, a measure ostensibly representing the antithesis of paraphrasing, and draw inferences accordingly. This approach, however, presents a potential

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 249 misconception: it implicitly assumes that transcription and paraphrasing are dichotomous, mutually exclusive processes. This is a supposition that remains largely untested and therefore, warrants scrutiny. For a more precise understanding of the impacts of paraphrasing and transcription on memory recall, future research should endeavour to develop a robust metric to quantitatively assess the degree of paraphrasing. This approach would offer an added advantage by enabling an assessment of the legitimacy of claims by students who report paraphrasing lectures.

Just as students report that they are paraphrasing the lectures while taking notes, they claim that they are using shorthand-like notetaking (Richardson & Lacroix, 2023b). As such, the use of shorthand and symbol-based notetaking methods, as well as drawing notes, were identified as potential areas for future exploration. Research is needed to examine the efficacy of these innovative strategies and their impact on comprehension and information retention. On the topic of unexamined notetaking strategies, future research should aim to investigate students’ propensity to strategy switch; that is, students might take notes using different methods and modalities depending on different learning contexts. For example, paraphrasing might be more attainable when students have a certain level of familiarity with the topic. However, if students are attempting to take notes about a subject they have not yet encountered, it might make more sense for them to transcribe their notes and worry about re-organizing the information (to capitalize on encoding) when they are not under the pressure of attending to an ongoing lecture with information being transmitted at a potentially rapid pace. As the landscape of education continues to evolve, so too must the research that seeks to understand and optimize learning strategies.

Lastly, the longitudinal implications of these findings ought to be considered. That is, future research should aspire to track the evolution of students' attitudes towards learning and their notetaking strategies as they transition from high school to university. This approach would provide a more comprehensive understanding of how students adapt and possibly enhance their study habits over time. Are students becoming more industrious as they advance in their academic careers? What factors serve as reliable predictors of sustained academic success?

While the present research examined immediate performance or outcomes within a single academic semester, a longer temporal lens could reveal whether and how notetaking strategies influence more enduring measures of success, such as graduation rates. In addition to these considerations, a focus on the longitudinal effects of different notetaking strategies could provide insights into the implications of technology adoption in the educational domain. It would be interesting to investigate whether the increasing prevalence of digital notetaking media impacts the evolution of students' learning attitudes and strategies over time.

This research has provided one step towards understanding the complex matrix of factors that influence academic performance. It builds on previous work, challenges some established notions, and offers new directions for future investigations, each endeavour aimed at enhancing the educational experience of students. These findings and future directions should serve as the foundation for discussing potential changes to pedagogical practices and the broader academic landscape. The goal remains to optimize student learning strategies in alignment with the evolving demands of education and technology.

Educational Implications

The collective body of research, encompassing both the broader literature and the work presented in this dissertation, underscores the pivotal role of notetaking and associated academic

CHAPTER 7: CONCLUSION 251 behaviours in students' educational outcomes. Amidst a dynamic and increasingly digital educational landscape, these insights serve as a call for educators, students, and researchers to rethink traditional paradigms of notetaking and academic success. First, it is critical to challenge the widespread perception that handwriting is universally superior to typing for notetaking. As shown in this research, the debate over handwriting versus typing is not as straightforward as it may have seemed initially. In fact, the evidence increasingly favours computers as potentially more effective tools for capturing reference materials conducive to review. The modality of notetaking, whether handwriting or typing, may not independently dictate the quality of information encoding or the resultant academic performance. Instead, how students interact with the information, including their use of paraphrasing and note review, may play a more substantial role.

Yet, the capacity to paraphrase information during an ongoing lecture—an assumed student ability in most literature—remains to be thoroughly examined. The pressing need for the academic research community to prioritize the development of robust and quantifiable measures for paraphrasing cannot be overstated, as this would considerably augment our understanding of notetaking efficacy. Furthermore, it remains crucial to recognize that notetaking is not an isolated act but part of a broader educational context, intertwining with various other academic behaviours. The quality of notes serves little purpose if not reviewed effectively. Therefore, while guiding students to hone their notetaking skills, minimally equal emphasis should be placed on teaching effective note review strategies. Educational practices that foster a shift from passive to active note review, such as elaboration, summarizing, and self-testing, need to be advocated and integrated into pedagogical methods.

The onus is not only on educators and researchers but also on students to adapt and optimize their learning strategies. In an era where academic success is often synonymous with active and self-directed learning, students need to be cognizant of the benefits of different notetaking strategies and adapt to the ones that best suit their unique skill set and the context of the course (e.g., course format, lecture pace, content). In conclusion, the overarching implication of this research for the educational realm is the need for a more nuanced and holistic approach to notetaking; that is, a shift from a singular focus on the modality to an encompassing view that considers paraphrasing, drawing, note review strategies, and broader academic behaviours. The challenge ahead for educators, researchers, and students is to take these insights and translate them into transformative educational practices that optimize learning and academic success.

APPENDIX A: SUPERVISOR’S (AND CO-AUTHOR’S) STATEMENT

I confirm that the information presented in this thesis’s Statement of Involvement and Contribution is accurate. Lindsay conceived and led all aspects of her doctoral research. At every step, my mentorship and supervisory role was exactly as it would have been if this thesis had been presented using a traditional format. Hence, my co-authorship for the published paper and the submitted ones is warranted, but Lindsay genuinely completed the work expected from a PhD candidate.
highlights:
Morehead et al., 2019a; Mueller & Oppenheimer, 2014). This research aimed to provide a comprehensive examination of the effects of notetaking modality and method (i.e., transcription vs. paraphrasing) on academic performance. The first set of experiments evaluated the recall performance for handwritten, typed, and drawn words, delving into the underlying cognitive processes. The second part of the research deployed a simulated-lecture experiment to analyze the impact of notetaking modality and method on information retention, considering relevant factors such as review, working memory, typing proficiency, and note quantity. Finally, an Academic Experience Survey study assessed nearly 600 students’ academic behaviours and notetaking strategies throughout the forced-shift to online learning during the COVID-19 pandemic. Overall, the findings did not show a reliable retention advantage for handwritten notes over typed ones concerning the encoding of information. Instead, the evidence pointed towards computers as potentially more effective tool for capturing reference materials for review, thereby highlighting the importance of the external storage benefit to notetaking. Interestingly, students reported a preference for paraphrasing via handwriting, possibly due to perceived retention benefits. However, a predominant inclination toward passive note review was observed, which could compromise the external storage advantage. Researchers, educators, and students should focus their efforts on developing efficient active note review habits that will optimize student learning and ultimately their success.
...
Unfortunately, the research examining the differential effects of handwriting and typing lecture notes on learning have yielded mixed findings. Some have found that handwriting lecture notes is more conducive to learning (e.g., Blankenship, 2016; Fried, 2008; Grace-Martin & Gay, 2001; Gaudreau et al., 2014; Hembrook & Gay, 2003; Kay & Lauricella, 2011; Kodaira, 2017;
...
et al.,
...
To further scrutinize this null result, Urry et al. (2021) conducted a mini meta-analysis, comparing eight different studies aimed at investigating performance differences between typing and handwriting. This analysis appeared to support their conclusion; that is, there seems to be no discernible difference in test performance between handwriting and typing notetakers. Despite this, the meta-analytic review showed a difference in note quantity, with keyboard notetakers generating more content and exhibiting more verbatim overlap in their notes. This suggests that the storage benefit of notetaking may be influenced by opting to take notes by hand, while the encoding benefit of notetaking seems less impacted by the choice of typing.

results.title: Typed Versus Handwritten Lecture Notes and College Student Achievement: A Meta-Analysis | Educational Psychology Review | Springer Nature Link
results.text:
Typed Versus Handwritten Lecture Notes and College Student Achievement: A Meta-Analysis | Educational Psychology Review | Springer Nature Link

# Typed Versus Handwritten Lecture Notes and College Student Achievement: A Meta-Analysis

- META-ANALYSIS
- Published: 12 July 2024

- Volume 36, article number 78, (2024)
- Cite this article

Educational Psychology Review Aims and scope Submit manuscript

## Abstract

Many college students prefer to type their lecture notes rather than write them by hand. As a result, the number of experimental and quasi-experimental studies comparing these two note-taking mediums has flourished over the past decade. The present meta-analytic research sought to uncover trends in the existing studies comparing achievement and note-taking outcomes among college students. Results from 24 separate studies across 21 articles revealed that taking and reviewing handwritten notes leads to higher achievement (Hedges’ g = 0.248; p < 0.001), even though typing notes benefits note-taking volume (Hedges’ g = 0.919; p < 0.001), among college students. Furthermore, our binomial effect size display shows that taking handwritten lecture notes is expected to produce higher course grades than typing notes among college students. We conclude that handwritten notes are more useful for studying and committing to memory than typed notes, ultimately contributing to higher achievement for college students.

results.title: Is typewriting more resources-demanding than handwriting in undergraduate students? | Reading and Writing | Springer Nature Link
results.text:
Is typewriting more resources-demanding than handwriting in undergraduate students? | Reading and Writing | Springer Nature Link

# Is typewriting more resources-demanding than handwriting in undergraduate students?

- Published: 04 April 2021

- Volume 34, pages 2227–2255 (2021)
- Cite this article

Reading and Writing Aims and scope Submit manuscript

## Abstract

The present study investigated cognitive effort of handwriting and typing of undergraduate students. In Experiment 1, we used a secondary reaction time task to assess the cognitive effort required by undergraduates when carrying out handwriting and typing copying tasks. Students had longer reaction times, indicating greater cognitive effort, when typing than when handwriting. In experiments 2a and 2b, we investigated whether the additional cost of typing affected an ongoing activity. Participants performed a short-term memory task that required them to type or write by hand words to recall. As Experiment 1 suggested that typewriting was more effortful than handwriting, so it should leave fewer resources to devote to memorizing words, which would result in a better handwritten than typed recall. Overall, handwriting led to better recall than typing, particularly with the longest lists of words. This implies that, even in undergraduates, typing is still more effortful than handwriting and therefore has a negative impact on performance on an ongoing activity. The educational implications of the findings are discussed.

Fig. 1

The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.

Fig. 2

The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.

### Typed Versus Handwritten Lecture Notes and College Student Achievement: A Meta-Analysis

### Mood Impact on Automaticity of Performance: Handwriting as Exemplar

### Handwriting Difficulties: Different Manifestations and Underlying Functions

## Notes

All students also performed the Alphabet Task initially designed to assess handwriting automaticity in children (Berninger & Rutberg, 1992). The students carried out this task both by handwriting and by typing. Mean typing scores (M = 31, SD = 6.2) were significantly higher than handwriting scores (M = 22; SD = 4.2, t(29) = − 9.12, p < .001). In addition, the typing scores at the alphabet task did not correlate significantly with cognitive effort of copying (r = − .18, p = .35). Because, to our knowledge, no other published data informs on the typed alphabet task in adults, it is therefore difficult to claim, based on the scores at the typed alphabet task, that typing is more automated than handwriting in undergraduate students. We rather suggest that these higher scores presumably do not capture typing automatization in adults, but rather the well-known higher speed of typing.

## Appendices

### Appendix 1

### Texts to copy used in Experiment 1

Vendredi, c’est la fête de l’école. Armando est dans sa chambre. Il met ses beaux vêtements: c’est la fête de l’école. Il enfile un pantalon blanc, une chemise blanche et un manteau. Armando va danser et chanter devant son papa et sa maman, et devant tous les parents. Sa maman lui dit qu'il est temps de partir sinon il va être en retard. Armando prend son manteau. Quand il sort dans la rue, le vent se lève et souffle si fort que les branches se cassent sur la caravane. Le chapeau d’Armando s’envole. C’est la tempête. Tous les enfants entrent en courant dans la cour de l’école. Les enfants sont trempés. Les lampes de la classe sont allumées. L’orage éclate: il tonne si fort que les vitres tremblent. Les enfants chantent une petite chanson pour se rassurer. C’est le jour du départ. Le maître nous emmène à la ferme. J’aime les animaux. Mais il ne fait pas beau, il fait frais. Je mets un bonnet et un bon pull en laine. Maman prépare mon pique-nique: elle met dans un sac, un paquet de gâteaux, une brique de lait et une bouteille d’eau. Le bus arrive et nous montons dedans. Hélène me demande si je connais le nom des bébés animaux ? Armelle répond que oui. Les bébés cochons s’appellent des cochonnets ou des porcelets, les bébés coqs sont les coquelets et les bébés poules s’appellent des poulets. Je lui dis que non, les bébés poules sont les poussins.

C’est les vacances: Armando et Valentine dorment sous la tente. Ils campent sous les arbres, devant la caravane. Maman et papa dorment au chaud dans la caravane. Dimanche, pendant la nuit, le vent se lève et souffle si fort que les branches se cassent. Les vêtements d'Armando et Valentine s'envolent et la pluie commence à tomber. Valentine et Armando rentrent en courant. Ils n’ont même pas le temps de prendre un manteau. Maman va chercher les enfants qui arrivent en courant. Papa leur demande de vite se mettre à l'abri. Il leur explique que pendant la tempête, il ne faut pas être dehors parce que le vent risque de les emporter. Valentine et Armando se demandent si le vent est assez fort pour emporter la caravane. Ils ne sont pas rassurés. Nous arrivons à la ferme. Il y a beaucoup d’animaux. Une dame nous dit bonjour et nous emmène dans l’étable pour voir une vache et son veau. Nous allons ensuite dans la porcherie découvrir les porcs: une truie et ses bébés porcelets se roulent dans la boue. Dans l’écurie, quatre chevaux tapent du pied. Puis, nous allons voir les lapins qui croquent des carottes dans leur clapier. Nous allons ensuite voir les poules qui picorent dans le poulailler. Il fait beau. Nous nous installons, alors, dans le pré pas loin d’une mare d’eau pour manger notre pique-nique. Notre maître nous ramène des gâteaux préparés par sa femme Hélène.

### Profiles

1. Thierry Olive View author profile
highlights:
The present study investigated cognitive effort of handwriting and typing of undergraduate students. In Experiment 1, we used a secondary reaction time task to assess the cognitive effort required by undergraduates when carrying out handwriting and typing copying tasks. Students had longer reaction times, indicating greater cognitive effort, when typing than when handwriting. In experiments 2a and 2b, we investigated whether the additional cost of typing affected an ongoing activity. Participants performed a short-term memory task that required them to type or write by hand words to recall. As Experiment 1 suggested that typewriting was more effortful than handwriting, so it should leave fewer resources to devote to memorizing words, which would result in a better handwritten than typed recall. Overall, handwriting led to better recall than typing, particularly with the longest lists of words. This implies that, even in undergraduates, typing is still more effortful than handwriting and therefore has a negative impact on performance on an ongoing activity. The educational implications of the findings are discussed.

results.text:
Frontiers | Handwriting but not typewriting leads to widespread brain connectivity: a high-density EEG study with implications for the classroom

#### Educational Psychology

Published inFrontiers in Psychology 3.8 impact factor7 citescore

## ORIGINAL RESEARCH article

Front. Psychol., 26 January 2024

Sec. Educational Psychology

Volume 14 - 2023 | https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1219945

# Handwriting but not typewriting leads to widespread brain connectivity: a high-density EEG study with implications for the classroom

- FRF. R. (Ruud) Van der Weel
- ALAudrey L. H. Van der Meer *

Developmental Neuroscience Laboratory, Department of Psychology, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway

Article metrics

View details

A commentary has been posted on this article:

Commentary: Handwriting but not typewriting leads to widespread brain connectivity: a high-density EEG study with implications for the classroom

1. Read general commentary

## Abstract

As traditional handwriting is progressively being replaced by digital devices, it is essential to investigate the implications for the human brain. Brain electrical activity was recorded in 36 university students as they were handwriting visually presented words using a digital pen and typewriting the words on a keyboard. Connectivity analyses were performed on EEG data recorded with a 256-channel sensor array. When writing by hand, brain connectivity patterns were far more elaborate than when typewriting on a keyboard, as shown by widespread theta/alpha connectivity coherence patterns between network hubs and nodes in parietal and central brain regions. Existing literature indicates that connectivity patterns in these brain areas and at such frequencies are crucial for memory formation and for encoding new information and, therefore, are beneficial for learning. Our findings suggest that the spatiotemporal pattern from visual and proprioceptive information obtained through the precisely controlled hand movements when using a pen, contribute extensively to the brain’s connectivity patterns that promote learning. We urge that children, from an early age, must be exposed to handwriting activities in school to establish the neuronal connectivity patterns that provide the brain with optimal conditions for learning. Although it is vital to maintain handwriting practice at school, it is also important to keep up with continuously developing technological advances. Therefore, both teachers and students should be aware of which practice has the best learning effect in what context, for example when taking lecture notes or when writing an essay.

## Introduction

Digital devices are more and more replacing traditional handwriting (Longcamp et al., 2006; Kiefer et al., 2015), and as both writing and reading are becoming increasingly digitized in the classroom, we need to examine the implications of this practice (Mangen and Balsvik, 2016; Patterson and Patterson, 2017). Using a keyboard is now often recommended for young children as it is less demanding and frustrating (Cunningham and Stanovich, 1990; Fears and Lockman, 2018), allowing them to express themselves in written form earlier (Hultin and Westman, 2013). Be that as it may, handwriting training has not only been found to improve spelling accuracy (Cunningham and Stanovich, 1990) and better memory and recall (Longcamp et al., 2006; Smoker et al., 2009; Mueller and Oppenheimer, 2014), but also to facilitate letter recognition and understanding (Longcamp et al., 2005, 2008; Li and James, 2016). Such benefits for learning have been reported irrespective of when writing by hand using a traditional pen or pencil or using a digital pen (Osugi et al., 2019). Also, brain research shows that it is not just any motor activity that facilitates learning, but that accurately coordinating the complex hand movements while carefully shaping each letter when using a pen, is crucial (Pei et al., 2021). Apparently, the pen causes different underlying neurological processes that provide the brain with optimal conditions for learning and remembering (Askvik et al., 2020).

Recent findings in neuroscience reveal that neural processes are not as localized and static as is commonly believed, but that the brain is organized in a highly dynamic functional manner (Lopes da Silva, 1991; Singer, 1993). Under normal circumstances, several brain systems are continually working together (Buzsáki, 2006), showing an extremely flexible organization with structurally different neural tissue being involved in neural circuits that are only temporarily assembled so as to enable a given task (Edelman and Gally, 2013; Van der Weel et al., 2019). In such a view, neurons can change function entirely when incorporated in different systems (Anderson, 2014). Bullmore and Sporns (2009) refer to this type of flexible organization of the brain as functional connectivity as against structural connectivity.

Electroencephalography is well suited to studying brain electrical activity as a function of handwriting and typewriting in the millisecond scale. It permits the investigation of changes in the status of the underlying active networks (Lopes da Silva, 1991) and can reveal the everchanging spatial patterns of activations that are specific to any given task (Pfurtscheller et al., 1996). In particular, studies of cortical oscillations detected with high-density EEG are now considered an indispensable aspect of contemporary systems neuroscience (Fröhlich, 2016).

Brain oscillations can be considered as the interplay between the cortex and the thalamus and are generated by changes involved in the control of oscillations in neural networks (Pfurtscheller and Lopes da Silva, 1999). The complex interactions and the resulting particular frequencies are thought to reflect distinct cognitive processes (Klimesch et al., 1994; Berens and Horner, 2017). The temporal organization of neuronal firing is crucial as it is assumed to be fundamental when forming long-term memories in the hippocampus (Berens and Horner, 2017).

Frequency-specific changes in EEG recordings can be observed as event-related synchronization (ERS) or event-related desynchronization (ERD; Pfurtscheller and Aranibar, 1977; Pfurtscheller and Lopes da Silva, 1999). Spectral analyses are used to detect differences in a given frequency band (Pfurtscheller et al., 1994; Salmelin and Hari, 1994; Klimesch et al., 1996), by calculating the temporal dynamics of EEG oscillations and quantifying event-related amplifications and/or suppressions of rhythms.

A recent EEG-study from our lab showed that drawing by hand causes more activity and involves larger areas in the brain as opposed to typing on a keyboard (Van der Meer and Van der Weel, 2017). We concluded that the involvement of fine and intricate hand movements in notetaking, in contrast with pressing keys on a keyboard that all require the same simple finger movement, may be more advantageous for learning (Van der Meer and Van der Weel, 2017). A follow-up study observed event-related synchronized activity in the theta range in both children and students in parietal and central brain regions, but only when writing by hand (Askvik et al., 2020). As these studies have found evidence that writing by hand facilitates learning, the present study further investigated the neurobiological differences related to cursive writing and typewriting in the young adult brain. Specifically, we investigated how the various brain regions interconnect via neural networks when writing by hand as opposed to typing on a keyboard using frequency modulation and the latest in brain connectivity analysis (c.f., Solomon et al., 2017).

## Methods

### Participants

Forty university students in their early twenties took part in the study at the Developmental Neuroscience Laboratory, Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU). HD EEG data from 36 students were of good enough quality and sufficiently artifact-free to be included in the analyses. The data from 12 adult participants were already used in analyses in the time-frequency domain (Askvik et al., 2020). The present study performed a brain connectivity analysis to investigate the underlying neural networks involved in tasks of handwriting and typewriting. Participants were mostly students and were recruited at the university campus. They received a $15 cinema ticket for taking part. To avoid crossover effects between the two hemispheres, only right-handed participants were included, as determined by the Edinburgh Handedness Inventory (Oldfield, 1971). Allowing the use of (the fingers of) both hands would cause many unforeseen effects on the brain, which would make it hard to interpret the results. Participants gave their informed written consent, and it was made clear that they could withdraw from the experiment at any time without consequences. The Regional Committee for Medical and Health Ethics (Central Norway) approved the study.

### Experimental stimuli and EEG data acquisition

E-prime 2.0 was used to individually display 15 different Pictionary words on a Microsoft Surface Studio. The participants used a digital pen to write in cursive by hand directly on the touchscreen, and a keyboard to typewrite the presented words.

The experiment comprised a total of 30 trials, where each word appeared in two different conditions, presented in a randomized order. For each trial, participants were instructed to either (a) write in cursive with their right hand the presented word with a digital pen directly on the screen, or (b) type the presented word using the right index finger on the keyboard. Before each trial, the instruction write or type appeared before one of the target words appeared, and the participants were given 25 s to either write by hand or type the word multiple times, separated by a space. EEG data were recorded only during the first 5 s of each trial. To prevent artifacts produced by head and eye movements caused by shifting gaze between the screen and the keyboard, typed words did not appear on the screen while the participant was typewriting. The writings produced by the participants (see Figure 1 for example) were stored for offline analyses.

Figure 1

Task design, behavioral performance, and sequence of the connectivity analyses. Visually presented words were either written by hand with a digital pen or typed on a keyboard while participants were wearing a 256-channel sensor array. EEG recordings were analyzed in terms of their functional connectivity, resulting in detailed network measures.

A Geodesic Sensor Net (GSN; Tucker et al., 1994) with 256 evenly distributed electrodes was used to record EEG activity from the participant’s scalp at 500 Hz. The signals were amplified using a high-input EGI amplifier (Picton et al., 2000).

### Procedure

On arrival in the lab, a consent form with all necessary information was given to the participants to sign. While the participant completed the handedness test, an appropriately sized net was soaked in a saline electrolyte for 15 min to optimize electrical conductivity. The participant was sitting comfortably in an adjustable chair in front of a table. The screen was placed on the table as closely as possible to the participant. A keyboard was also placed in a preferred position for the participant, and a digital pen was used for writing on the touchscreen. A pre-test was completed before the experiment started, where one of the experimenters was present in the room with the participant.

### Brain data pre-analyses

Brain Electrical Source Analysis (BESA version 7.0) research software and BESA Connectivity (version 1.0) were used to analyze the EEG data. Epoch and filter settings were the same as in Askvik et al. (2020).

Channels contaminated by movement artifacts were either removed or interpolated using spherical spline interpolation (Perrin et al., 1989; Picton et al., 2000). Up to 10% of channels could be defined as bad. Artifact correction was applied using manual and semi-automatic artifact correction with fitting spatial filters (Berg and Scherg, 1994; Ille et al., 2002; Fujioka et al., 2011).

The mean number of accepted trials out of 15 was 14.1 (SD = 1.1) for handwriting and 13.3 (SD = 1.3) for typewriting. To analyze oscillatory brain activity, a time-frequency analysis in brain space was then performed on accepted trials, see Askvik et al. (2020) for details. Optimal separation of brain activity was achieved using source montages derived from a multiple source model where waveforms separated different brain activities (Scherg and Berg, 1991). Using this procedure, the time-frequency content of different brain regions can be separated even if their activities severely overlap at the surface of the scalp (Hoechstetter et al., 2004). Then, the connectivity measure of Coherence was applied, resulting in a symmetric connectivity matrix with the upper and lower triangular matrix showing pairwise clusters symmetrical to the diagonal.

### Statistical analyses

Probability of significance in connectivity values was tested with BESA Statistics 2.0, where connectivity measures for all participants were computed and the significant connectivity regions were used as guides in finding the extent of connectivity between the two experimental conditions of writing and typing. A combination of permutation tests and data clustering was employed. Permutation tests were applied to each set of time samples belonging to one frequency bin (Simes, 1986). Data clusters that showed a significant effect between conditions were assigned initial cluster values. Using within-group ANOVA’s, these initial cluster values were passed through permutation and assigned new clusters so that the significance of the initial cluster could be determined. A Bonferroni correction was used for multiple comparisons. As in Askvik et al. (2020), cluster alpha, the significance level for building clusters in time and/or frequency, was set at 0.01 and the number of permutations was set at 10.000. Low- and high cut-offs for frequency were kept at 2 Hz and 60 Hz respectively, and epochs were set from −250 to 4,500 ms.

## Results

High-density EEGs were recorded during the experimental handwriting and typing conditions. Artifacts were removed from the raw EEG recordings, then the inverse problem was solved by using a 4-shell ellipsoidal head model to analyze the brain regions of interest. The time series of the reconstructed sources were obtained and transformed into the frequency domain using complex demodulation. The functional connectivity between the reconstructed sources was computed using the coherence method. A high-resolution functional connectivity matrix was obtained, and the corresponding functional brain network was visualized. Network measures were then extracted from the network (Figure 1).

A time-frequency display is shown for three important brain regions in Figure 2 where the power/amplitude for each time is normalized to the mean power/amplitude of the baseline epoch for that frequency. The x-axis shows the time relative to the event, the y-axis shows the frequencies. The intensities are displayed as a color-coded plot.

Figure 2

Grand average coherence results. Displayed are only three selected connectivity areas of interest for the two experimental conditions handwriting and typewriting (left panels), together with the difference in coherence between writing and typing and their permutation results (right panels). Connectivity areas of large significant difference between handwriting and typewriting included brain regions CR-PM (central right-parietal midline, top two panels on the left) and CL-PM (central left-parietal midline, middle two panels on the left), as well as CM-CR (central midline-central right, bottom two panels on the left), in frequencies ranging from theta (2 Hz) and up to gamma (60 Hz). The x-axes display the time interval from baseline to 4,500 ms of recordings of the trial. The signal magnitude reflects the estimated neural connectivity strength between the various brain areas during the experimental conditions compared to baseline activity (−250 to 0 ms). Positive connectivity is shown as (shades of) red-colored contours in handwriting/typewriting plots (panels on the left) and difference plots between handwriting and typewriting/permutation results (panels on the right). Positive connectivity is significantly more prominent in lower frequencies (theta 3.5–7.5 Hz and alpha 8–12.5) for handwriting (0 ≤ p < 0.05, see also Figure 4).

Figure 2 displays the results of grand average coherence results from just three selected connectivity areas of interest for clarity, for the two experimental conditions handwriting and typewriting (left panels), together with the difference in coherence between writing and typing and their permutation results (right panels). Connectivity areas of large significant difference between writing and typing included central and parietal brain regions in frequencies ranging from theta (2 Hz) and up to gamma (60 Hz). The signal magnitude reflects estimated connectivity strength between brain areas compared to baseline (−250 to 0 ms) activity. Positive connectivity patterns are shown in (shades of) red. In the central and parietal areas, positive coherence patterns were more prominent in the lower frequencies (theta 3.5–7.5 Hz and alpha 7.5–12.5 Hz) for handwriting as opposed to typewriting. For handwriting, this activity appeared between 1,000 to 2000 ms and lasted throughout the trial.

### The connectivity matrix of writing over typing

Comparisons between the two conditions handwriting and typewriting were computed for each participant with time-frequency displays (changes in amplitude over time). TSE displays were limited between frequency cut-offs of 2–60 Hz, while frequency and time were sampled at 1 Hz and 50 ms, respectively. Symmetric connectivity measures were then obtained from BESA Connectivity and a high-resolution functional connectivity matrix between the reconstructed sources was computed using the coherence method (Rosenberg et al., 1989). Here, the number of in-phase components of two brain source signals at a specific frequency were described, and the corresponding functional brain network was visualized in Figure 3. Finally, network measures were extracted from the network and presented in Figure 4.

Figure 3

Connectivity results of writing over typing. (A) Grand average connectivity matrix results show widespread theta/alpha coherence results (in red) between PL, PM, PR and CL, CM, CR brain regions when writing by hand, but not when typing. The y-axes display frequencies from 2 to 60 Hz. The x-axes display the time interval from baseline to 4,500 ms of recordings of the trial for all involved brain regions. The signal magnitude (coherence) reflects the estimated neural connectivity between the various brain regions during the writing condition compared to baseline activity (−250 to 0 ms). (B) Further illustration of connectivity patterns revealing a concentration of 16 significant connections for handwriting compared to typewriting. Connection lines in red indicate connectivity in the theta range whereas lines in blue indicate connectivity in the alpha range. Levels of significance in connectivity strength for handwriting, but not for typewriting are further indicated by solid (<0.0001), dashed (<0.005), and dotted (<0.05) connection lines.

Figure 4

Symmetric connectivity matrix with t-values (A) and significance Table (B) with significant data clusters in the various sources of interest when handwriting is compared to typewriting in all participants. Thirty-two significant cluster differences marked in orange in (A) and fully described in (B) were found in the matrix and came out particularly significant in the parietal left (PL), parietal midline (PM), and parietal right (PR) areas.

Figure 3A displays the grand average connectivity matrix for writing compared to typing. The matrix offers a compact description of the pairwise connectivity between all separate regions of the brain. Throughout the matrix there is evidence for widespread theta/alpha coherence results (in red) particularly between areas parietal-right, parietal-mid, and parietal-left and between areas central-right and central-left. These connectivity patterns are further illustrated in Figure 3B revealing a concentration of no less than 32 significant clusters (see Figure 4 for details) for handwriting, but not for typewriting. A pair of clusters will represent a single link between the corresponding pair of sources. The 32 significant clusters thus represent 16 significant connections.

### Main statistical effects

Analyses were run to test for statistical differences in brain activity between handwriting and typewriting. Figure 4 displays the detailed effects (t-tests) of the permutation results. These results showed 32 significant cluster differences between the two experimental conditions. The t-tests revealed significant differences in connectivity primarily in the theta (3.5–7.5 Hz) and alpha (8–12.5 Hz) range within three positive clusters (in orange), namely in the parietal left (PL), parietal midline (PM), and parietal right (PR) areas (see also Figure 3). These positive clusters suggest separate processes (differences in band power) between handwriting and typewriting mainly in the parietal but also in the central regions.

As can be seen in Figure 4, significant clusters of differences in band power were found mainly in parietal and central brain regions.

### Network measures

Figure 5 shows the adjacency matrix for handwriting in the form of a hub, nodes, and edges of a simplified theoretical network (Figure 5A). Hubs have a higher degree of involvement in the network than nodes as expressed through their functional connectivity values (edges). Figure 5B shows the brain connectivity network results of handwriting compared to typewriting in this experiment. Proposed hubs (in red, ≥ 4 departures/arrivals) and nodes (in black, ≤ 3 departures/arrivals) interacting between brain regions PL, PM, PR and CL, CM, CR show widespread theta/alpha coherence patterns indicating stronger connectivity when writing as opposed to typing (Figure 5C).

Figure 5

The adjacency matrix for handwriting. (A) Hub, nodes, and edges of a simplified theoretical network. (B) Brain connectivity network of handwriting compared to typewriting in this experiment. (C) Hubs (in red, ≥ 4 departures/arrivals) and nodes (in black, ≤ 3 departures/arrivals) interacting between brain regions PL, PM, PR and CL, CM, CR show widespread theta/alpha connectivity patterns when writing by hand, but not when typing.

## General discussion

This study investigated brain electrical connectivity as a function of handwriting and typewriting using high-density EEG in young adults. Participants used a digital pen to write visually presented words directly on a touchscreen and used a keyboard to type the words. Going beyond our previous study where we reported synchronized theta oscillations in parietal and central brain regions when children and students wrote by hand (Askvik et al., 2020), the present study performed connectivity analyses on the brain data of 36 students to explore underlying differences in coherence patterns when participants were typing versus writing by hand.

Focusing on brain connectivity that has shown to facilitate learning and memory (Pfurtscheller and Lopes da Silva, 1999), we investigated parietal and central areas in specific frequency bands. These brain areas have been associated with attentional mechanisms and cognitive processes in visual perception (Pfurtscheller et al., 1994; Vilhelmsen et al., 2019) and language (Brownsett and Wise, 2010; Benedek et al., 2014), and have strong links to sensorimotor cortex (Velasques et al., 2007). We set out to investigate whether it is actually the act of forming the letters by hand itself that brings about larger connectivity in the brain, since perceptual, motor, and higher cognitive areas are more involved during handwriting as opposed to typewriting.

### Increased connectivity in theta/alpha range for handwriting

The present findings revealed increased connectivity for handwriting over typewriting, suggesting that different underlying cognitive processes are involved in the two tasks. Increased connectivity within the theta (3.5–7.5 Hz) and alpha (8–12.5 Hz) frequency bands has been linked to mechanisms underlying sensorimotor integration (Bland and Oddie, 2001). As increased connectivity in the brain was observed only when writing by hand and not when simply pressing keys on the keyboard, our findings can be taken as evidence that handwriting promotes learning. Interestingly, the increased connectivity between the various brain regions seems to be linked to the specific sensorimotor processes that are so typical in handwriting.

The theta/alpha connectivity patterns found in the present study may indicate that different neural networks are involved in handwriting and typewriting. Interestingly, whereas connectivity in the alpha band is considered highly task-specific and is said to correspond to long-term memory performance, theta connectivity seems to be related to working memory and the ability to apprehend novel information (Klimesch et al., 1994, 1996, 2001; Klimesch, 1999; Raghavachari et al., 2001; Clouter et al., 2017). Thus, the enhanced brain connectivity for handwriting appears not to be related to differences in muscular involvement. It has also been proposed that hippocampal activity is reflected within the theta band (Klimesch et al., 1994), adding further support for the benefits of handwriting in terms of learning and memory formation.

Lower frequencies are considered especially suited for facilitating communication over longer distances in the brain, and are often reported to “gate” the occurrence of faster oscillations, for example when theta oscillations in humans are proposed to gate gamma (> 30 Hz) oscillations (Canolty et al., 2006; Halgren et al., 2018). In general, this theta-to-gamma cross-frequency coupling can be linked to gamma networks desynchronizing and theta networks synchronizing during encoding, retrieval, and episodic memory formation (Burke et al., 2013). Others have suggested that theta connectivity activity (see Figure 3) is positively correlated with a brain region’s gamma power, suggesting a potent low-frequency mechanism for communication between brain regions (Solomon et al., 2017). Exploring these interactions may disclose the relationship between a brain region’s functional connectivity and local processing. Our results reflect such a low-frequency mechanism for interregional communication. Present findings of theta synchrony for handwriting suggest that low-frequency connections support the integration of information during memory formation, and follow from earlier studies that have reported low-frequency entrainment to be essential to cognition (Solomon et al., 2017).

### The importance of handwriting practice in a learning environment

Handwriting requires fine motor control over the fingers, and it forces students to pay attention to what they are doing. Typing, on the other hand, requires mechanical and repetitive movements that trade awareness for speed. Our results reveal that whenever handwriting movements are included as a learning strategy, more of the brain gets stimulated, resulting in the formation of more complex neural network connectivity. It appears that the movements related to typewriting do not activate these connectivity networks the same way that handwriting does. The concurrent spatiotemporal pattern from vision, motor commands, and proprioceptive feedback provided through fine hand and finger movements, is lacking in typewriting, where only a simple key press is required to produce the entire wanted form (Longcamp et al., 2006; James, 2010; Vinci-Booher et al., 2016, 2021). In the present study, participants only used their right index finger for typing to prevent undesired crossover effects between the two hemispheres.

Thus, the ongoing substitution of handwriting by typewriting in almost every educational setting may seem somewhat misguided as it could affect the learning process in a negative way (Alonso, 2015; Mangen and Balsvik, 2016; Arnold et al., 2017). The present findings suggest that the intricate and precisely controlled handwriting movements have a beneficial impact on the brain’s connectivity patterns related to learning and remembering. The present study did not find evidence of such positive activation patterns when using a keyboard.

Even though maintaining handwriting practice in school is crucial, it is also important to keep up in the ever-developing digital world. Children should receive handwriting training at school to learn to write by hand successfully, and, at the same time learn to use a keyboard, depending on the task at hand. The present study shows that the neural connectivity patterns underlying handwriting and typewriting are distinctly different. Hence, being aware of when to write by hand or use a digital device is crucial, whether it is to take lecture notes to learn new concepts or to write longer essays.

A recent EEG-study from our lab showed that drawing by hand causes more activity and involves larger areas in the brain as opposed to typing on a keyboard (Van der Meer and Van der Weel, 2017). We concluded that the involvement of fine and intricate hand movements in notetaking, in contrast with pressing keys on a keyboard that all require the same simple finger movement, may be more advantageous for learning (Van der Meer and Van der Weel, 2017). A follow-up study observed event-related synchronized activity in the theta range in both children and students in parietal and central brain regions, but only when writing by hand (Askvik et al., 2020). As these studies have found evidence that writing by hand facilitates learning, the present study further investigated the neurobiological differences related to cursive writing and typewriting in the young adult brain. Specifically, we investigated how the various brain regions interconnect via neural networks when writing by hand as opposed to typing on a keyboard using frequency modulation and the latest in brain connectivity analysis (c.f., Solomon et al., 2017).
