# Cornell note-taking strategy instruction for Gen Z: enhancing EFL students’ reading comprehension

Asian-Pacific Journal of Second and Foreign Language Education, 2025. 1 citation.

## Authors

- Jiyoung Seo (Kookmin University): h-index 6; 137 citations; corresponding author

## Topics

Reading and Literacy Development, Second Language Acquisition and Learning, Language, Metaphor, and Cognition, Reading comprehension, Mathematics education

---

# Cornell note-taking strategy instruction for Gen Z: enhancing EFL students' reading comprehension

## Abstract

In this technological and AI era, Generation Z spends more time reading texts on technological devices, prefers bullet points over lengthy, complex material (Bano et al., in JEED 5 42-59, 2018), and is increasingly dependent on AI-driven tools, which has made them more reluctant to read independently. The Cornell note-taking method encourages students to organize information, generate questions, and summarize ideas using their own words, helping them develop their metacognitive abilities and critical thinking. Therefore, this study examines the extent to which the Cornell note-taking method improves the reading comprehension of Gen Z students when engaging with longer academic texts. Seventy-seven students divided into two groups participated in the 15-week study: a Cornell note group and a Personal note group. Two reading comprehension tests were administered, and a repeated measures two-way ANOVA was conducted to identify whether there were any statistically significant differences in the two groups' test scores. A questionnaire was also administered at the end of the intervention to assess their perceptions of the Cornell note-taking method. The results revealed that the Cornell notetaking group outperformed the personal note-taking group, with a significant difference in test scores over time. A significant interaction effect was also observed between time and group. The Cornell note-taking group also exhibited higher confidence in class engagement and better retention of English content and expressions, which benefited their exam preparation. A majority also held positive attitudes toward using this method in the future. The findings highlight the value of teaching reading using the Cornell note-taking strategy.

## Introduction

Rapid advancements in AI have transformed learning environments. Generation Z (Gen Z) university students prefer digital devices to traditional tools when recording and transcribing lecture notes (Evans & Shively, 2019; Flanigan et al., 2024) . While some students effectively organize their lecture notes, many outsource the notetaking task only to their digital devices. This passive learning behavior is made Page 2 of 24 Seo Asian. J. Second. Foreign. Lang. Educ. (2025) 10:40 possible by AI-supported tools including voice recording apps, speech-to-text (STT) transcription, and generative AI summaries. However, students' increased reliance on AI is a growing concern as it might discourage them from fully engaging with reading materials. In English as a foreign language (EFL) contexts, such as Korea, students are now heavily dependent on AI-generated Korean translations and use generative AI at various stages of writing, from brainstorming to proofreading. Mohamed (2024) warned that EFL students' excessive reliance on AI may weaken their critical thinking skills, raising concerns about its long-term impact on language learning. Despite the continuous advancement of AI-powered summarization and notetaking tools, learners remain responsible for evaluating and refining these outputs. To achieve learning, students need to critically assess AI-generated results, actively engage with the material, and regulate their thought processes accordingly. Simply put, metacognitive awareness-the ability to monitor and control one's own learning (Flavell, 1979) -has become an essential skill in the AI era. To develop this ability, learners require explicit training in organizing information and identifying key concepts, and one method that provides a structured approach to such development is Cornell note-taking.

Note-taking is not a natural skill and requires the integration of multiple cognitive processes and abilities (Kobayashi, 2005; Peverly, 2006; Piolat et al., 2005) . Van der Meer (2012) argued that because students cannot easily university lecture note-taking, college freshmen require explicit note-taking instruction (Dean et al., 2012) . Baharev (2016) also found that explicit instruction on the selection, encoding, storage, and retrieval of information can significantly affect student performance.

Recent empirical studies on second-language (L2) proficiency have been increasingly interested in the impacts of note-taking (Jin & Webb, 2025) . Of the various notetaking methods, the Cornell note-taking system has been found particularly effective as it facilitates efficient note-taking and enhances reading comprehension (RC) (Faber et al., 2000) . Moreover, Cornell notes integrate note-taking, self-questioning, and summarization, serving as a powerful tool for strengthening metacognitive learning strategies. This study investigates the use of Cornell note-taking in a first-year English reading class to assess whether this intervention can enhance RC skills.

This study contributes to the literature through its empirical demonstration of the effectiveness of Cornell note-taking for Gen Z EFL college students, who actively depend on digital tools and AI-driven assistance for learning. As AI technology evolves, there is an increasing need to evaluate whether traditional note-taking strategies can still enhance cognitive and metacognitive skills in students who have relied on automated transcription and summarization tools.

In addition, this study's focus on handwritten Cornell notes helps sheds light on whether structured note-taking can help Gen Z students deepen their engagement more deeply with texts and improve their RC. This research expands on previous research by examining digital-native students' evolving learning habits. While studies have established the benefits of structured note-taking, only a few have explored its relevance to students with access to AI-generated assistance. The findings also offer insights into students' perceptions of Cornell note-taking strategies and provide pedagogical implications for effective university note-taking instruction to improve RC. This study was driven by two research questions:

RQ1: To what extent does Cornell note-taking method affect EFL learners' reading comprehension? RQ2: What are college students' perceptions of Cornell note-taking?

## Note-taking and L2 reading

Note-taking is based on information-processing theory and levels-of-processing theory. The information-processing model (Atkinson & Shiffrin, 1968 ) suggests that learners transfer knowledge into their long-term memory by actively recording, differentiating, and associating information with prior knowledge. Meanwhile, the levels-of-processing theory posits that deeper cognitive processing, such as semantic processing, results in better retention and recall compared with superficial, sensory processing. This deeper processing, also known as generative processing, actively connects new information with prior knowledge and can improve learning and long-term knowledge transfer (Craik & Turving, 1975) . Using their native language, many EFL students jot down the meaning of unfamiliar words and mark texts by underlining, highlighting, circling, or annotating, typically using these notes for test preparation (Dunkel, 1988) .

For effective note-taking, students must first comprehend the text and then paraphrase, summarize, select, and organize the information before writing it down to transfer these ideas into their long-term memory (Piolat et al., 2005) . For college students, note-taking is a critical learning skill as it demands cognitive and physical effort within limited time (Siegel, 2020) . When listening to lectures or reading a book, note-takers must simultaneously comprehend the information, decide what is important, and write down these notes. Note-taking has two primary functions: encoding and external storage. Encoding pertains to the information processing at a deeper level that occurs during note-taking (Jansen et al., 2017) . As students read, they select important information and organize them into an understandable format, helping them link new knowledge to their existing schema and remember it more easily. Therefore, more than simply writing down information, note-taking is an active process of encoding new knowledge into the brain by connecting it with prior knowledge. Another function of note-taking is the external storage function. It refers to revisiting and reviewing notes after they have been taken, which allows students to use them as reference for later study, such as during test preparation (Bui et al., 2013 ). Flanigal et al. (2024) asserted that of these two functions, the latter is more practical as students benefit more from reviewing their notes after class than from the encoding process during note-taking.

During the encoding process, note-takers can adopt a nongenerative or generative strategy or both. The nongenerative method involves students taking notes verbatim (Siegel, 2020) , a strategy similar to Jin and Webb's (2024) "linear method, " in which learners focus on selecting and recording information in the order it is presented without necessarily copying everything word for word. For example, they might write down key vocabulary or linguistic forms they deem useful. In contrast, the generative strategy involves the reorganization of information to create a coherent structure (Ponce & Mayer, 2014) . Instead of copying text, note-takers produce their own interpretations and formats, which requires greater cognitive effort but leads to higher informationprocessing levels. Generative strategies including the use of abbreviations, summaries of key ideas, condensed versions of long sentences, or structured note-taking formats such as Cornell notes promote deeper engagement with the material (Jin & Webb, 2024) . Igo and Kiewra (2007) observed that verbatim note-taking results in shallow processing, and Evans and Shively (2019) found that students who took verbatim notes using a laptop often forgot the main ideas and needed to reread the entire material multiple times.

With the advancement of technology, considerable research has been conducted on digital note-taking and note-taking modalities such as computer note-taking, as opposed to handwritten notes, with mixed findings. While digital tools allow students to quickly take notes and efficiently revise them, these applications have also been associated with shallow cognitive processing and higher susceptibility to distractions such as browsing nonacademic websites or social media platforms (Aagaard, 2015) . Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) found that students who took handwritten notes were better at processing and organizing information than those who took notes verbatim on a laptop, which had been linked to better test performance.

Several studies have been conducted on teaching L2 reading and note-taking strategies. Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) observed the critical role of purposeful note-taking in enhancing RC, and Evans and Shively (2019) found that note-taking during reading facilitates the encoding process. When students organize their notes as they read, they are encouraged to re-read the material, increasing their reading time. Notably, students who use generative note-taking strategies must go beyond mere sentence-level translation or understanding and are instead encouraged to comprehend individual parts relative to the overall reading context. This approach helps them achieve a discourse-level comprehension of content and structure, allowing for a more holistic understanding. Whereas most L2 learners recognize the value of note-taking (Armbruster, 2000) , those who lack a systematic note-taking approach or strategy often end up producing poor-quality notes because of their inability to identify and encode essential points while integrating the new information with prior knowledge for comprehension (Brunner, 2013; Quintus et al., 2012) . One way to address such a challenge is by teaching note-taking strategies such as the structured Cornell notetaking framework (Siegel, 2022) .

## Cornell note-taking and related works

Developed by Walter Pauk of Cornell University, the Cornell method is an approach to teaching effective note-taking. Information-processing theory (Atkinson & Shiffrin, 1968 ) posits that information passes through sensory memory and is processed in working memory before being stored in long-term memory through rehearsal or repeated exposure. However, the limited capacity of working memory and the excessive cognitive overload necessitate the implementation of effective learning strategies such as the Cornell note-taking method to facilitate long-term retention. At the initial encoding stage, the note-taking aspect of the Cornell format helps learners selectively identify and record key information while organizing related concepts into meaningful chunks. This reduces learners' cognitive load and allows them to process more efficiently within their working memory, thus preparing the information for successful transfer to long-term memory. Furthermore, the Cornell method encourages learners to organize the selected information using their own words and generate questions that promote elaborative rehearsal. These activities not only deepen learners' understanding but also improve their chances of storing and retrieving information from long-term memory.

The Cornell note-taking method can be particularly effective in overcoming the limitations of working memory and facilitating the transfer of information to longterm memory. According to Baddeley and Hitch's (1974) model, working memory comprises three components: the central executive, the phonological loop, and the visuospatial sketchpad. As learners read, incoming information is temporarily stored in the phonological loop. At the same time, learners selectively attend to important content, which is temporarily stored in the visuospatial sketchpad. The central executive coordinates the operation of these two subsystems by managing attention and integrating information. The Cornell method naturally aligns with the functions of these three components. By encouraging learners to identify and record key ideas from extensive texts, the method promotes focused attention and chunking. Visual representations such as diagrams or tables in the note-taking section help reduce cognitive overload and support the retention of information. Furthermore, transferring information to longterm memory requires rehearsal. The cue section of the Cornell format enables students to generate self-questions, while the summary section encourages them to condense and integrate the material. These processes reinforce deeper encoding and enhance the likelihood of long-term retention.

According to Pauk and Owens (2010) , the Cornell format consists of three sections, each serving a unique purpose. In the note-taking section, students record information as completely as possible in telegraphic sentences using bullet points, graphic organizers, or abbreviations rather than verbatim notes. In the cue section, students take note of main ideas or write questions based on their notes, and in the summary section, students must condense the entire content into a few sentences. This organizational layout encourages students to efficiently synthesize, record, and annotate information. Pauk (1997) described the Cornell method as a systematic study system for knowledge acquisition rather than a mere note-taking tool. It helps students acquire the skills to organize, review, and synthesize the learned material in six key steps:

(1) Record: Put the actual notes in the note-taking section.

(2) Question: Generate questions and identify key points in the cue section.

(3) Recite: Answer questions or expand on information by concealing the notes in the note-taking section. (4) Recapitulate: Condense the essential information into a few sentences.

(5) Reflect: Deeply examine the content and its implications and connections to one's experiences. (6) Review: Regularly review the notes to reinforce memory retention.

Within the educational context, one notable benefit of Cornell note-taking is its practicality; that is, it can be readily implemented into lesson delivery. Marzano et al. (2001) discussed the Cornell method's applicability to all subjects and age groups and positive impacts on academic achievement. This approach allows students to work individually, in pairs, or in groups, which also encourages collaboration during pair or group work (Brunner, 2013) . It also fosters learners' active engagement and participation, gradually cultivating their independent learning and enhancing their critical thinking through the note-taking process (Donohoo, 2010) . Recently, many students have been using the Cornell note format on their mobile devices through handwriting. Popular note-taking applications such as Goodnotes, Samsung Note, and Noteshelf have the Cornell note format as a template (Goodnotes Blog, 2024). Demirelli et al. (2024) also developed a digital note-taking application called Cornell Technique. For teachers, the Cornell method provides an effective way to monitor students' understanding of the learning material. The method has also been shown to significantly improve students' comprehension and performance (Ahmad, 2019; Evans & Shively, 2019) . Advocating the Cornell method as a means of instruction for college students, Hendel (2024) asserted that it promotes higher-order instruction by allowing teachers to structure lessons with keywords and real-world examples.

Several complex cognitive processes occur when one takes notes within a limited time frame. The note-taker must simultaneously comprehend, evaluate, sort, record, and modify information, enhancing their metacognitive skills (Piolat & Olive, 2005) . They temporarily store the new information in their working memory, which helps them identify key points, control their attention, remember instructions, keep track of plans, and solve complex problems (Klingberg, 2008) . While note-taking itself can be cognitively demanding, Evans and Shively (2019) found that the Cornell format reduced the cognitive load on working memory, which we use to temporarily store and manipulate limited information while also managing attention and multitasking. Hence, recording key information while reading lightens the cognitive burden on one's working memory. Further, even if students' attention is interrupted by distractions, having written notes makes it easier for them to regain focus. The Cornell method, therefore, can alleviate the impacts of distractions on working memory.

Several studies have explored how the Cornell note-taking method shapes learning outcomes. Investigating the effect of the method on college students' listening comprehension in Saudi Arabia, Abualzain (2024) found that the group that did not receive note-taking instruction showed poor performance, and survey results revealed that many students had never been taught note-taking strategies. Ahmad (2019) examined the impact of two note-taking strategies (Cornell and REAP) on Egyptian secondary school students' critical reading skills and observed that those who used the Cornell method had significantly higher posttest scores because it helped them analyze, synthesize, reflect on their reading, and make personal connections to the text. Similarly, Sholikhah et al. (2019) found that the Cornell method encouraged Indonesian middle school students to actively analyze texts, increasing their motivation to read as well as their enjoyment. However, students with lower vocabulary proficiency struggled to complete the cue section when writing key points and generating questions. Thus, to provide better support, Sholikhah et al. (2019) suggested providing students with more examples and demonstrations before implementing the Cornell method and allowing them to use dictionaries. Evans and Shively (2019) divided 101 eighth-graders learning social studies at an international school in China into four groups that used handwritten Cornell notes, computer-typed Cornell notes, personal notes, and no notes. The researchers found a significant difference in performance between the note-taking and no-note-taking groups but no notable differences between note-taking groups. In a questionnaire on students' self-efficacy in preparing for RC tests, those who used handwritten Cornell notes reported the highest self-efficacy followed by those who used personal notes, digital Cornell notes, and no notes. Evans and Shively (2019) concluded that handwritten Cornell notes are valuable even in technology-rich classrooms. Alzu'bi (2019) explored the Cornell method's ability to improve the writing composition skills of 58 Jordanian university students. Posttest results showed a statistically significant difference between experimental and control groups. That is, students who adopted the Cornell method were more motivated to learn and demonstrated better writing organization and cohesion. Nevertheless, the mean score difference between groups was minimal possibly because of insufficient training in the method and the fast pace of the lectures.

## Participants

This study was conducted in South Korea for 15 weeks from September to December 2023. To determine the minimum sample size for hypothesis testing, this study performed a priori power analysis using G*Power version 3.1.9.7 (Faul et al., 2009) . The analysis was based on a two-way repeated-measures analysis of variance (ANOVA) with a significance level of α = 0.05, a desired power of 0.95, and an estimated medium effect size of f = 0.25. The results indicated a required minimum of 54 participants; however, this study included a total of 77 participants, providing sufficient statistical power to detect potential effects. All participants provided informed consent after reviewing the study objectives and procedures and obtaining assurance that their participation was voluntary and would not affect their grades and that they could withdraw from the study at any time.

The 77 participants were divided into two groups: Cornell notes (experimental group, n = 37) and Personal notes (control group, n = 40). Their average score in the Test of English for International Communication (TOEIC) was 494, classifying them as preintermediates (A2) according to Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) standards (ETS Global, 2021). Students' proficiency was examined based on their TOEIC reading scores: intermediate and above and preintermediate. A TOEIC reading score of 275 or higher corresponds to CEFR level B1 (intermediate) or above (ETS Global, 2021) . Therefore, the intermediate-and-above group included students with TOEIC RC scores of 275 or higher, while the preintermediate group included those with scores below 275. To verify group homogeneity, a chi-squared test was performed to examine relations between gender, major, TOEIC RC scores, experience with Cornell notes, and preferred activity types. Table 1 summarizes the participant data and chi-squared test results.

The two groups were deemed homogeneous because of the lack of significant differences in any of the variables. Before the intervention, the participants underwent a mock TOEIC test to assess their English proficiency. Although the TOEIC contains listening comprehension (LC) and RC sections, only the scores for the latter were used in this study as LC was outside the research scope. The homogeneity of the groups' English proficiency was assessed using an independent samples t-test based on RC scores (Table 2 ).

The results indicated that there were no statistically significant differences in the TOEIC RC scores between the two groups, which confirmed their homogeneity in terms of English proficiency.

## Data collection instrument and analysis method

Quantitative and qualitative data were collected from two RC tests and a questionnaire.

## Reading comprehension questions

The researcher designed pre-and posttests to assess the participants' ability to recall detailed information (see Appendix). Multiple-choice questions were chosen for their objectivity, high reliability, and accurate measurement of specific reading skills (Javid, 2014) . Short-answer items were designed to assess recall of facts from the text and required responses of a maximum of two words, minimizing variation in possible answers.

Each reading test contained 10 items: eight multiple-choice and two short-answer questions. Each test passage was 444 words long, and the participants were given 10 min to complete it. The researcher/instructor developed both tests based on the reading materials and administered them online via the college LMS; these tests were automatically graded using the platform's auto-scoring feature. The researcher selected reading materials from Media Matters (Ryu et al., 2022) , a textbook that the researcher developed for upper-intermediate college students with the aim to enhance their English proficiency through engaging topics relevant to collegelevel EFL learners, such as superheroes, games, movies, travel, and viruses.

To maintain lexical control, the texts were revised by a native professional writer, referencing the academic vocabulary list (AVL) and the new general service list (NGSL) to ensure appropriate lexical diversity. Each chapter contained approximately 900-1000 words, with a content word ratio of 50%, representing high lexical density, characteristic of expository texts. Sentence length was approximately 20 words on average, consistent with the optimal range for academic writing (15-20 words). Additionally, each sentence contained an average of two clauses, reflecting a moderate to high level of syntactic complexity with frequent use of subordinate clauses.

To assess the reading test's difficulty level, a pilot test was conducted with students from the previous semester, and a comparison of average scores confirmed that the preand posttests were of comparable difficulty.

## Questionnaire

A pre-intervention questionnaire was administered at the beginning of the intervention to collect demographic data, and a post-intervention questionnaire was administered at the end of the intervention to assess the participants' perceptions of the Cornell method. The closed-ended items were rated using a five-point Likert-type scale (from 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree) to determine whether Cornell note-taking (1) facilitated students' learning, (2) improved their English proficiency, and (3) encouraged future use whereas the open-ended questions collected additional perspectives from the Cornell note-taking group. The Cornell participants were also asked to explain their Likert scale choices, describe any changes or difficulties they encountered when using the Cornell method, and offer suggestions for possible improvements.

## Procedure

The Cornell and Personal notes groups read the same texts from a printed book during the intervention and were not allowed to use digital devices during the class intervention activities, which were conducted for three hours per week. Each session for both groups consisted of 1.5 h of reading instruction and note-taking activities followed by another 1.5 h of reviews, warm-up activities, and vocabulary learning.

In Week 1, students were given information about the study, and consent forms were collected. A mock TOEIC test and a pre-intervention questionnaire were administered to assess the students' English proficiency and provide background information. In Week 2, the students were introduced to general note-taking and different note-taking strategies, and the educational benefits were outlined. In Week 3, English reading instruction was provided, followed by the pre-test.

The intervention period ran from weeks 4-13. Students in the Personal note group took notes in their preferred style without specific guidance, while those in the Cornell note group followed the Cornell note-taking format, which included structured sections for organizing their notes. In Week 14, the post-test was administered to assess students' reading comprehension and recall after the intervention. In Week 15, a post-questionnaire was conducted to examine the perceptions and experiences of students in the Cornell note group.

The reading instruction followed the process outlined below:

(1) Before reading: Students previewed the topic, activated schemata and prior knowledge, and reviewed key vocabulary. (2) During reading: Students took notes on main ideas and key details. The Personal note group took notes freely in any format, while the Cornell note group used the structured Cornell note-taking format, which features telegraphic sentences, abbreviations, bullet points, graphic organizers, and symbols instead of complete sentences.

(3) After reading: The Personal note group revisited and refined their notes independently. The Cornell note-taking group completed the cue section by identifying key points or guiding questions based on their notes and then condensed their notes into four or five succinct sentences in the summary section to reinforce comprehension.

Since it is difficult to expand the capacity of working memory to reduce cognitive overload, it is more effective to clear the working space as quickly as possible. To achieve this, information entering working memory should be transferred to long-term memory efficiently. This transfer process requires connecting new information with prior knowledge. However, such connections can only be made if relevant prior knowledge already exists. Therefore, activating prior knowledge is essential, and when students lack such knowledge, it must be provided in advance. Table 3 shows the details of the weekly study procedure.

This structured approach ensured that both groups engaged in note-taking during the reading sessions, with the Personal note group was instructed to take notes using their personally preferred note-taking method as they received no explicit note-taking instruction. To protect the participants' attention and working memory from distractions such as social media app notifications, Internet searches, or AI-generated assistance, this study focused on handwritten Cornell notes. This study also used printed materials as research has found that physical books are still preferred in academic settings despite digital reading being more convenient (Mushtaq et al., 2020; Rue, 2018) . The Cornell note group was given instructions following Donohoo's (2010) approach, which consists of training, modeling, guided instruction, and independent practice:

(1) Training: Introduce the Cornell method and explain its benefits, the format structure, and the six steps to study with it.

(2) Modeling: Demonstrate the method using a think-aloud process, select key points, and explain the rationale behind these selections.

(3) Guided instruction: Use a few paragraphs from the textbook, pausing after each to ask students to identify keywords and main ideas and engage the whole class to build their confidence. (4) Practice: Students independently practice Cornell note-taking. The researcher then asks the students about their selected keywords and expressions and reasons for each. Explanations are briefly reiterated every session. Students produce handwritten Cornell notes to avoid using a translator or generative AI, such as ChatGPT.

## Data analysis

Quantitative data were analyzed using SPSS 25. First, chi-squared tests were performed to assess group homogeneity before intervention. An independent-samples t-test was conducted using pre-intervention TOEIC scores to confirm homogeneity in English proficiency. Second, Shapiro-Wilk tests were applied to the pre-test and post-test scores to verify the normality assumptions. Fourth, a paired samples t-test was conducted to examine the within-group improvements from the pre-test to post-test. Finally, a repeated measures two-way ANOVA was used to identify statistically significant differences between the groups' test scores over time.

Before the questionnaire was administered, the closed-ended questions were reviewed by two language education experts, and a pilot test was then conducted with a small group of students to refine the items' wording and clarity. The five-point Likert-scale responses were examined using frequency analysis to determine overall trends. The students' open-ended responses, which explored the reasoning behind their Likertscale ratings, underwent thematic analysis. To ensure interrater reliability and minimize subjectivity in the qualitative data interpretation, two assessors (the researcher and a teaching assistant) independently coded the responses and resolved any discrepancies through discussion and agreement on emerging themes. The interrater reliability test had a kappa value of 0.80, indicating substantial agreement beyond chance.

## RQ1: to what extent does cornell note-taking method affect EFL learners' reading comprehension?

Table 4 shows the results for the independent samples t-test to compare the pre-test scores of the Cornell note and Personal note groups. The mean score for the Cornell note group was slightly lower than that of the Personal note group; however, this difference was not statistically significant (t(75) = -1.381, p = 0.171).

The Shapiro-Wilk normality test results for the pre-and post-test scores for both groups are shown in Table 4 . A p-value greater than 0.05 indicates that the data do not significantly deviate from a normal distribution, which satisfies the normality assumption; therefore, as all p-values were greater than 0.05, the normality assumption was met for both groups in both tests.

The pre-test and post-test score for both groups were compared. Table 5 presents the paired sample t-test and descriptive statistical results for the two test scores for each group. In the pre-test, the Personal note group (M = 47.25, SD = 20.470) scored higher than the Cornell note group (M = 40.95, SD = 19.502); however, in the posttest, the Cornell note group (M = 61.38, SD = 16.958) outperformed the Personal note group (M = 55.68, SD = 19.810).

The increase in mean scores from pre-to post-test were greater in the Cornell note group (M = 20.43, SD = 3.456) than in the Personal note group (M = 8.43, SD = 3.324). The paired samples t-test revealed that the score pre-test to post-test improvements were statistically significant in both groups, with the Cornell note group showing a highly significant increase (p < 0.001) and the Personal note group also showing a significant improvement (p = 0.013). These results suggested that the Cornell note-taking method led to a greater improvement in test scores than the Personal note method. A repeated measures two-way ANOVA was conducted to detect statistically significant differences in test scores between the two groups (see Table 6 ). The results showed a significant increase in the test scores over time (F = 36.221, p < 0.001) with a large effect size (partial η 2 = .326), indicating a substantial improvement in participants' scores over time. According to Richardson (2011) , partial η 2 values of .01, .06, and .14 correspond to small, medium, and large effects, respectively. The significant interaction between time and group (F = 6.271, p < 0.05) with a medium effect size (partial η 2 = .077) suggests that the extent of improvement differed between groups. These findings show that the Cornell note-taking group achieved statistically higher post-test scores and that the participants' scores were significantly influenced by time. This implies that learners' continued use of the Cornell note-taking method could positively affect their test scores.

Figure 1 illustrates changes in RC scores over time by group. The Cornell note-taking group showed a steeper improvement in scores than the personal note-taking group, whose scores rose at a slower pace. This suggests that the Cornell method provides additional RC performance benefits.

## RQ2: what are college students' perceptions of cornell note-taking?

A total of 19 students (51.3%) reported that Cornell note-taking boosted their confidence in engaging with the reading material, while 23 (62.2%) found it beneficial for learning English (Table 7 ). Finally, 31 students (83.8%) expressed their intention to use the Cornell method in learning English and other subjects in the future.

## Increased confidence during engagement

The Cornell note-taking method led to higher confidence among the students, particularly those who felt overwhelmed by long English passages. The systematic organizing and summarizing methods reduced their cognitive load, allowing them to engage more deeply with the material, develop a better understanding of the text's flow, structure information more effectively, and identify key points independently. As a result, they were more confident in their reading ability and participated in class more actively. Student 4 noted, "I felt less intimidated by long texts after breaking them down into smaller parts. " Many students reported that taking structured notes helped them actively engage with the reading materials as opposed to passively listening to lectures or skimming texts. They felt that note-taking helped them maintain their focus during class as they had to actively identify important points and structure the content. Student 15 stated, "I think more critically instead of passively listening to lectures, " and student 9 noted, "I paid more attention in class because I had to take notes. " Student 31 also reported, "Through note-taking, I could identify what I didn't understand and what the key points were. " Similarly, student 11 remarked, "Rather than mindlessly writing, it was helpful to think while identifying key points and writing them down. " Student 14 stated, "Summarizing helped in understanding long texts. "

For other participants, creating questions helped pinpoint areas requiring further review. They noted that generating questions in English stimulated their learning and increased their awareness of sections they did not understand, which naturally led them to ask the teacher more questions. Student 10 commented, "I came up with more questions during class, which helped me build confidence in learning. "

Meanwhile, five students (13.5%, 4 upper-intermediate and 1 intermediate) reported that Cornell note-taking did not help with their confidence. These students already had their own note-taking systems, found it difficult to switch methods, and felt no need to adopt a new one. Additionally, 13 students (35.1%) gave neutral responses, stating that although the method was new, they needed more time to be familiar with it.

## Improved English language retention and usefulness for exam preparation

According to several students, the structured Cornell note format helped them memorize not only the content but also English expressions. The ability to summarize the material in their own words helped them understand and recall factual information. For instance, student 22 stated, "It helped me remember what I learned, both content and English vocabulary. " Many students appreciated the structured format of Cornell notes for its usefulness in exam reviews. These organized notes facilitated students' reviews and clarified the points to study during exams. The summary section allowed them to quickly revisit key concepts, minimizing the effort required for last-minute studying. Student 25 explained, "I felt more confident in exam preparation. " Student 9 said that note-taking in class helped them save time for exam preparation, stating, "I usually summarize material before exams myself, but doing it during class saved time. "

Of the students in the Cornell note-taking group, 31 (83.8%) reported that they would continue using the method in the future, reflecting an overall positive evaluation (see Table 8 ). They found that the Cornell method increased their motivation to learn English and expressed interest in applying it to other subjects. The students' positive reflections included "I could develop a habit of summarizing other subjects as well" (Student 30), and "I felt the need to learn vocabulary" (Student 2).

## Challenges

Despite acknowledging the benefits of the Cornell note-taking system, students also highlighted several notable challenges. Those with lower English proficiency reported struggling to understand the texts in Korean, which left them little time to understand the note-taking method and complete their notes in English during class. According to Student 19, "I found it difficult to write in English while trying to understand the content, " and Student 11 stated, "I struggled to create questions, and it took me a long time. "

In addition, students who had already developed their own note-taking habits found it difficult to adjust to the Cornell note-taking system. Of the five students (13.5%) who felt that the method failed to boost their confidence, four were upper-intermediate learners who preferred their own strategies. Student 20 remarked, "I had already established my own system, so switching to a new one was unnecessary for me. " Some students were accustomed to typing on their digital devices and therefore found writing by hand slow and difficult to revise, expressing their preference for digital tools for note-taking. Student 23 commented, "I had difficulty in revising handwritten notes compared to a tablet PC. " Others stated that they preferred digital note-taking tools that allow for easier editing and organization.

Overall, while students appreciated the positive impact of the Cornell method on engagement, language learning, and exam preparation, they faced challenges in adapting to the method and using pens and pencils. These findings suggest that additional training or digital adaptations could facilitate broader adoption among students.

## Discussion

The findings suggest that both printed books and handwritten note-taking continue to positively influence Gen Z students' academic achievement. Specifically, this study found that the Cornell note-taking method effectively improved students' RC. The results of the repeated measures two-way ANOVA that was conducted to examine the reading comprehension score changes over time and by group revealed that while both groups experienced significant RC score improvements, the Cornell note group achieved a greater increase, highlighting the method's crucial role in enhancing RC performance.

First, the findings are consistent with the levels-of-processing theory (Craik & Tulving, 1975) , which posits that learners who process information at a deeper, more meaningful level are more likely to retain such knowledge. The Cornell note-taking method fosters deeper cognitive processing by prompting students to identify key ideas, generate questions, and summarize content rather than merely copying text. Unlike shallow processing such as verbatim note-taking, the Cornell system encouraged students to actively engage with the material. By summarizing key ideas, identifying main concepts, and generating guiding questions, students moved beyond superficial processing and performed generative processing, strengthening their comprehension and recall (Jin & Webb, 2024) . In this study, the Cornell note-taking group recalled the answers to the short-answer questions more accurately, which aligns with Jacob (2008), who found that the Cornell method increased students' performance on higher-level questions, and Evans and Shively (2019) , who observed that the Cornell note-takers recalled more facts and details, identified more key ideas, and made more connections between different concepts. The Cornell note-taking method enhanced students' RC by facilitating connections between main ideas and details while helping them temporarily store information in their working memory. Through note-taking, students actively processed and organized key concepts, promoting deeper cognitive engagement and interaction with existing long-term memory schemas. This process is consistent with the encoding effect, in which selecting and structuring information deepens processing and strengthens memory retention (Jansen et al., 2017) . Students' active linking of new knowledge to prior schemas reinforced their learning, making recall more efficient. Before and during reading, students activated prior knowledge by previewing topics and connecting new inputs with existing schemas, helping prepare their working memory for processing new textual information and allowing for more efficient selection and organization of key information during note-taking. As a result, the Cornell note-taking group achieved higher RC scores, highlighting the benefits of structured note-taking.

Second, the Cornell method can help today's Gen Z students build the capacity to read longer texts and stay more engaged in class despite being accustomed to shortform content. Because Gen Zers' preference for short-form content has also affected their attention spans, they tend to find it challenging to listen to 3-h lectures and read long, complex texts. Darwin et al. (2023) also reported the struggle experienced by today's students with reading and listening between the lines. The participants' responses to the open-ended questions support Evans and Shively's (2019) assertion that the Cornell format reduces cognitive load and distractions and allows students to refocus more easily after interruptions. Reading lengthy material can overwhelm one's working memory and result in cognitive overload, and note-taking requires cognitive and physical effort within time constraints (Siegel, 2020) . However, by organizing information into three sections through Cornell note-taking, students can manage their cognitive load more effectively, which helps them develop the perseverance to read and comprehend longer texts.

Lastly, the Cornell method is not only a note-taking technique but also a tool for improving learning habits. Its developer, Pauk (1997) , described it as a "natural learning system. " After taking notes in the note section, students are required to identify key points and develop questions in the cue section, improving their ability to recognize important information and possible areas of misunderstanding. The practice of reviewing questions and elaborating on main points by covering up the note section helps students form effective learning habits, which extends beyond reading classes, as evidenced by the participants' reports of having developed the habit of summarizing other subjects as well.

## Implications for classroom instruction

From these findings, several recommendations can be made for classroom instruction. First, it is important to provide explicit, sustained strategy instructions when seeking to improve students' RC. Despite note-taking management often being left to students, this study showed that explicit instruction in the Cornell method can positively affect both RC scores and student attitudes. Given that different learning strategies can produce different results, teachers need to provide educational support to assist students in effectively utilizing tools such as Cornell notes. Rather than providing a brief introduction to the Cornell method, teachers should explain, model, guide, and provide independent practice in the method and give students time to internalize the process.

Second, differentiated instruction based on language proficiency and preferences is essential to effectively implement note-taking methods in the classroom. In this study, students who already had their own note-taking systems were less inclined to adopt the Cornell method. Therefore, rather than imposing the approach on all students, teachers could encourage them to compare Cornell notetaking with other note-taking methods and evaluate their strengths and weaknesses. Alternatively, teachers should guide students in integrating Cornell notetaking into their own systems. Because students with lower language proficiencies experienced difficulty in analyzing the texts and simultaneously taking notes, providing scaffolding, such as pre-made cue sections or structured note-taking guides, could help alleviate their cognitive load. An initial time investment is also necessary to allow students to familiarize themselves with the Cornell method. During this period, students find it challenging to engage in overly complex academic reading materials as these may demand higher-level thinking and deeper cognitive processing. Therefore, teachers could facilitate the transition by using simpler and more engaging reading materials first, as suggested by Tsai and Wu (2010) .

Third, Cornell notes can be integrated into classroom activities and serve as a portfolio assessment tool. To prevent note-taking from becoming the focus, teachers must incorporate the six-step study system-record, question, recite, recapitulate, reflect, and review-into classroom activities. Students could provide opportunities for review and reflection by sharing their keywords and asking and answering questions with peers; that is, the Cornell note-taking method could present a clear indication of students' current learning by providing tangible evidence of their understanding and progress as well as qualitative information about their understanding and emotional responses. Therefore, teachers could use these insights to provide more targeted feedback based on students' note-taking outcomes as well as evaluate portfolios.

## Conclusion

This study examined the impact of handwritten Cornell note-taking instruction on the RC of Korean Gen Z EFL university freshmen, who tend to prefer digital media and struggle with long, complex texts. A total of 77 college students were divided into a Cornell note-taking group and a Personal note-taking group for a 15-week experimental course. These groups used the same reading materials and were not allowed to use digital devices in class. A repeated measures two-way ANOVA was conducted to assess the statistical significance of the RC score differences in the two groups over time, the results from which indicated that both groups had significantly improved but the Cornell note group had a steeper score rise, indicating that the method provided additional RC improvement benefits. The questionnaire results revealed that more than half of the students increased their confidence in reading English and found the Cornell method helpful for their English learning and retention. Furthermore, 83.8% of the Cornell notetaking participants expressed their intention to continue using the method.

These findings provide some implications for reading instruction using the Cornell strategy. Cornell note-taking could effectively improve Gen Z students' RC skills as the Cornell group not only retained more of what they read but also experienced less cognitive load, gained confidence in tackling longer texts, and remained more engaged. The Cornell method is not just a note-taking technique but is also a tool that fosters positive learning habits in Gen Z students. Additionally, it can function as a learning system that can be applied to all subjects.

The current study has several limitations that should be acknowledged and addressed in future research. First, because handwritten Cornell notes were used to avoid dependence on e-dictionaries, translators, or generative AI, this study may have limited the potential of Gen Z students who are more accustomed to digital tools. Future studies could therefore use technology to improve learning outcomes by exploring digital notetaking. Second, because this study involved a small group of first-year EFL students from Korea, the findings may not be broadly generalizable. Hence, future research should examine the effects of Cornell note-taking in diverse proficiency levels, age groups, and cultural contexts. Lastly, since this study did not examine the quality of student notes in depth, future studies should investigate the relation between the content of student notes and their comprehension to further evaluate the effectiveness of the Cornell method.

highlights:
In this technological and AI era, Generation Z spends more time reading texts on technological devices, prefers bullet points over lengthy, complex material (Bano et al., in JEED 5 42-59, 2018), and is increasingly dependent on AI-driven tools, which has made them more reluctant to read independently. The Cornell note-taking method encourages students to organize information, generate questions, and summarize ideas using their own words, helping them develop their metacognitive abilities and critical thinking. Therefore, this study examines the extent to which the Cornell note-taking method improves the reading comprehension of Gen Z students when engaging with longer academic texts. Seventy-seven students divided into two groups participated in the 15-week study: a Cornell note group and a Personal note group. Two reading comprehension tests were administered, and a repeated measures two-way ANOVA was conducted to identify whether there were any statistically significant differences in the two groups' test scores. A questionnaire was also administered at the end of the intervention to assess their perceptions of the Cornell note-taking method. The results revealed that the Cornell notetaking group outperformed the personal note-taking group, with a significant difference in test scores over time. A significant interaction effect was also observed between time and group. The Cornell note-taking group also exhibited higher confidence in class engagement and better retention of English content and expressions, which benefited their exam preparation. A majority also held positive attitudes toward using this method in the future. The findings highlight the value of teaching reading using the Cornell note-taking strategy.

...
With the advancement of technology, considerable research has been conducted on digital note-taking and note-taking modalities such as computer note-taking, as opposed to handwritten notes, with mixed findings. While digital tools allow students to quickly take notes and efficiently revise them, these applications have also been associated with shallow cognitive processing and higher susceptibility to distractions such as browsing nonacademic websites or social media platforms (Aagaard, 2015) . Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) found that students who took handwritten notes were better at processing and organizing information than those who took notes verbatim on a laptop, which had been linked to better test performance.

results.title: The effects of note-taking methods on lasting learning: the role of motivation and cognitive load
results.text:
The effects of note-taking methods on lasting learning: the role of motivation and cognitive load

## Abstract

Introduction: This randomized controlled experimental study examined the effects of four note-taking methods (Cornell, Parallel, Digital, and Sentence) on lasting learning (retention) in primary-level teacher candidates, while also exploring the roles of learning motivation and cognitive load.

Methods: A total of 134 participants were randomly assigned to three experimental groups (Cornell, Parallel, Digital) and one control group (Sentence). Following a one-session orientation, the intervention spanned 5 weeks (2 h/week). Measures included a researcher-developed Academic Achievement Test (pre-, post-, retention), a validated Learning Motivation Scale (pre-, post-), and a single-item cognitive load rating collected after each lesson. Analyses used mixed ANCOVA with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc tests and regression analyses.

Results: Mixed ANCOVA revealed significant main effects of time and group, whereas the time × group interaction was not significant. Post-hoc tests showed no significant pairwise differences at post-test; at retention, only the Cornell group scored significantly higher than the Sentence group. Motivation increased significantly in the Cornell and Parallel groups, while Digital and Sentence showed no significant pre-post change. Cognitive load differed by method, with Digital reporting significantly lower load than Parallel and Sentence. Regression analyses indicated that motivation was significantly associated with retention, whereas cognitive load was not.

Conclusions: Overall, retention differences were limited and observed only for the Cornell-Sentence comparison. Learning motivation showed the most consistent association with retention scores, whereas cognitive load showed no significant association with retention in the regression analyses.

Keywords: cognitive load; experimental study; lasting learning; learning motivation; note-taking methods.

results.title: Academic Wellbeing Study Techniques
results.text:
Academic Wellbeing presents. . .
Study Techniques &
Note-Taking Strategies
Active Recall Techniques.................................................................................................. 1
Charting Technique........................................................................................................... 2
Concrete Examples.......................................................................................................... 3
Concept Mapping..............................................................................................................4
The Cornell Note-Taking Method...................................................................................... 5
Elaboration (the Feynman Technique).............................................................................. 6
Interleaving....................................................................................................................... 7
Leitner System - Studying with Flashcards.......................................................................8
Spaced Recall................................................................................................................... 9
Outline Method............................................................................................................... 10
The “SQ3R” Method........................................................................................................ 11
Resource Links............................................................................................................... 13
Quick Study Tips............................................................................................................. 15
1
Active Recall Techniques
Active Recall Techniques encourage the brain to move information from short-term
memory into long-term memory. This allows students to have better recall of information
when taking exams or participating in class discussions. The following are common
active recall techniques:
1. Flashcards
2. Write Questions While Taking Notes
3. Complete Study or Reading Questions
4. Practice Tests
5. Visual Aids
6. “Teach a Friend”
2
Charting Technique
The Charting Technique allows students to keep track of important events, dates, key
figures, and event significance. If your lecture will follow a chronological format, you can
prepare a chart, labeling categories and dates, in advance. This technique can also be
used to quiz yourself and organize important events when studying for exams.
How to Use the Charting Method:
*Think of the created chart as a
quick fact sheet for your future
reference.*
3
Concrete Examples
In order to help with understanding and recalling information, concrete examples can be
paired with term definitions. First, create
a declarative definition (a short one or
two sentence explanation of a concept).
Then, write down an example of the
definition. Pairing a concrete example
with an abstract concept allows students
to understand concepts better. See
format below:
Term: Classical Conditioning - first
studied by Ivan Pavlov, classical
conditioning is a learning process that
occurs when two stimuli are repeatedly
paired: a response which is at first elicited
by the second stimulus is eventually
elicited by the first stimulus alone.
Concrete Example: Dog salivating to Bell
4
Concept Mapping
Students often use concept mapping to study the relationships between ideas. Through
diagraming, students can connect concepts together and learn how these connections
may be impactful.
Whether used for brainstorming purposes or for linking terms, concept mapping is an
easy way for students to organize course content.
*Want a bigger surface? Try using a giant white board to create a concept map.*
5
The Cornell Note-Taking Method
Students use the Cornell Method to take, organize, and review class notes. To practice
this method, divide your pages into three main sections: notes, cues, and summary. See
the below image for guidance on how to format the sections.
During class, take notes in the section
for note taking. Try to be concise and
focus on writing down key points,
definitions, and other important
concepts the professor shares.
After class, write down important cues,
comments, or questions in the cues
section.
Finally, use the summary section to
summarize the content on the page or
add additional notes for the textbook
that you want to have as a supplement.
*The picture on the left is an example
of how a page would be structured.*
6
Elaboration (the Feynman Technique)
An easy technique to pinpoint gaps in knowledge. Students a
1. Select a topic
2. Practice recall - write down everything you know about a topic. Then in simple
language, practice teaching the content to someone else or outloud to yourself.
3. Identify gaps in understanding - review what you remembered and identify gaps
in material and understanding. Return to the textbook or lecture notes to clarify
and fill in these gaps. Write down any corrections or elaborations.
4. Simplify language - make sure that all your language is simplified for ease of
recall during future repetitions
5. Repeat process as needed
7
Interleaving
While studying a chosen subject, students alternate the types of practice problems and
questions they solve or answer. Interleaving allows students to attend more to the
individual concepts and decrease fatigue. Additionally, interleaving helps students build
retention of understanding and promotes mastery of content long-term.
Studying the same concept for an extended period, or massed practice, can lead to
mental blocks in memory once you switch to a new topic. Interleaving reduces the
likelihood of “blocking” and, instead, allows students to remember concepts better.
The differences between Interleaving and Massed Practice is illustrated below.
8
Leitner System - Studying with Flashcards
The Leitner System is a study technique that allows students to space out their learning
in order to boost memory and performance. Students who use the Leitner System
create flashcards for each individual concept in a course they want to study; in addition,
students label 3-5 small boxes that flashcards will be placed into based on level of
knowledge.
As flashcards are answered correctly, the student can move them forward in the
sequence of boxes. Flashcards that are further along in the box sequence can be
reviewed less often, while flashcards closer to the beginning of the box sequence
should be reviewed daily. At any point, if a flashcard is answered incorrectly, it should be
moved backward into the previous box so that it can be reviewed more often.
The images to the left and
below depict the Leitner
System.
9
Spaced Recall
Research shows that spacing out the learning process has positive effects on transfer of
learning and recall. Using Spaced Recall allows students to improve their mastery of
content by increasing the amount of review time while maintaining space between study
sessions to build retrieval skills.
*Another tip is to study in 15 minute
increments. Set a timer and work for
15 minutes, take a short break, and
then repeat*
Key Points: (information from Kang 2016)
● The timing or arrangement of review/practice affects learning.
● Practice is more effective when spaced out over time, instead of massed or
grouped together (equating total practice time)
● Spaced practice enhances memory, problem solving, and transfer of learning to
new contexts.
● Spaced practice offers great potential for improving students’ educational
outcomes.
10
Outline Method
The Outline Method is a note-taking technique that allows students to quickly record key
points and course content in an organized way.
Important Things to Remember about the Outlining Method (according to the University
of Tennessee Chattanooga):
1. The information which is most general begins at the left with each more specific
group of facts indented with spaces to the right.
2. The relationships between the different parts are carried out through indenting.
3. No numbers, letters, or Roman numerals are needed because you can use a
bullet point system; however, if you prefer to use numbers, letters, or Roman
numerals, then by all means do so.
*This note-taking method can be very helpful to use for lecture style classes; however,
some students leave a little extra space between points in case they want to go back
and add additional thoughts or questions later.*
11
The “SQ3R” Method
According to the Marbella International University Centre, using the SQ3R method while
reading allows “the readers to increase their understanding of the text by engaging in
the reading process, both before, during, and after, intentionally’.
Steps of SQ3R:
1. Survey - a preliminary skim of the text, acknowledging notable headers and
charts.
2. Question - write down a list of questions based on the survey of content. This
may include turning the headers into questions themselves.
3. Read - read the text, keeping in mind your previously formed questions
4. Recall/Recite - either orally or in writing, answer your questions based on your
understanding of the reading. Try to use your own words as much as possible.
5. Review - look back over your answers and general content notes. Make a note
of any additional questions or key points you want to remember.
12
Additional SQ3R Strategies
*Try creating a chart with each prompt
that you can fill in as you read through
a chapter or article.*
13
Resource Links
